## 5.4 ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultra-condensed revision checklist ŌĆö read 5 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- Ō£ģ **Variable assignment** uses `=` and is **volatile** (RAM-only).
- Ō£ģ **File saving** uses `open(path, mode)` and is **persistent** (disk).
- Ō£ģ **Primary modes:** `r` (read), `w` (write/truncate), `a` (append), `x` (exclusive create).
- Ō£ģ **Type modes:** `t` (text, default), `b` (binary).
- Ō£ģ **Update modifier:** `+` adds read capability to write modes and vice-versa.
- Ō£ģ **Always prefer `with open(...) as f:`** for automatic closure.
- Ō£ģ **`write()`** returns the number of characters/bytes written ŌĆö useful for logging.
- Ō£ģ **`writelines()`** writes an iterable of strings but does **not** add newlines automatically.
- Ō£ģ **CSV** is best for tabular data; use `csv.writer` and `csv.DictWriter`.
- Ō£ģ **JSON** is best for nested key-value data and cross-language exchange.
- Ō£ģ **Pickle** is best for Python-native objects; **never unpickle untrusted data**.
- Ō£ģ **Encoding** for text files in Python 3 defaults to **`utf-8`**.
- Ō£ģ **File size** for a plain-text string of $n$ ASCII chars is exactly $n$ bytes.
- Ō£ģ **The `os` and `pathlib` modules** (advanced) build portable file paths ŌĆö bonus marks in long answers.
- Ō£ģ **Memory vs Disk trade-off:** Variables are fast but ephemeral; files are slow but permanent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: And Running a Script. How Python Works? Using Editors - IDLE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** And Running a Script. How Python Works? Using Editors - IDLE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Running a Script" Mean in Python?

A **Python script** is a plain text file containing a sequence of Python statements, conventionally saved with the **`.py`** extension (e.g., `hello.py`). *Running a script* is the act of instructing the operating system to hand the file over to the **Python interpreter**, which then reads, translates, and executes the statements line-by-line (or rather, block-by-block) to produce the desired result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned)**
> A Python script is a reusable, standalone file containing Python source code. *Running* a script invokes the Python interpreter, which performs **lexical analysis ŌåÆ parsing ŌåÆ compilation to bytecode ŌåÆ interpretation by the PVM**, ultimately producing output or side effects on the host machine.

## 1.2 How Python Works ŌĆö The Two-Stage Execution Model

Python is a **bytecode-compiled, interpreted language**. It does NOT compile directly to native machine code like C/C++, nor is it purely line-by-line interpreted like old BASIC. Instead, it occupies a sweet spot:

| Stage | Component | What It Does |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Source Code** (`.py`) | Human-readable Python written by the programmer |
| 2 | **Compiler** (front-end) | Translates source ŌåÆ **Bytecode** (`.pyc`) ŌĆö a low-level, platform-independent instruction set |
| 3 | **Python Virtual Machine (PVM)** | Interprets bytecode instructions and maps them to the underlying hardware/OS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** Python is **NOT** a purely interpreted language. It compiles to **bytecode** (semi-compiled form), and the **PVM** then interprets that bytecode. This two-stage process is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The Multilingual Restaurant

Imagine you are an **English-speaking customer** in a restaurant in **France**, and the **chef only understands French**.

- **You (Programmer)** write your order in **English** (Python source code ŌĆö high-level, human-readable).
- The **waiter (Python Compiler)** translates your English order into a **standardized kitchen ticket in French** (Bytecode ŌĆö an intermediate, structured representation).
- The **chef (PVM)** reads the French ticket and **cooks the dish** (executes the instructions on hardware).
- The **dish arrives at your table** (program output).

The waiter (compiler) does NOT cook ŌĆö the chef (PVM) does. This is precisely how Python works under the hood.

## 1.4 What is IDLE?

**IDLE (Integrated Development and Learning Environment)** is the default editor that ships bundled with every standard CPython installation. It was created by **Guido van Rossum** (Python's creator) and is written in Python itself using the **Tkinter** GUI toolkit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IDLE is NOT a compiler and NOT an interpreter by itself.** It is a *front-end GUI* that simply calls the underlying `python.exe` / `python3` interpreter in the background. Students often confuse this in exams.

### IDLE Provides Two Sub-Windows
1. **Python Shell (Interactive Mode)** ŌĆö A REPL (`Read ŌåÆ Evaluate ŌåÆ Print ŌåÆ Loop`) where each statement is executed immediately upon pressing `Enter`.
2. **Script Editor** ŌĆö A standard text editor window where you can write, save, load, and run multi-line `.py` files using `F5` or `Run ŌåÆ Run Module`.

## 1.5 Physical Constants, Defaults & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Default file extension:** `.py` for source, `.pyc` for cached bytecode.
- **Bytecode cache location:** `__pycache__/` directory adjacent to the source.
- **Standard Python prompt:** `>>>` (primary) and `...` (continuation).
- **IDLE shortcut to run a script:** **F5** (or `Run ŌåÆ Run Module`).
- **Standard Python install size:** approximately **~25ŌĆō100 MB** depending on version and OS bundles.
- **Current stable major versions:** **Python 3.x** (Python 2 reached end-of-life on **January 1, 2020**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete Python Execution Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

When you run `python myscript.py`, the following orchestrated sequence occurs inside the interpreter:

### Step 1 ŌĆö **Lexical Analysis (Tokenization)**
The source file is read as raw characters. A component called the **Tokenizer** scans the stream left-to-right and groups characters into meaningful units called **tokens** (keywords, identifiers, operators, literals, punctuation).

### Step 2 ŌĆö **Parsing (Syntax Analysis)**
The token stream is fed into the **Parser**, which validates it against Python's grammar (the *Grammar/Grammar* file in CPython source). The parser constructs a **Concrete Syntax Tree (CST)** and then simplifies it to an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** ŌĆö a hierarchical tree representation of the program's logical structure.

### Step 3 ŌĆö **Compilation to Bytecode**
The AST is passed to the **Python Code Generator / Compiler**, which emits **bytecode instructions** ŌĆö low-level, stack-based operations stored as integers (opcodes). Each bytecode file is saved to disk as a **`.pyc`** file inside the `__pycache__` folder for faster re-execution.

### Step 4 ŌĆö **Interpretation by the PVM (Python Virtual Machine)**
The PVM is a **software-based CPU emulator**. It maintains an **instruction pointer**, a **stack** for operands, and a **namespace/dictionary** for variables. It iterates through bytecode instructions, dispatching each to a corresponding C function (in CPython) that performs the actual work.

### Step 5 ŌĆö **Memory & Object Management**
All Python data (numbers, strings, lists, functions) are **heap-allocated objects**. The **Reference Counter** frees an object the moment its count drops to zero. A cyclic **Garbage Collector (GC)** periodically hunts for unreachable reference cycles.

### Step 6 ŌĆö **Output / Side Effects**
Results are written to `stdout` (standard output) via the `print()` function, errors to `stderr`, and any file/network I/O is dispatched to the operating system via Python's C API.

## 2.2 Modes of Running Python

| Mode | How to Invoke | Behavior | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interactive Mode (REPL)** | Type `python` in terminal/IDLE Shell | Reads ŌåÆ Evaluates ŌåÆ Prints ŌåÆ Loops on each `Enter` | Quick testing, learning, debugging snippets |
| **Script Mode** | `python filename.py` | Reads entire file, compiles, executes top-to-bottom | Production programs, reusable code |
| **IDLE Script Editor** | Open IDLE ŌåÆ `File ŌåÆ New File` ŌåÆ write ŌåÆ `F5` | Same as Script Mode, but with GUI conveniences | Beginners, classroom labs |
| **Notebook / IDE Mode** | Jupyter, VS Code, PyCharm | Cell-based or full-project execution | Data Science, ML workflows |

## 2.3 IDLE ŌĆö Detailed Features Map

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| **Syntax Highlighting** | Color-codes keywords, strings, comments for readability |
| **Auto-Indentation** | Mirrors the indentation of the previous line automatically |
| **Smart Indent** (`Ctrl+\[`) | Auto-indents a pasted block correctly |
| **Code Completion** (`Ctrl+Space`) | Pop-up suggestions for attributes/methods (basic) |
| **Call Tips** | Shows function signature on typing `(` |
| **Debug Tool** | `Debug ŌåÆ Debugger` opens a pane with Step, Over, Out, Go, Quit |
| **Path Browser** | Inspect Python's `sys.path` and module search locations |
| **Run Module** | `F5` ŌĆö executes the current file in a fresh Python subprocess |

## 2.4 Comparison of Common Python Editors (KTU-Favorite Table)

| Editor | Type | Best For | Bundled with Python? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IDLE** | Lightweight IDE | Beginners, classroom, learning | **Yes** |
| **VS Code** | Extensible Code Editor | General development, ML, web | No (free) |
| **PyCharm** | Full-Featured IDE | Professional, large projects | No (Community = free) |
| **Jupyter Notebook** | Web-based Notebook | Data Science, ML experimentation | No (via Anaconda/pip) |
| **Sublime Text** | Text Editor | Quick edits, lightweight scripting | No (paid) |
| **Atom** | Hackable Text Editor | Light customization | No (discontinued) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Lab Exams (PEERT526):** The default expectation is **IDLE**, since it is pre-installed and zero-configuration. Knowing `F5` to run and `Ctrl+N` to open a new file is essential.

## 2.5 Why Bytecode? ŌĆö Engineering Rationale

- **Portability:** The same `.pyc` runs on any OS as long as PVM is available ŌåÆ enables the *"write once, run anywhere"* promise.
- **Speed:** Re-running a script skips the parsing/AST step because `.pyc` is cached.
- **Security/Sandboxing:** Bytecode is easier to inspect/validate than raw source for restricted environments.
- **Optimization surface:** Tools like **Cython**, **PyPy** (JIT), and **Nuitka** operate on or replace bytecode for performance.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ML Pipelines:** Scripts in `train.py` / `evaluate.py` are run in script mode from CI/CD systems (Airflow, Kubeflow).
- **Web Backends:** Flask/Django servers run as long-lived scripts under WSGI servers (Gunicorn, uWSGI).
- **Embedded Scripting:** Software like Blender, FreeCAD, and Maya embed a Python interpreter and use script mode for plugins.
- **Scientific Computing:** Researchers write `.py` scripts for batch processing of experimental data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section mirrors the **engineering derivation style** required for KTU analytical questions. For a programming topic, "derivation" = the *deterministic transformation* of source code into executed behavior, step-by-step.

## 3.1 Worked Example ŌĆö Tracing a Script Through the Pipeline

Consider the following minimal Python script saved as `greet.py`:

```python
x = 5
y = 3
total = x + y
print("Sum is", total)
```

### Step 1 ŌĆö Source Acquisition
The OS loads `greet.py` and hands the file handle to the CPython interpreter (`python.exe` or `python3`).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Tokenization
The tokenizer scans each character and emits the following token stream:

$$
\text{Token Stream} = [ \text{NAME}(x),\ \text{OP}(=),\ \text{NUMBER}(5),\ \text{NEWLINE},\ \text{NAME}(y),\ \text{OP}(=),\ \text{NUMBER}(3),\ \text{NEWLINE},\ \text{NAME}(total),\ \text{OP}(=),\ \text{NAME}(x),\ \text{OP}(+),\ \text{NAME}(y),\ \text{NEWLINE},\ \text{NAME}(print),\ \text{OP}(\text{(}),\ \text{STRING}("Sum\ is"),\ \text{OP}(,),\ \text{NAME}(total),\ \text{OP}(\text{)}),\ \text{NEWLINE} ]
$$

> *Each token is a tuple: (type, value, line, column).*

### Step 3 ŌĆö AST Construction
The parser builds an **Abstract Syntax Tree** with `Module` as root and four child statements.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AST} &= \text{Module}(\text{body} = [ \\
&\quad \text{Assign}(x,\ 5), \\
&\quad \text{Assign}(y,\ 3), \\
&\quad \text{Assign}(total,\ \text{BinOp}(x,\ \text{Add},\ y)), \\
&\quad \text{Expr}(\text{Call}(\text{print},\ ["Sum\ is",\ total])) \\
&])
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Bytecode Compilation
The compiler emits stack-based instructions. A simplified `dis.dis()` output would resemble:

```text
  1           RESUME                   0
  2           LOAD_SMALL_INT           5
              STORE_NAME               1 (x)
  3           LOAD_SMALL_INT           3
              STORE_NAME               1 (y)
  4           LOAD_NAME                1 (x)
              LOAD_NAME                1 (y)
              BINARY_OP                0 (+)
              STORE_NAME               2 (total)
  6           LOAD_GLOBAL              0 (print)
              LOAD_CONST               0 ('Sum is')
              LOAD_NAME                2 (total)
              CALL                     1
              RETURN                   None
```

### Step 5 ŌĆö PVM Execution Trace
The PVM executes the bytecode in a **fetch ŌåÆ decode ŌåÆ execute** cycle, analogous to a physical CPU:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1:} &\quad \text{Instruction Pointer (IP) = 0} \rightarrow \text{RESUME} \\
\text{Cycle 2:} &\quad \text{IP} = 1 \rightarrow \text{LOAD\_SMALL\_INT}\ 5 \rightarrow \text{Push 5 onto operand stack} \\
\text{Cycle 3:} &\quad \text{IP} = 2 \rightarrow \text{STORE\_NAME}\ x \rightarrow \text{Pop 5, bind to local 'x'} \\
&\quad \vdots \\
\text{Cycle 8:} &\quad \text{IP} = 7 \rightarrow \text{BINARY\_OP}\ (+) \rightarrow \text{Pop 5 and 3, push 8} \\
\text{Cycle 9:} &\quad \text{STORE\_NAME}\ total \rightarrow \text{Pop 8, bind to local 'total'} \\
\text{Cycle 10:} &\quad \text{LOAD\_GLOBAL}\ print \rightarrow \text{Push <print function>} \\
\text{Cycle 11:} &\quad \text{LOAD\_CONST}\ "Sum\ is" \rightarrow \text{Push string} \\
\text{Cycle 12:} &\quad \text{LOAD\_NAME}\ total \rightarrow \text{Push 8} \\
\text{Cycle 13:} &\quad \text{CALL}\ 1 \rightarrow \text{Invoke print}("Sum is", 8) \\
\text{Cycle 14:} &\quad \text{Output emitted to stdout:}\ \texttt{Sum is 8}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Interactive vs Script Mode

| Criterion | Interactive Mode (REPL) | Script Mode (`.py` file) |
|---|---|---|
| **Entry point** | `python` typed in shell | `python script.py` |
| **Execution unit** | One statement at a time | Entire file at once |
| **Code persistence** | Lost when session ends | Saved to disk |
| **Suitability for loops/functions** | Awkward (needs blank line after `def`) | Native and clean |
| **Use case in KTU labs** | Quick syntax checks | Full program submissions |
| **`__name__` value** | `__main__` | `__main__` (when run directly) |
| **Multi-line editing** | Limited | Full editor support |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö When Does `.pyc` Get Regenerated?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} &= \text{Compile new .pyc if} \\
&\quad [\text{Source .py does not exist}] \ \lor \\
&\quad [\text{Source .py mtime} > \text{.pyc mtime}] \ \lor \\
&\quad [\text{Source .py size} \ne \text{.pyc source-size field}] \ \lor \\
&\quad [\text{Python version mismatch}]
\end{aligned}
$$

> This conditional logic is what makes Python's startup faster on the second run.

## 3.4 Bytecode Instruction Format ŌĆö Formal Derivation

Each CPython bytecode instruction has the form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction} &= \text{Opcode}\ (\text{1 byte})\ \vert\ \text{arg}\ (\text{0, 1, or 2 bytes depending on opcode}) \\
\text{Opcode} &\in \left\{ 0, 1, 2,\ \ldots,\ 255 \right\} \\
\text{Execution} &= f(\text{Opcode},\ \text{arg},\ \text{OperandStack},\ \text{LocalNamespace})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\vert$ here is a **mathematical "such that / divides" pipe**, written using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in any KTU-typed answer sheet rendering. In printed answer scripts, students should simply write it as a vertical bar.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture ŌĆö Python Script Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer Writes Source Code .py File] --> B[Save File to Disk]
    B --> C[OS Invokes python interpreter]
    C --> D[Stage 1 Lexical Analysis Tokenizer]
    D --> E[Stage 2 Parsing Builds AST]
    E --> F[Stage 3 Compiler Emits Bytecode]
    F --> G{Is .pyc Cache Valid?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Load Cached .pyc from __pycache__]
    G -- No --> I[Write New .pyc to __pycache__]
    H --> J[Stage 4 Python Virtual Machine PVM]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Stage 5 Memory Management RefCount and GC]
    J --> L[Stage 6 Output to stdout stderr or File I/O]
    K --> J
    J --> M[Program Output or Runtime Error]
```

## 4.2 IDLE Architecture ŌĆö Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDLE_GUI[Tkinter Based IDLE Frontend]
        A1[Python Shell Window REPL] --> A2[Interactive >>> Prompt]
        A3[Script Editor Window] --> A4[F5 Run Module]
        A5[Debugger Pane] --> A6[Step Over Step Into Breakpoints]
        A7[Syntax Highlighting Engine] --> A3
    end
    IDLE_GUI --> B[Subprocess Call python executable]
    B --> C[CPython Interpreter]
    C --> D[Bytecode + PVM]
    D --> E[Program Output Back to Shell]
```

## 4.3 Editor Comparison Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose a Python Editor] --> Q1{Is this your first Python class?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> IDLE1[Use IDLE - Bundled and Simple]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Working on Machine Learning or Data Science?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Need interactive cell based execution?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> JN[Use Jupyter Notebook or JupyterLab]
    Q3 -- No --> VS1[Use VS Code with Python Extension]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Large production codebase?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> PC[Use PyCharm Professional or Community]
    Q4 -- No --> VS2[Use VS Code]
```

## 4.4 Bytecode Compilation ŌåÆ PVM Execution Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant OS
    participant Py as CPython Interpreter
    participant Comp as Compiler
    participant PVM as Python Virtual Machine
    participant HW as Operating System and Hardware
    User->>OS: python greet.py
    OS->>Py: Load and Initialize
    Py->>Comp: Read .py and Generate Bytecode
    Comp-->>Py: Bytecode .pyc Object
    Py->>PVM: Pass Bytecode for Execution
    loop Fetch Decode Execute
        PVM->>PVM: Process Next Bytecode Instruction
        PVM->>HW: Request OS Service if needed
        HW-->>PVM: Return Result
    end
    PVM-->>User: Program Output to stdout
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain how a Python script is executed. Differentiate between interactive mode and script mode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Python script is executed through a **two-stage process**. In the first stage, the **compiler** reads the source code (`.py` file), performs lexical analysis, parsing, and translates it into an intermediate representation called **bytecode** (`.pyc`). In the second stage, the **Python Virtual Machine (PVM)** interprets this bytecode instruction-by-instruction and performs the actual operations on the underlying hardware.

| Aspect | Interactive Mode | Script Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Invocation | Type `python` in terminal | `python filename.py` |
| Execution | One statement at a time | Entire file at once |
| Persistence | Lost on exit | Saved permanently |

*(1 mark for two-stage explanation, 1 mark for key terms ŌĆö compiler, bytecode, PVM, 1 mark for the comparison table.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is IDLE? List any four features of IDLE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**IDLE (Integrated Development and Learning Environment)** is the default GUI-based editor bundled with the standard CPython distribution. It provides an interactive Python Shell (REPL) and a script editor for writing `.py` files. It is written in Python using the Tkinter toolkit.

**Four key features of IDLE:**
1. **Syntax Highlighting** ŌĆö Color-codes keywords, strings, and comments.
2. **Smart Indent & Auto-completion** ŌĆö Automates Python's mandatory indentation and suggests completions.
3. **Integrated Debugger** ŌĆö Allows step-by-step execution with breakpoints.
4. **Run Module via F5** ŌĆö Compiles and executes the current file in a fresh Python subprocess.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four features with one-line descriptions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

> **Note on KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Module-level internal choice is provided. Both alternatives must be from the same module but test different cognitive levels per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete architecture of the **Python Interpreter**. Describe the role of each stage in transforming source code to executed output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the process of **lexical analysis and parsing** in Python. How does the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) aid in subsequent bytecode generation? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

The **Python Interpreter** follows a layered architecture consisting of six stages:

1. **Source Code Loader:** Reads the `.py` file from disk into memory. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Tokenizer / Lexer:** Converts the raw character stream into a token stream (identifiers, keywords, literals, operators). *(1 mark)*
3. **Parser:** Validates the token stream against Python's grammar and builds a **Concrete Syntax Tree**, then simplifies it to an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Compiler:** Walks the AST and emits **bytecode instructions** ŌĆö platform-independent, stack-based opcodes. Caches them as `.pyc` in `__pycache__`. *(1.5 marks)*
5. **Python Virtual Machine (PVM):** A software CPU that loops through bytecode instructions, maintaining an operand stack, instruction pointer, and local namespaces. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Memory & I/O Layer:** Handles object allocation (heap), reference counting, garbage collection, and OS-level I/O. *(1 mark)*

*+ 0.5 mark for the block diagram.*

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Lexical Analysis (Tokenization):**
- The source code is read character-by-character by the tokenizer.
- Whitespace and comments are ignored (except for indentation markers, which are significant in Python).
- The output is a flat sequence of **tokens**, each being a tuple `(type, value, line, column)`.
- Example: `x = 5` ŌåÆ `('NAME','x',1,1)`, `('OP','=',1,3)`, `('NUMBER',5,1,5)`. *(2 marks)*

**Parsing (Syntax Analysis):**
- The token stream is fed into the **Parser**, which uses a **context-free grammar** (LL(1) parser in CPython) to check syntactic validity.
- A **Concrete Syntax Tree** is built first, which is then condensed to an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**.
- The AST omits redundant syntactic details and represents the program's logical structure. *(2 marks)*

**Role of AST in Bytecode Generation:**
- The compiler traverses the AST in a depth-first manner.
- Each AST node type corresponds to specific bytecode emission patterns.
- The AST enables **optimization passes** (constant folding, dead-code elimination) before bytecode emission.
- Tools like `ast` module let programmers inspect/modify the AST programmatically. *(2 marks)*

*+ 1 mark for a clear example tracing source ŌåÆ tokens ŌåÆ AST.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **IDLE** with any two other popular Python editors. Mention the salient features of IDLE that make it suitable for beginners. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a step-by-step procedure to **create, save, and run a Python script** named `welcome.py` using IDLE that displays `Welcome to KTU PEERT526!` on the screen. Include screenshots (text-described) of each step. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | IDLE | VS Code | PyCharm |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | Free, bundled | Free | Community = free |
| Installation | Comes with Python | Separate download | Separate, large |
| Memory Footprint | Very low | Medium | High |
| Beginner Friendly | ŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģŌśģ | ŌśģŌśģŌśģ | ŌśģŌśģ |
| ML/Debugging Tools | Basic | Extensions needed | Built-in pro |
| Plugin Ecosystem | Minimal | Massive | Rich (Pro) |

*(2 marks for the comparison table)*

**Salient features of IDLE for beginners:**
1. **Zero Setup:** Pre-installed with Python ŌĆö no separate download. *(1 mark)*
2. **Integrated REPL:** Instant feedback while learning syntax. *(1 mark)*
3. **One-key Execution:** `F5` runs the script without needing terminal commands. *(1 mark)*
4. **Friendly Debugger:** Step-through execution helps visualize program flow. *(1 mark)*
5. **Lightweight & Cross-platform:** Runs on Windows, macOS, Linux with identical UI. *(1 mark)*

#### Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks

**Procedure to create, save, and run `welcome.py` in IDLE:**

| Step | Action | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Launch IDLE** | Click Start ŌåÆ type `IDLE` ŌåÆ open | IDLE Shell window appears with `>>>` prompt |
| **2. New File** | `File ŌåÆ New File` (or `Ctrl+N`) | Untitled editor window opens |
| **3. Write Code** | Type `print("Welcome to KTU PEERT526!")` | Code appears in the editor |
| **4. Save File** | `File ŌåÆ Save` (or `Ctrl+S`) ŌåÆ filename `welcome.py` ŌåÆ Save | File saved with `.py` extension |
| **5. Run Script** | `Run ŌåÆ Run Module` (or press `F5`) | If unsaved, IDLE prompts to save; then executes |
| **6. Observe Output** | Switch back to IDLE Shell | `Welcome to KTU PEERT526!` is printed on the next line after `=== RESTART ===` |

*(5 marks for the step-by-step table)*

*+ 1 mark for correct code: `print("Welcome to KTU PEERT526!`)*

*+ 1 mark for noting the `=== RESTART ===` shell marker and absence of `.py` extension error.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls**
> - **Do NOT confuse "Python is interpreted" with "Python is NOT compiled."** Always mention the bytecode compilation step to secure full marks on the two-stage question.
> - **Do NOT call IDLE an "interpreter" or "compiler."** It is strictly a **GUI front-end** that calls the interpreter in a subprocess. Examiners deduct 1ŌĆō2 marks for this slip.
> - **Do NOT skip the `.pyc` cache file mention** when describing execution. The presence of the `__pycache__/` folder is a strong indicator of full understanding.
> - **Do NOT write `print` without parentheses** (Python 2 style). The 2024 scheme expects **Python 3.x** syntax throughout.
> - **Do NOT forget the `if __name__ == "__main__":` idiom** if asked about script-vs-module invocation ŌĆö it is a frequently tested advanced concept.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Python is a **bytecode-compiled, interpreted language** ŌĆö NOT purely interpreted, NOT purely compiled.
- [x] The execution pipeline is: **Source ŌåÆ Tokens ŌåÆ AST ŌåÆ Bytecode ŌåÆ PVM Execution ŌåÆ Output**.
- [x] **Bytecode** is platform-independent and cached as `.pyc` inside `__pycache__/`.
- [x] The **Python Virtual Machine (PVM)** is the actual runtime engine; it is a software CPU emulator.
- [x] **IDLE = Integrated Development and Learning Environment** ŌĆö Tkinter-based, bundled with CPython.
- [x] IDLE is a **GUI front-end**, NOT a compiler or interpreter itself.
- [x] **Two modes of running Python:** Interactive (REPL with `>>>`) and Script (`python file.py`).
- [x] **Interactive mode** = one statement at a time, lost on exit; **Script mode** = persistent, multi-line.
- [x] **F5** in IDLE runs the current file; **Ctrl+N** opens a new file; **Ctrl+S** saves.
- [x] All Python data are **heap-allocated objects** managed by **reference counting + cyclic GC**.
- [x] **Default file extensions:** `.py` (source), `.pyc` (cached bytecode).
- [x] **Python 2 is EOL** (January 1, 2020); all KTU 2024 work uses **Python 3.x**.
- [x] Popular alternatives to IDLE: **VS Code, PyCharm, Jupyter Notebook** ŌĆö IDLE is preferred for KTU labs.
- [x] The **Tokenizer** produces a token stream; the **Parser** produces the AST; the **Compiler** produces bytecode.
- [x] The AST can be inspected and modified using Python's built-in `ast` module.
- [x] Always use **`print()` with parentheses** ŌĆö Python 3 syntax is mandatory in KTU 2024 scheme.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jupyter. Basic Coding Skills - Working with Data Types
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Jupyter. Basic Coding Skills - Working with Data Types

# Module 1 ŌĆö Jupyter & Basic Coding Skills: Working with Data Types

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Jupyter Notebook ŌĆö The Computational Lab

**Jupyter Notebook** is an open-source, browser-based interactive computing environment that allows a programmer to write and execute Python code in a document that combines live code, equations, visualizations, and narrative text. For KTU 2024 Scheme, Jupyter is the **official pedagogical platform** for PEERT526 because it enables *literate programming* ŌĆö mixing explanation with execution.

Formally, a Jupyter Notebook (file extension **`.ipynb`** ŌĆö short for *Interactive Python Notebook*) consists of a sequence of **cells**. Each cell can be of one of three types:

| Cell Type | Purpose | Kernel Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Code Cell** | Contains executable Python | Sent to kernel; output returned |
| **Markdown Cell** | Contains formatted text/LaTeX | Rendered in HTML; not executed |
| **Raw Cell** | Unformatted plain text | Passed through unchanged |

The **kernel** is the computational engine (typically IPython for Python) that maintains the live state of variables between cell executions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Jupyter is *not* just a text editor. It is a **REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)** wrapped in a notebook UI with persistent state. This means a variable defined in Cell 5 is still accessible in Cell 47 ŌĆö a critical concept students confuse with a regular IDE.

### 1.2 Python Data Types ŌĆö The Intuitive Analogy

A **data type** is a classification that specifies which kind of value a variable can hold and what operations can be performed on it. Python is a **dynamically typed** language, meaning the interpreter infers the type at runtime without explicit declaration (contrast with C's `int x = 5;`).

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Warehouse of Boxes:**
Imagine Python's memory as a giant warehouse. Every time you write `x = 42`, you grab an **empty box**, paste a label `x` on it, and place the integer `42` inside. If you later write `x = "hello"`, you don't change the box ŌĆö you **replace the contents** with a different *kind* of object (now a string). Each *kind* of object (integer, float, string, list, etc.) comes with its own set of rules ŌĆö its **type**.

- **Type inference** happens at assignment time.
- **Strong typing** means Python will not silently mix incompatible types (e.g., `"5" + 3` raises `TypeError` instead of guessing).
- The built-in function **`type(x)`** returns the *exact* class of any object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> *A data type is a category of data values that defines the operations that can be performed on them, the meaning of the data, and the way values of that type can be stored.* ŌĆö Standard textbook phrasing.

### 1.3 The Two Super-Families: Scalar vs. Collection

Python's built-in types fall under **two super-families**:

1. **Scalar (Atomic) Types** ŌĆö Hold a *single* value. Immutable.
   - `int`, `float`, `complex`, `bool`, `str`, `NoneType`
2. **Collection (Container) Types** ŌĆö Hold *multiple* values. May be mutable or immutable.
   - `list`, `tuple`, `set`, `dict`

**Immutability** is a key concept: an immutable object cannot be changed in place after creation. If you "modify" it, Python actually creates a *new* object. **`int`, `float`, `bool`, `str`, `tuple`** are immutable. **`list`, `set`, `dict`** are mutable.

### 1.4 Key Constants & Limits

- Python `int` has **arbitrary precision** ŌĆö limited only by available memory. (Unlike C's `int` which is 32/64-bit.)
- **`sys.maxsize`** = $2^{63} - 1 = 9{,}223{,}372{,}036{,}854{,}775{,}807$ on a **64-bit** system.
- **`float`** is IEEE 754 **double-precision (64-bit)** ŌĆö ~15ŌĆō17 significant decimal digits, range roughly $\pm 10^{\pm 308}$.
- **`bool`** is a subclass of `int` ŌåÆ `True == 1`, `False == 0`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Built-in Data Types

#### 2.1.1 Numeric Types ŌĆö `int`, `float`, `complex`

- **`int` (Integer):** Whole numbers, positive or negative, with **no decimal point**. Internally stored as base-2 binary.
- **`float` (Floating-Point):** Real numbers with a decimal point or in scientific notation (e.g., `3.14e-2`). Follows the **IEEE 754 double-precision binary floating-point** standard.
- **`complex`:** Numbers of the form $a + bj$, where $j$ is the imaginary unit ($j^2 = -1$). Accessed as `z.real` and `z.imag`.

#### 2.1.2 `bool` ŌĆö Boolean

Logical values **`True`** and **`False`**. Note the **capitalization** ŌĆö this is a frequent KTU exam pitfall. Internally `True` behaves like integer `1`, and `False` like `0`. They are the *result* of comparison and logical operations.

#### 2.1.3 `str` ŌĆö String

A **sequence of Unicode characters**. Defined using single quotes `'...'`, double quotes `"..."`, or triple quotes `'''...'''` for multi-line. Strings are **immutable sequences** ŌĆö you cannot change a character in place; you must create a new string.

#### 2.1.4 `list` ŌĆö List

An **ordered, mutable, indexable** collection that can hold **heterogeneous** items (mixed types allowed). Defined with square brackets `[ ]`. Supports indexing (`lst[0]`), slicing (`lst[1:4]`), and in-place modification (`lst[2] = 99`).

#### 2.1.5 `tuple` ŌĆö Tuple

An **ordered, immutable** collection. Defined with parentheses `( )`. Once created, items cannot be changed. Used for fixed records and as dictionary keys.

#### 2.1.6 `set` ŌĆö Set

An **unordered, mutable collection of unique** elements. Defined with braces `{ }` or `set()`. Supports mathematical set operations: union, intersection, difference. **Cannot contain mutable items** (lists can't go inside sets).

#### 2.1.7 `dict` ŌĆö Dictionary

An **unordered (insertion-ordered since Python 3.7), mutable** mapping of **key-value pairs**. Keys must be **unique and immutable**. Defined with `{key: value, ...}` syntax.

### 2.2 Type Conversion (Type Casting)

Python provides built-in constructor functions that act as type converters. The convention is *function-call* syntax: `int(x)`, `float(x)`, `str(x)`, `list(x)`, `tuple(x)`, `set(x)`, `bool(x)`.

**Why conversion matters:** ML libraries like NumPy and Pandas demand specific types. A CSV column read as `str` must be cast to `float` before training a regression model.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as the **last-page reference** during revision. KTU examiners frequently test these conversions in Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Operation / Syntax | Result Type | Example Expression | Output | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `int("42")` | `int` | `int("42")` | `42` | String must contain only digits |
| `int(3.9)` | `int` | `int(3.9)` | `3` | Truncates toward zero (no rounding) |
| `float("3.14")` | `float` | `float("3.14")` | `3.14` | Standard decimal parse |
| `str(2024)` | `str` | `str(2024)` | `'2024'` | Used for concatenation |
| `bool(0)` | `bool` | `bool(0)` | `False` | Zero is falsy |
| `bool("hi")` | `bool` | `bool("hi")` | `True` | Non-empty string is truthy |
| `list("abc")` | `list` | `list("abc")` | `['a','b','c']` | Splits iterable into chars |
| `tuple([1,2])` | `tuple` | `tuple([1,2])` | `(1,2)` | Freezes the list |
| `set([1,1,2])` | `set` | `set([1,1,2])` | `{1,2}` | Removes duplicates |
| `type(x)` | `type` object | `type(3.0)` | `<class 'float'>` | Returns the class |
| `isinstance(x, int)` | `bool` | `isinstance(5, int)` | `True` | Preferred over `type()` for subclass checks |
| `len(seq)` | `int` | `len([1,2,3])` | `3` | Works on strings, lists, tuples, dicts |

### 2.4 The 'Why' Behind Dynamic Typing

Python achieves dynamic typing through **object references**. A variable name is just a **pointer (reference)** bound to an object in memory. The object itself carries a type tag and a reference count.

This design has two engineering consequences:
1. **Flexibility:** A single variable can hold any type over its lifetime ŌĆö ideal for data exploration in Jupyter.
2. **Performance cost:** Every operation requires a runtime type check. CPython mitigates this with *type-specialization* in the bytecode interpreter.

In the ML pipeline, this dynamic flexibility lets data scientists rapidly prototype: a column of integers can be reassigned as a categorical string without compile-time errors ŌĆö but **silent bugs** (e.g., `NaN` propagating as `float`) require vigilance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough ŌĆö Type Detection and Conversion

Below is an **analytical trace** showing how Jupyter's kernel evaluates a sequence of expressions step-by-step. This is the style of reasoning KTU examiners expect in 7-mark derivations.

**Problem:** Evaluate the following Jupyter cells in order and explain the state of each variable.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Initialization**

```python
a = 10
b = 3.5
c = "100"
```

- The kernel creates three objects: `int(10)`, `float(3.5)`, `str("100")`.
- Names `a`, `b`, `c` are bound to these objects in the local namespace.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Examine Types**

```python
print(type(a), type(b), type(c))
```

- Logic: `type(a)` queries the object referenced by `a` ŌåÆ returns `<class 'int'>`.
- Similarly `type(b)` ŌåÆ `float`, `type(c)` ŌåÆ `str`.
- Output: `<class 'int'> <class 'float'> <class 'str'>`

**Step 3 ŌĆö Attempt a Mixed Operation**

```python
result = a + c
```

- Logic: Python invokes the `int.__add__` method. Since `c` is a `str`, there is no registered addition between `int` and `str`.
- Result: `TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'int' and 'str'`

**Step 4 ŌĆö Fix via Explicit Conversion**

```python
result = a + int(c)
```

- Step-by-step:
  - `int(c)` parses the string `"100"` ŌåÆ integer `100`.
  - `a + int(c)` ŌåÆ `10 + 100` ŌåÆ `110`.
- Final result: `result = 110` (type `int`).

**Step 5 ŌĆö Implicit Conversion Pitfall**

```python
x = 5 / 2
```

- In Python 3, the `/` operator is **true division** and **always returns `float`**.
- Result: `x = 2.5` (note this differs from C/Java which would yield `2` with `/`).
- If integer division is desired: `x = 5 // 2` ŌåÆ `2` (floor division, also `float` if either operand is float, else `int`).

### 3.2 Numerical Reasoning ŌĆö Floating-Point Precision

Floating-point numbers in Python follow the **IEEE 754** standard. Let us derive why `0.1 + 0.2 ŌēĀ 0.3`.

**Derivation:**

$$
0.1_{10} = 0.000110011001100\ldots_2
$$

This is a **non-terminating binary fraction**, so it must be rounded to 52 significant bits. Let:

$$
a = \text{round}(0.1) = 0.10000000000000000555\ldots_{10}
$$

$$
b = \text{round}(0.2) = 0.20000000000000001110\ldots_{10}
$$

Adding the two approximations:

$$
a + b = 0.30000000000000004440\ldots_{10}
$$

Which is **not equal** to the rounded value of $0.3$:

$$
c = \text{round}(0.3) = 0.29999999999999998889\ldots_{10}
$$

Hence:

$$
0.1 + 0.2 \neq 0.3
$$

In Jupyter, verify with:

```python
0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3     # False
round(0.1 + 0.2, 1) == 0.3   # True
```

The proper ML workflow uses `math.isclose()` or `numpy.isclose()` for such comparisons.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Collection Types Side-by-Side

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners often set a **comparison question** (e.g., *"Differentiate between list, tuple, set, and dict"* ŌĆö 7 marks). The matrix below is the gold-standard answer skeleton.

| Property | `list` | `tuple` | `set` | `dict` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Syntax** | `[1,2,3]` | `(1,2,3)` | `{1,2,3}` | `{'a':1,'b':2}` |
| **Ordered?** | Yes | Yes | **No** | Insertion-ordered (3.7+) |
| **Mutable?** | Yes | **No** | Yes | Yes |
| **Duplicates?** | Allowed | Allowed | **Disallowed** | Keys must be unique |
| **Indexable?** | Yes (`lst[0]`) | Yes (`tup[0]`) | **No** | By key (`d['a']`) |
| **Heterogeneous?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (immutable items only) | Yes |
| **Use Case** | Dynamic sequence | Fixed record, dict key | Membership test, dedup | Lookup table |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step ŌĆö Boolean Coercion Rules

Python's `bool()` function uses the following deterministic rules. These are **frequently asked** in 3-mark questions.

| Input to `bool(x)` | Result |
|---|---|
| `False`, `0`, `0.0`, `0j` | `False` |
| `''`, `[]`, `()`, `{}`, `set()` | `False` |
| `None` | `False` |
| Everything else (non-zero number, non-empty container) | `True` |

**Derivation Example:**

$$
\text{bool}([]) \rightarrow \text{False} \quad \text{(empty container, falsy)}
$$

$$
\text{bool}([0]) \rightarrow \text{True} \quad \text{(non-empty container, even if element is falsy)}
$$

This is a **container truthiness** rule ŌĆö the container itself is checked, not its contents.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Jupyter Architecture ŌĆö Block-Level Functional Flow

The following diagram maps the **client-server interaction** when a user runs a code cell. KTU examiners appreciate diagrams that show the *flow of execution*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    browser[Web Browser UI] -->|HTTP request| server[Jupyter Server]
    server -->|routes cell| kernel[IPython Kernel]
    kernel -->|executes Python| state[In-Memory Namespace]
    state -->|variable bindings| kernel
    kernel -->|stdout / stderr / plots| server
    server -->|JSON response| browser
    notebookFile[(.ipynb File on Disk)] -.->|save/load| server
    style browser fill:#FFD580
    style server fill:#9EC1CF
    style kernel fill:#B5EAD7
    style state fill:#FFD3B6
    style notebookFile fill:#C7CEEA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The browser sends the cell source to the **server**.
- The server forwards it to the **kernel**, which runs it.
- The kernel consults its **in-memory state** (where all your variables live).
- Output flows back: kernel ŌåÆ server ŌåÆ browser.
- The `.ipynb` file on disk is updated only when the user *saves* ŌĆö it is not the live state.

### 4.2 Python Data-Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    objectRoot[object - Base of all types]
    objectRoot --> numeric[Numeric]
    objectRoot --> sequence[Sequence]
    objectRoot --> mapping[Mapping]
    objectRoot --> setGrp[Set]
    objectRoot --> noneType[NoneType]

    numeric --> intT[int]
    numeric --> floatT[float]
    numeric --> complexT[complex]
    numeric --> boolT[bool]

    sequence --> strT[str]
    sequence --> listT[list]
    sequence --> tupleT[tuple]

    mapping --> dictT[dict]
    setGrp --> setT[set]
    setGrp --> frozensetT[frozenset]

    intT -.subclass.- boolT
    listT -.mutable.- listT
    tupleT -.immutable.- tupleT

    style objectRoot fill:#FFE08A
    style numeric fill:#A8E6CF
    style sequence fill:#FFAAA5
    style mapping fill:#DCD6F7
    style setGrp fill:#FDFFB6
    style boolT fill:#FFB3BA
```

**Key takeaways from the diagram:**
1. **`bool` is a subclass of `int`** (dotted line). This is why `True + True == 2`.
2. **Sequences are ordered**; **Sets are not.**
3. Every type in Python ultimately inherits from **`object`** ŌĆö the root of the type hierarchy.

### 4.3 Jupyter Execution Lifecycle of a Cell

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant N as Notebook UI
    participant S as Jupyter Server
    participant K as IPython Kernel

    U->>N: Click "Run" or press Shift+Enter
    N->>S: Send cell source code
    S->>K: Execute code in current namespace
    K->>K: Parse -> Compile to bytecode -> Execute
    K-->>S: Return output (stdout, return value, errors)
    S-->>N: Send JSON payload
    N-->>U: Render output below cell
    Note over K: Kernel state persists across cells
```

This **stateful kernel** is the single most important Jupyter concept: variables survive between cells. If you redefine `x` in cell 10, cell 5's reference to `x` will use the *new* value. Use **Kernel ŌåÆ Restart** to clear state.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (2 ├Ś 3 Marks)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Explain the difference between mutable and immutable data types in Python. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A *mutable* data type allows its contents to be modified in place after creation, whereas an *immutable* type cannot be changed once created; any "modification" produces a new object in memory.
- **Mutable examples (1 mark):** `list` ŌĆö e.g., `lst = [1,2,3]; lst[0] = 99` succeeds. `dict` ŌĆö e.g., `d = {'a':1}; d['a'] = 2` succeeds.
- **Immutable examples (1 mark):** `str` ŌĆö e.g., `s = "hi"; s[0] = 'H'` raises `TypeError`. `tuple` ŌĆö e.g., `t = (1,2); t[0] = 9` raises `TypeError`.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]`

**What is the output of the following code? Justify.**

```python
print(type(5.0))
print(bool(""))
print(3 * "ab")
```

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `type(5.0)` ŌåÆ `<class 'float'>` (1 mark) ŌĆö because the literal `5.0` has a decimal point, so it is interpreted as a floating-point number, not an integer.
- `bool("")` ŌåÆ `False` (1 mark) ŌĆö empty string is a falsy value per Python's truthiness rules.
- `3 * "ab"` ŌåÆ `'ababab'` (1 mark) ŌĆö the `*` operator on a string and an integer performs **string repetition**, concatenating `"ab"` three times.

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
**Differentiate between Python's `list`, `tuple`, `set`, and `dict` with respect to ordering, mutability, indexing, and duplicate handling. Provide one use-case for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Ordered? | Mutable? | Indexable? | Duplicates? | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `list` | Yes | Yes | By integer index | Allowed | Dynamic collection, e.g., appending readings from a sensor stream. |
| `tuple` | Yes | No | By integer index | Allowed | Fixed record, e.g., `(latitude, longitude)` coordinate. |
| `set` | No | Yes | **No** | **Disallowed** | Deduplication, e.g., extracting unique tokens from a document. |
| `dict` | Yes (insertion) | Yes | By **key** | Keys must be unique | Lookup table, e.g., mapping student ID ŌåÆ grade. |

**Step-by-step reasoning (to fetch full 7 marks):**
- Correctly state ordering property for each ŌĆö **1 mark per type (4 marks total)**.
- Correctly state mutability and indexing ŌĆö **1 mark** each (2 marks).
- Provide a coherent use case ŌĆö **1 mark**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
**Write a Jupyter-style Python program that:**
1. Takes a list of mixed-type elements: `[10, "20", 30.5, "forty", 40, "50.5"]`
2. Separates them into three new lists: integers, floats, and invalid strings.
3. Computes the sum of the numeric values.

**Model Solution:**

```python
raw = [10, "20", 30.5, "forty", 40, "50.5"]

integers, floats, invalid = [], [], []

for item in raw:
    if isinstance(item, int) and not isinstance(item, bool):
        integers.append(item)
    elif isinstance(item, float):
        floats.append(item)
    elif isinstance(item, str):
        try:
            # try int first, then float
            if "." in item:
                floats.append(float(item))
            else:
                integers.append(int(item))
        except ValueError:
            invalid.append(item)

print("Integers :", integers)   # [10, 20, 40]
print("Floats   :", floats)     # [30.5, 50.5]
print("Invalid  :", invalid)    # ['forty']
print("Sum      :", sum(integers) + sum(floats))  # 151.0
```

**Step-by-step justification (marking scheme):**
- Correct use of `isinstance()` for type-checking ŌĆö **2 marks**.
- Use of `try/except ValueError` for string-to-number conversion ŌĆö **2 marks**.
- Three correct output lists after execution ŌĆö **2 marks**.
- Final correct sum ŌĆö **1 mark**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**
**Explain dynamic typing in Python. Why does `0.1 + 0.2` not equal `0.3`? How should a Machine Learning engineer handle this in numerical code?**

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Typing (3 marks):**
- Python is dynamically typed ŌĆö variable types are determined at runtime, not at compile time.
- A variable name is a *reference* to an object; the object itself stores the type.
- The same variable can be reassigned to objects of different types: `x = 5; x = "hello"` is legal.
- Built-in `type()` returns the class; `isinstance()` checks inheritance.

**Floating-Point Imprecision (2 marks):**
- Python `float` follows IEEE 754 double-precision binary representation.
- The decimal $0.1$ has no exact binary expansion ($0.1 = 0.0001100110011\ldots_2$), so it is rounded to the nearest representable 64-bit value.
- Hence $0.1 + 0.2$ evaluates to approximately $0.30000000000000004$, not $0.3$.

**ML Best Practice (2 marks):**
- Use `math.isclose(a, b, rel_tol=1e-9)` or `numpy.isclose(a, b)` for floating-point comparisons.
- Use `decimal.Decimal` for financial or high-precision arithmetic.
- Use `np.float64` consistently in NumPy pipelines.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**
**For the dictionary `data = {"age": [21, 25, None, 30], "salary": [50000, None, 60000, 70000]}`, write Python code that:**
1. Replaces every `None` with the column mean (for numeric columns).
2. Casts the cleaned `age` column to integer type.
3. Prints the cleaned dictionary.

**Model Solution:**

```python
data = {"age": [21, 25, None, 30], "salary": [50000, None, 60000, 70000]}

for key, values in data.items():
    clean_vals = [v for v in values if v is not None]
    mean_val = sum(clean_vals) / len(clean_vals)
    data[key] = [v if v is not None else mean_val for v in values]

# Cast age to int
data["age"] = [int(v) for v in data["age"]]

print(data)
# {'age': [21, 25, 25, 30], 'salary': [50000, 60000.0, 60000, 70000]}
```

**Marking scheme:**
- Correct identification of `None` values via `is None` ŌĆö **2 marks**.
- Correct computation of column mean excluding `None` ŌĆö **2 marks**.
- Correct list-comprehension replacement ŌĆö **2 marks**.
- Type casting to `int` ŌĆö **1 mark**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Capitalization of `True`/`False`/`None`** ŌĆö lowercase versions (`true`, `none`) are undefined variables and throw `NameError`. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing `is` with `==`** ŌĆö `is` checks *object identity* (same memory address); `==` checks *value equality*. Using `is` for string/int comparison is a classic mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting that `bool` is a subclass of `int`** ŌĆö `True + True == 2` is a frequent trick question.
> 4. **Not mentioning Kernel Restart** in Jupyter-related questions ŌĆö the examiner expects you to know that kernel state persists.
> 5. **Using `type(x) == int` instead of `isinstance(x, int)`** ŌĆö the former fails for subclasses; the latter is the Pythonic and KTU-preferred form.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap ŌĆö Rapid-Revision Checklist

- Ō£ģ **Jupyter** = browser-based REPL with cells; uses **IPython kernel**; state persists across cells.
- Ō£ģ File extension is **`.ipynb`** (JSON under the hood).
- Ō£ģ Cell types: **Code**, **Markdown**, **Raw**.
- Ō£ģ Python is **dynamically and strongly typed**.
- Ō£ģ Use `type()` to inspect, `isinstance()` to check.
- Ō£ģ **Scalar types:** `int` (arbitrary precision), `float` (IEEE 754), `complex`, `bool` (subclass of `int`), `str`, `NoneType`.
- Ō£ģ **Collection types:** `list` (mutable, ordered), `tuple` (immutable, ordered), `set` (mutable, unordered, unique), `dict` (mutable, key-value mapping).
- Ō£ģ **Mutability rule:** `list`, `set`, `dict` are mutable; `int`, `float`, `str`, `tuple`, `frozenset`, `bool` are immutable.
- Ō£ģ **Type conversion:** `int()`, `float()`, `str()`, `bool()`, `list()`, `tuple()`, `set()`, `dict()` ŌĆö name of type is the constructor.
- Ō£ģ **Truthiness:** Zero, empty containers, `None`, `False` are falsy. Everything else is truthy.
- Ō£ģ **`/`** = true division (returns float); **`//`** = floor division; **`%`** = modulus; **`**`** = power.
- Ō£ģ **`0.1 + 0.2 ŌēĀ 0.3`** ŌĆö use `math.isclose()` or `numpy.isclose()` for FP comparisons.
- Ō£ģ **String repetition:** `n * s` or `s * n` repeats `s` `n` times.
- Ō£ģ **Deduplication idiom:** `list(set(my_list))` ŌĆö but loses order; use `dict.fromkeys()` if order matters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numeric Data Types and Character Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numeric Data Types and Character Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Numeric Data Types ŌĆö Formal Definition

In the context of the **PEERT526 ŌĆō Python Basics for Machine Learning** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), **Numeric Data Types** refer to the built-in Python data classes that store numerical values used for arithmetic computation, statistical operations, and the core mathematical layer of Machine Learning algorithms. The three primary numeric types defined by the Python language specification (PEP 3141 ŌĆö Numeric Tower) are:

1. **`int`** ŌĆö Arbitrary precision integers.
2. **`float`** ŌĆö IEEE 754 double-precision binary floating-point numbers.
3. **`complex`** ŌĆö Numbers with real and imaginary parts, stored as a pair of `float` values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *data type* is a classification of data which tells the compiler/interpreter how the programmer intends to use the data. In Python, numeric types are **immutable** ŌĆö meaning once a numeric object is created in memory, its value cannot be altered; reassignment creates a new object.

## 1.2 Character Set ŌĆö Formal Definition

A **Character Set** (or *character repertoire*) is a finite, well-defined collection of distinct characters (letters, digits, punctuation marks, control codes, ideographs, emojis) that a computing system can represent, store, manipulate, and render. Each character in the set is assigned a unique numeric code point. The two most relevant character sets in KTU-level Python programming are:

- **ASCII** (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) ŌĆö 7-bit, **128** code points (0 to 127).
- **Unicode** ŌĆö A universal character set that can theoretically address **1,114,112** code points (U+0000 to U+10FFFF) across 17 planes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout ŌĆö Character Encoding vs Character Set**
> A **character set** maps characters to code points. An **encoding** (e.g., UTF-8, UTF-16) defines how those code points are represented as bytes. Confusing these two concepts is a common KTU exam pitfall.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 ŌĆö Numeric Types as Containers:** Think of numeric data types as differently shaped *jugs* in a kitchen. An `int` jug is a large, whole-numbered container that can hold any quantity of whole apples (you can keep adding apples infinitely ŌĆö Python adjusts the jug size). A `float` jug is a measuring cylinder marked with fine milliliter divisions ŌĆö perfect for partial amounts, but it can introduce small rounding errors. A `complex` jug is a special two-chamber container that holds a *real* apple in one chamber and an *imaginary* (theoretical) apple in the other.

**Analogy 2 ŌĆö Character Set as a Postal System:** Imagine the world needs a unique postal code for every character ever used by any human language. **ASCII** is a small-town postal system with only 128 codes (good for English). **Unicode** is the global postal system with over a million codes ŌĆö English, Malayalam, Mandarin, Arabic, and even emojis all have unique, permanent addresses.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bold-Flagged)

| Metric / Constant | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **ASCII range** | **0 ŌĆō 127** | 7-bit encoding range |
| **Extended ASCII range** | **0 ŌĆō 255** | 8-bit (Latin-1) range |
| **Unicode range** | **0 ŌĆō 1,114,111** | U+0000 to U+10FFFF |
| **IEEE 754 double precision** | **64 bits** | Storage size of `float` in CPython |
| **Mantissa precision (`float`)** | **~15ŌĆō17 significant decimal digits** | Display/precision limit |
| **Maximum `int` size** | **Unlimited (RAM-bound)** | Python 3 arbitrary precision |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall:** `sys.maxsize` on a 64-bit system returns $2^{63} - 1 = 9,223,372,036,854,775,807$. This is the largest *practical* integer the OS can address, though Python `int` itself has no fixed upper limit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Python Numeric Tower

Python's numeric types form a logical hierarchy (the *numeric tower*) where subtypes are *subclasses* of their broader type. This is encoded in `numbers` module of the standard library.

```
numbers.Number
   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ numbers.Complex
   Ōöé       ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ numbers.Real
   Ōöé              ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ numbers.Rational
   Ōöé                     ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ numbers.Integral
   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ int        (inherits Integral)
   Ōö£ŌöĆŌöĆ float      (inherits Real)
   ŌööŌöĆŌöĆ complex    (inherits Complex)
```

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Numeric Type

### 2.2.1 `int` ŌĆö Integers

- **Storage:** Arbitrary precision. CPython uses a C struct with an array of "digits" in base $2^{30}$ or $2^{15}$ depending on the build.
- **Literal forms:**
  - Decimal: `42`
  - Binary (prefix `0b`): `0b101010`
  - Octal (prefix `0o`): `0o52`
  - Hexadecimal (prefix `0x`): `0x2A`
- **Why it matters for ML:** Loop counters, indices, dimensions, label encodings, epoch counts.

### 2.2.2 `float` ŌĆö Floating-Point Numbers

- **Storage:** IEEE 754 double-precision (binary64), 64 bits = 1 sign + 11 exponent + 52 mantissa.
- **Why and How:** Most ML computations (gradients, weights, activations) live in `float` because GPUs/CPUs have hardware acceleration. However, **floating-point cannot represent every decimal exactly** (e.g., `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3`).
- **Special values:** `inf`, `-inf`, `nan` (Not a Number).

### 2.2.3 `complex` ŌĆö Complex Numbers

- **Syntax:** `x + yj` (note: Python uses `j`, not `i`, for the imaginary unit).
- **Internal storage:** Two C doubles ŌĆö one for real part, one for imaginary part.
- **Why it matters for ML:** Fourier transforms (used in signal processing and some neural network architectures), eigenvalue computations, and quantum-inspired algorithms.

## 2.3 Character Set Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 ASCII (1963, ANSI X3.4)

- **7 bits** = **128 code points** (0 to 127).
- Range breakdown:
  - **0 ŌĆō 31:** Control characters (NUL, TAB, LF, CR, ESC...).
  - **32 ŌĆō 47:** Space and punctuation.
  - **48 ŌĆō 57:** Digits `'0'` to `'9'`.
  - **58 ŌĆō 64:** More punctuation.
  - **65 ŌĆō 90:** Uppercase letters `'A'` to `'Z'`.
  - **91 ŌĆō 96:** Brackets and backtick.
  - **97 ŌĆō 122:** Lowercase letters `'a'` to `'z'`.
  - **123 ŌĆō 127:** Delimiters and DEL.

### 2.3.2 Unicode (1991, Unicode Consortium)

- Code point notation: `U+XXXX` (hexadecimal).
- **Planes:**
  - **Plane 0 (BMP):** U+0000 to U+FFFF ŌĆö covers most modern languages.
  - **Plane 1 (SMP):** U+10000 to U+1FFFF ŌĆö historic scripts, emojis.
  - **Planes 2ŌĆō16:** Specialized (symbols, etc.).
- **Encodings:**
  - **UTF-8:** 1ŌĆō4 bytes, variable width, dominant on the web.
  - **UTF-16:** 2 or 4 bytes.
  - **UTF-32:** Fixed 4 bytes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table uses `\vert` for absolute value notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Range | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ASCII code point range | $0 \leq c \leq 127$ | 7-bit unsigned | Legacy data, file headers |
| 2 | Unicode code point range | $0 \leq c \leq 1{,}114{,}111$ | Hex U+0000 ŌĆō U+10FFFF | Multilingual NLP, emojis |
| 3 | Float sign bit | $s \in \{-1, +1\}$ | 1 bit | Direction of magnitude |
| 4 | Float mantissa (significand) | $M = 1.f$ (normalized) | 52 bits + implicit 1 | Precision of fractional values |
| 5 | Float exponent | $-1022 \leq e \leq 1023$ | 11 bits (biased) | Magnitude scaling |
| 6 | Float numeric value | $(-1)^{s} \cdot 2^{e-1023} \cdot (1 + \sum_{i=1}^{52} b_i 2^{-i})$ | IEEE 754 | Scientific computation |
| 7 | Float machine epsilon | $\varepsilon = 2^{-52} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$ | dimensionless | Numerical error bounds |
| 8 | Largest finite `float` | $\approx 1.7976931348623157 \times 10^{308}$ | dimensionless | Overflow detection |
| 9 | Smallest positive normalized `float` | $\approx 2.2250738585072014 \times 10^{-308}$ | dimensionless | Underflow detection |
| 10 | Complex modulus | $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{a^{2} + b^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Signal magnitude, FFT |
| 11 | Complex argument | $\arg(z) = \arctan(b / a)$ | radians | Phase angle in DSP |
| 12 | Int to `float` precision loss threshold | $\vert n \vert > 2^{53}$ | integer threshold | Large-ID safety in ML |
| 13 | `ord()` / `chr()` mapping | $\text{chr}(n) \iff \text{ord}(c)$ | bidirectional | String Ōåö code point conversion |
| 14 | UTF-8 byte count for code point $c$ | $1$ byte if $c<128$; $2$ if $<2048$; $3$ if $<65536$; $4$ otherwise | bytes | File size estimation |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ML Feature Engineering:** Encoded labels (categorical ŌåÆ integer) often overflow into `int64` (NumPy) or `int32` (GPU) ranges ŌĆö knowing the underlying numeric limits prevents silent overflow.
- **Deep Learning:** Mixed-precision training uses `float16` and `bfloat16` to fit larger models on GPUs. Understanding IEEE 754 is essential to debug NaN issues.
- **NLP (Natural Language Processing):** Tokenizers convert text to Unicode code points or UTF-8 byte pairs (e.g., Byte-Pair Encoding). A working knowledge of character sets is mandatory.
- **Embedded Systems:** Microcontrollers often lack `float` hardware; using `int` arithmetic is a critical optimization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: IEEE 754 Double-Precision Representation of a Float

> **Problem (typical KTU Part A scenario):** *Convert the decimal value `-12.625` to its IEEE 754 double-precision binary representation.*

### Step 1 ŌĆö Separate Sign, Integer, and Fractional Parts

The decimal number is negative, so the **sign bit** $s = 1$.

For the magnitude, split into integer and fractional parts:

$$
12.625 = 12 + 0.625
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Convert the Integer Part to Binary

Repeatedly divide 12 by 2, recording remainders from least to most significant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
12 \div 2 &= 6 \text{ remainder } 0 \\
6  \div 2 &= 3 \text{ remainder } 0 \\
3  \div 2 &= 1 \text{ remainder } 1 \\
1  \div 2 &= 0 \text{ remainder } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders **bottom-up**: $12_{10} = 1100_2$.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Convert the Fractional Part to Binary

Repeatedly multiply 0.625 by 2, recording the integer part each iteration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.625 \times 2 &= 1.25 \quad \rightarrow \text{integer part } 1 \\
0.25  \times 2 &= 0.50  \quad \rightarrow \text{integer part } 0 \\
0.50  \times 2 &= 1.00  \quad \rightarrow \text{integer part } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The process terminates because the fractional part reached exactly 0.

Reading integer parts **top-down**: $0.625_{10} = 0.101_2$.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Combine and Normalize

$$
12.625 = 1100.101_2
$$

Normalize to the form $1.\text{fraction} \times 2^{\text{exponent}}$:

$$
1100.101_2 = 1.100101_2 \times 2^{3}
$$

So the **biased exponent** is $e_{\text{biased}} = 3 + 1023 = 1026$.

Convert $1026$ to an 11-bit binary number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1026 \div 2 &= 513 \text{ rem } 0 \\
513  \div 2 &= 256 \text{ rem } 1 \\
256  \div 2 &= 128 \text{ rem } 0 \\
128  \div 2 &= 64  \text{ rem } 0 \\
64   \div 2 &= 32  \text{ rem } 0 \\
32   \div 2 &= 16  \text{ rem } 0 \\
16   \div 2 &= 8   \text{ rem } 0 \\
8    \div 2 &= 4   \text{ rem } 0 \\
4    \div 2 &= 2   \text{ rem } 0 \\
2    \div 2 &= 1   \text{ rem } 0 \\
1    \div 2 &= 0   \text{ rem } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading bottom-up: $1026_{10} = 10000000010_2$ (11 bits).

### Step 5 ŌĆö Extract the Mantissa

The normalized form is $1.100101_2 \times 2^{3}$. The implicit leading 1 is dropped from the mantissa storage; we store the remaining 52 bits:

$$
\text{mantissa} = 100101\underbrace{000\ldots0}_{46 \text{ zeros}}
$$

### Step 6 ŌĆö Assemble the 64-Bit Word

$$
\underbrace{1}_{s}\ \underbrace{10000000010}_{e}\ \underbrace{10010100\ldots0}_{f}
$$

That is: `1 10000000010 1001010000...0`.

This is exactly the IEEE 754 binary64 encoding of $-12.625$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "biased" exponent?** The bias of $1023$ shifts the exponent range from $[-1022, 1023]$ to $[1, 2046]$ (excluding 0 and 2047, reserved for zero/denormals and infinity/NaN). Biased representation lets the hardware compare exponents using unsigned integer comparison.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Why `0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3`

> **Problem:** *Explain why Python's expression `0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3` evaluates to `False`.*

### Step 1 ŌĆö Express 0.1 in Base 2

The decimal $0.1$ has no finite binary representation:

$$
0.1_{10} = 0.0001100110011001100110011001100110011001100110011001101\ldots_2
$$

The pattern `0011` repeats forever.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Round to 52 Mantissa Bits

Python's `float` keeps the first 52 mantissa bits, then rounds. The stored value is *slightly more* than $0.1$:

$$
0.1_{\text{stored}} \approx 0.1000000000000000055511151231257827021181583404541015625
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Same for 0.2 and 0.3

Both 0.2 and 0.3 also have infinite binary expansions and are rounded. The arithmetic yields:

$$
0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Conclusion

$$
0.30000000000000004 \neq 0.30000000000000000
$$

Therefore `0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3` is `False`. The standard remedy is the `math.isclose()` function or the `decimal` module.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** If asked *how to correctly compare floats*, answer: use `math.isclose(a, b, rel_tol=1e-09, abs_tol=0.0)` or compare `abs(a - b) < epsilon`.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Int Ōåö Float Ōåö Complex Conversions

> **Problem:** *Demonstrate the implicit and explicit conversion rules among the three numeric types.*

The conversion hierarchy is **lossless going down** and **potentially lossy going up**:

$$
\text{int} \longrightarrow \text{float} \longrightarrow \text{complex}
$$

### Step 1 ŌĆö `int` ŌåÆ `float` (may lose precision for large ints)

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 2^{53} + 1 = 9007199254740993 \\
f &= \text{float}(n) \\
f &= 9007199254740992.0 \quad \text{(rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö `float` ŌåÆ `int` (truncates toward zero)

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= 3.7 \\
i &= \text{int}(x) = 3 \quad \text{(NOT 4)} \\
j &= \text{int}(-3.7) = -3 \quad \text{(truncation, not floor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Any numeric ŌåÆ `complex` (lossless)

$$
\text{complex}(5) = (5+0j), \quad \text{complex}(3.14) = (3.14+0j)
$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö `complex` ŌåÆ `int` or `float` (NOT directly allowed)

Python refuses direct narrowing:

$$
\text{int}(3+4j) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{TypeError: can't convert complex to int}
$$

You must extract the real or imaginary part first: `int((3+4j).real) = 3`.

## 3.4 ASCII Ōåö Unicode Code Point Demonstration

> **Problem:** *Show the `ord` and `chr` conversion chain for the character `'A'`.*

### Step 1 ŌĆö Forward mapping

$$
\text{chr}(65) = \text{'A'}
$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö Reverse mapping

$$
\text{ord}('A') = 65
$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Across character sets

The Malayalam letter 'Ó┤ģ' (first vowel in Malayalam script):

$$
\text{ord}('\u0D05') = 3333
$$

This code point $3333$ lies in the **BMP** (Plane 0), and in UTF-8 it is encoded as **3 bytes**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical ŌĆö Float Storage Size

> **Problem:** *Compute the storage size of an IEEE 754 double-precision float in bytes.*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits} &= \underbrace{1}_{\text{sign}} + \underbrace{11}_{\text{exponent}} + \underbrace{52}_{\text{mantissa}} \\
&= 64 \text{ bits} \\
&= 64 \div 8 = 8 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches CPython's `sys.getsizeof(1.0)` output, which reports **24 bytes** total ŌĆö the extra 16 bytes are the PyObject header and reference count, not the value storage itself.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Python Numeric Type Hierarchy (Mermaid Class Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    baseNum["numbers Number abstract base"]
    absComplex["numbers Complex"]
    absReal["numbers Real"]
    absRational["numbers Rational"]
    absIntegral["numbers Integral"]
    pyInt["int arbitrary precision"]
    pyFloat["float IEEE 754 binary64"]
    pyComplex["complex a + bj"]
    decimal["decimal Decimal"]
    fraction["fractions Fraction"]

    baseNum --> absComplex
    absComplex --> absReal
    absReal --> absRational
    absRational --> absIntegral
    absIntegral --> pyInt
    absReal --> pyFloat
    absComplex --> pyComplex
    absReal --> decimal
    absRational --> fraction
```

## 4.2 ASCII vs Unicode Coverage (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ASCII["ASCII 128 code points 0 to 127"]
        ctrl["Control Codes 0 to 31"]
        asciiPrint["Printable 32 to 126"]
        delCode["DEL 127"]
    end

    subgraph Unicode["Unicode 1114112 code points 0 to 1114111"]
        bmp["BMP Plane 0 U+0000 to U+FFFF most languages"]
        smp["SMP Plane 1 emojis historic scripts"]
        otherPlanes["Planes 2 to 16 symbols and specialized"]
    end

    ASCII -- "subset of" --> bmp
    bmp -- "subset of" --> Unicode
```

## 4.3 IEEE 754 Double-Precision Bit Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    bit63["Bit 63 Sign s"]
    bits62_52["Bits 62 to 52 Exponent 11 bits biased by 1023"]
    bits51_0["Bits 51 to 0 Mantissa 52 bits fractional part"]

    bit63 --> bits62_52
    bits62_52 --> bits51_0

    bit63 -. "value is 1 means negative" .-> note1["Sign of mantissa"]
    bits62_52 -. "value 0 to 2047" .-> note2["0 and 2047 are reserved for special values"]
    bits51_0 -. "implicit leading 1 in normalized form" .-> note3["Stored mantissa has 53 bits of precision"]
```

## 4.4 Endianness and Float Bit Pattern (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    step1["Step 1 Take a Python float e.g. 3.14"]
    step2["Step 2 Use struct pack to get raw bytes"]
    step3["Step 3 Interpret bytes as 64 bit unsigned int"]
    step4["Step 4 Unpack to sign exponent mantissa fields"]
    step5["Step 5 Print human readable bit pattern"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5
```

## 4.5 Character Encoding Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    char["Character e.g. A or Ó┤ģ"]
    codePoint["Code Point ord c 65 or 3333"]
    encode["Encoding UTF 8 or UTF 16"]
    bytes["Bytes b'\\x41' or b'\\xe0\\xb4\\x85'"]
    decode["Decoding bytes.decode utf8"]
    char2["Reconstructed Character"]

    char --> codePoint --> encode --> bytes --> decode --> char2
```
