# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023]**

### Q1. List any three built-in numeric data types in Python with one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three built-in numeric data types in Python are:

1. **`int`** ŌĆö integer values with arbitrary precision. Example: `x = 42`.
2. **`float`** ŌĆö IEEE 754 double-precision floating-point numbers. Example: `y = 3.14159`.
3. **`complex`** ŌĆö numbers with a real and imaginary part, written with the suffix `j`. Example: `z = 2 + 5j`.

All three are **immutable** in Python, meaning a new object is created on reassignment rather than modifying the original in place. *(1 mark each for listing and example, plus 0 mark for the immutability note.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024]**

### Q2. Differentiate between ASCII and Unicode character sets.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | ASCII | Unicode |
|---|---|---|
| **Acronym** | American Standard Code for Information Interchange | Universal Coded Character Set |
| **Bit width** | 7 bits (originally), 8 bits (extended) | Variable (up to 21 bits) |
| **Total code points** | **128** (or 256 extended) | **1,114,112** |
| **Range** | $0 \text{ to } 127$ | $U+0000 \text{ to } U+10FFFF$ |
| **Coverage** | English alphabet, digits, basic punctuation | All modern + many historic scripts, symbols, emojis |
| **Encoding** | Single fixed scheme | UTF-8, UTF-16, UTF-32 (variable) |

Unicode is a **superset** of ASCII ŌĆö the first 128 Unicode code points are identical to ASCII. *(1.5 marks for ASCII facts, 1.5 marks for Unicode facts.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE) typically offer an *internal choice* between two questions on the same module. Both choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 1]*

> **(a)** Explain the three numeric data types in Python in detail, with the internal storage and memory considerations of each. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** With a suitable example, demonstrate the difference between `int`, `float`, and `complex` types, including type conversion operations. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to A(a) ŌĆö Numeric Data Types in Detail

1. **`int` (Integer):**
   - Represents whole numbers ŌĆö positive, negative, or zero.
   - In **Python 3**, integers have **arbitrary precision**, limited only by available memory. CPython stores them as a C `long` plus an array of 30-bit "digits" when the value exceeds native register size.
   - Supports multiple literal bases: decimal (`42`), binary (`0b101010`), octal (`0o52`), hex (`0x2A`).
   - **Immutability:** Reassigning creates a new object; the old one is garbage-collected.

2. **`float` (Floating-Point):**
   - Represents real numbers as **IEEE 754 binary64** values: **1 sign bit + 11 exponent bits + 52 mantissa bits = 64 bits total (8 bytes)**.
   - Provides approximately **15ŌĆō17 significant decimal digits** of precision.
   - Special values include `float('inf')`, `float('-inf')`, and `float('nan')`.
   - **Why it matters:** Hardware-accelerated on virtually every modern CPU; the default numeric type in NumPy, TensorFlow, and PyTorch.

3. **`complex`:**
   - Represents numbers of the form $a + bj$ where $j$ is the imaginary unit with $j^{2} = -1$.
   - Internally stored as a **C struct of two doubles** ŌĆö one for the real part, one for the imaginary part.
   - Supports conjugate (`z.conjugate()`), magnitude (`abs(z)`), and phase (`cmath.phase(z)`).
   - **Use cases:** FFT (signal processing), AC circuit analysis, eigenvalues in linear algebra.

#### Model Solution to A(b) ŌĆö Type Conversion Demonstration

```python
# Define one variable of each numeric type
a = 10             # int
b = 3.14           # float
c = 2 + 5j         # complex

# Implicit promotion in mixed arithmetic
print(a + b)       # 13.14   (int promoted to float)
print(a + c)       # (12+5j) (int promoted to complex)

# Explicit conversions
print(float(a))    # 10.0
print(complex(b))  # (3.14+0j)
print(int(b))      # 3  (truncation toward zero)

# Type checking
print(type(a), type(b), type(c))
# Output: <class 'int'> <class 'float'> <class 'complex'>

# Precision warning
large_int = 2**53 + 1
print(large_int == float(large_int))
# Output: False  (float cannot represent this exactly)
```

**Explanation of output (for board-exam writing style):**

- Line 1: `10 + 3.14` promotes `10` (int) to `10.0` (float), yielding `13.14`.
- Line 2: `10 + (2+5j)` promotes `10` to `(10+0j)`, yielding `(12+5j)`.
- Line 3: `float(10)` returns `10.0`.
- Line 4: `complex(3.14)` returns `(3.14+0j)`.
- Line 5: `int(3.14)` truncates toward zero, returning `3`.
- Line 6ŌĆō7: `type()` confirms the class of each variable.
- Line 8ŌĆō9: `2**53 + 1 = 9007199254740993` cannot be exactly represented in a 64-bit `float`; the round-trip `int ŌåÆ float ŌåÆ int` loses the `+1`, producing `9007199254740992`. This illustrates the **$2^{53}$ precision boundary** of IEEE 754 doubles.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Model Paper, Module 1, Alternative Choice]*

> **(a)** Describe the ASCII character set with its organization. Explain Unicode and the need for it. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Write a Python program that performs the following: *(7 marks)*
> 1. Takes a character as input.
> 2. Prints its ASCII/Unicode code point.
> 3. Prints the next 5 characters in sequence.
> 4. Determines whether the input character is a digit, an alphabet (upper/lower), or special.

#### Model Solution to B(a) ŌĆö ASCII and Unicode

The **ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)** character set is a 7-bit encoding that defines **128 characters** (code points **0 to 127**):

- **0ŌĆō31 (Control characters):** Non-printable; e.g., NUL (0), TAB (9), LF (10), CR (13), ESC (27).
- **32ŌĆō47 (Punctuation/Space):** Space (32), `!`, `"`, `#`, ...
- **48ŌĆō57 (Digits):** `'0'` (48) through `'9'` (57). Note that digit code points are **contiguous** and in numeric order.
- **58ŌĆō64:** More punctuation `:` to `@`.
- **65ŌĆō90 (Uppercase AŌĆōZ):** `'A'` = 65, `'B'` = 66, ..., `'Z'` = 90.
- **91ŌĆō96:** Brackets and backtick.
- **97ŌĆō122 (Lowercase aŌĆōz):** `'a'` = 97, ..., `'z'` = 122. Difference from uppercase: $97 - 65 = 32$.
- **123ŌĆō127:** Braces, tilde, DEL (127).

**Need for Unicode:**

ASCII is **English-centric** and lacks code points for non-Latin scripts (Malayalam, Tamil, Mandarin, Arabic, Cyrillic), mathematical symbols, and emojis. Storing multilingual data using ASCII leads to *mojibake* (garbled text). **Unicode** solves this by providing a single, unified **code point** for every character across all major writing systems, with **1,114,112** possible code points (planes 0ŌĆō16). It is the foundation of modern operating systems, the World Wide Web (HTML5 mandates UTF-8), and Python 3 strings (which are **Unicode by default**, unlike Python 2's byte strings).

#### Model Solution to B(b) ŌĆö Character Analysis Program

```python
# Character analysis program
ch = input("Enter a single character: ")

# Validate input length
if len(ch) != 1:
    print("Please enter exactly one character.")
else:
    # 1. Print code point
    code = ord(ch)
    print(f"Unicode code point of '{ch}' = {code} (U+{code:04X})")

    # 2. Next 5 characters in sequence
    print("Next 5 characters:")
    for i in range(1, 6):
        print(f"  {chr(code + i)} -> {code + i}")

    # 3. Classify the character
    if '0' <= ch <= '9':
        category = "Digit"
    elif 'A' <= ch <= 'Z':
        category = "Uppercase Alphabet"
    elif 'a' <= ch <= 'z':
        category = "Lowercase Alphabet"
    else:
        category = "Special Symbol / Other Unicode"

    print(f"Category: {category}")
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter a single character: A
Unicode code point of 'A' = 65 (U+0041)
Next 5 characters:
  B -> 66
  C -> 67
  D -> 68
  E -> 69
  F -> 70
Category: Uppercase Alphabet
```

**Explanation (board-exam style):**

- `ord(ch)` returns the Unicode code point of a single character.
- `chr(n)` returns the character for a given code point.
- The format specifier `U+{code:04X}` prints the code point in **4-digit uppercase hexadecimal**, the standard Unicode notation.
- String comparisons like `'A' <= ch <= 'Z'` work because Python compares strings lexicographically by their code points ŌĆö and ASCII/Unicode places uppercase letters in a contiguous range.
- The `for i in range(1, 6)` loop walks forward through the code point sequence, demonstrating that consecutive code points represent consecutive characters within the same script block.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Don't confuse "character set" with "encoding"** ŌĆö saying "Unicode is 16-bit" loses 2 marks. The correct answer is "Unicode is a 21-bit code space, encoded in UTF-8, UTF-16, or UTF-32."
> 2. **Don't write `i` for the imaginary unit in Python** ŌĆö it must be `j`. Code like `2 + 5i` is a `SyntaxError`.
> 3. **Floating-point exact comparison** ŌĆö avoid `==` for floats. Use `math.isclose()`.
> 4. **Sign of the imaginary part** ŌĆö `complex(-1, -2)` is `(-1-2j)`, not `(-1+2j)`. Mixing the sign costs marks.
> 5. **`int()` truncation vs `math.floor()` rounding** ŌĆö `int(-3.7)` returns `-3` (truncation toward zero), not `-4`. Examiners test this.
> 6. **Implicit promotion direction** ŌĆö saying "float promotes to int" is wrong; the direction is `int ŌåÆ float ŌåÆ complex`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Three numeric types in Python:** `int` (arbitrary precision), `float` (IEEE 754 binary64, **8 bytes**), `complex` (two `float`s, written as `a + bj`).
- [x] **Numeric tower inheritance:** `int` ŌŖé `Rational` ŌŖé `Real` ŌŖé `Complex` ŌŖé `Number`.
- [x] **ASCII = 128 code points** in the range **0 to 127**, 7-bit encoding.
- [x] **Unicode = 1,114,112 code points** in the range **U+0000 to U+10FFFF**, 17 planes.
- [x] **First 128 Unicode code points = ASCII** (full backward compatibility).
- [x] **`ord(c)`** returns the code point; **`chr(n)`** returns the character ŌĆö they are inverses.
- [x] **Implicit promotion direction:** `int ŌåÆ float ŌåÆ complex` (always lossless going right).
- [x] **Explicit narrowing can be lossy:** `int(3.7) = 3`, `int(-3.7) = -3` (truncation, not rounding).
- [x] **IEEE 754 fields:** 1 sign bit + 11 exponent bits + 52 mantissa bits = **64 bits**.
- [x] **Machine epsilon for `float`:** $\varepsilon \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$.
- [x] **Int Ōåö Float precision boundary:** integers above $2^{53}$ lose precision when converted to `float`.
- [x] **Character set vs encoding:** A *set* maps characters to code points; an *encoding* maps code points to bytes. UTF-8 is the dominant encoding.
- [x] **Python uses `j`, not `i`**, for the imaginary unit in complex literals.
- [x] **All numeric types are immutable** in Python ŌĆö reassignment creates a new object.
- [x] **Special float values:** `inf`, `-inf`, `nan`. Test for NaN with `math.isnan()`, never with `==`.
- [x] **Type checking functions:** `type(x)`, `isinstance(x, int)`, `isinstance(x, (int, float))`.
- [x] **Kerala-script support:** Malayalam characters occupy Unicode range **U+0C00 to U+0C7F** (BMP Plane 0), making Python 3 fully Unicode-ready for regional NLP.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonics:**
> - **"S-E-M"** for IEEE 754 layout ŌåÆ **S**ign, **E**xponent, **M**antissa.
> - **"IFC"** for promotion order ŌåÆ **I**nt ŌåÆ **F**loat ŌåÆ **C**omplex.
> - **"A = 65, a = 97, '0' = 48"** ŌĆö memorize these three ASCII anchors; the rest follows.
> - **"Unicode is a *set*; UTF-8 is an *encoding*"** ŌĆö never mix them up in an exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Strings # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the **Python** programming language, a **String** is formally defined as an **immutable, ordered sequence of Unicode characters** that is used to represent textual data. According to the **Python 3.x Language Reference (PEP 3101, PEP 498)**, a string is an instance of the built-in class `str`, which internally stores its data as a contiguous array of code points following the **Unicode Standard (ISO/IEC 10646)**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):** *A string is a sequence of characters enclosed within quotes. Python treats single quotes (' ') and double quotes (" ") identically, while triple quotes (''' ''' or """ """) are used for multi-line strings and docstrings.* Key formal properties of Python strings: | Property | Specification | KTU Relevance | |----------|---------------|---------------| | **Type** | `str` (Built-in class) | Core data type for ML data preprocessing | | **Mutability** | **Immutable** (cannot be changed in-place) | Critical for hashing and dictionary keys | | **Ordering** | Ordered (Left-to-right indexed) | Enables slicing operations | | **Encoding** | Unicode (UTF-8/UTF-16/UTF-32) | Internationalization support | | **Iteration** | Iterable (supports `for` loops) | Basis of NLP tokenization | | **Hashable** | Yes (can be used as dict keys) | Used in feature mapping | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Beaded Necklace" Model Imagine a **string in Python** as a **beaded necklace** where each bead is a single character: - The **entire necklace** is the *string object* (e.g., `"Kerala"`). - Each **individual bead** is a *character* (e.g., `'K'`, `'e'`, `'r'`, `'a'`, `'l'`, `'a'`). - The beads are **strung in a fixed order** (ordered sequence) ŌĆö you cannot randomly insert a bead in the middle of an existing necklace without **re-stringing the whole thing** (this is **immutability**). - You can refer to any bead by its **position number** (indexing), and you can select a **group of consecutive beads** to form a smaller necklace (slicing). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In **Machine Learning** contexts, strings are the foundational data structure for handling: > - Text datasets (CSV columns, JSON values) > - Labels and class names (`"spam"`, `"ham"`) > - File paths and URLs > - Tokenized sentences in **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** ## 1.3 String Creation ŌĆö The Four Canonical Syntaxes Python provides **four** syntactically distinct ways to create string literals, each serving a specific purpose in the KTU 2024 syllabus: ### Syntax 1: Single Quotes ```python s1 = 'Machine Learning' ``` ### Syntax 2: Double Quotes ```python s2 = "Python Basics" ``` ### Syntax 3: Triple Single Quotes (Multi-line) ```python s3 = '''This is a multi-line string spanning three lines.''' ``` ### Syntax 4: Triple Double Quotes (Docstrings) ```python s4 = """Function-level docstring used to document modules, classes, and functions.""" ``` > [!TIP] > **Why multiple quote types?** They allow the string itself to contain quote characters without escape sequences. For example, `"It's raining"` is valid, but `'It's raining'` would cause a `SyntaxError`. ## 1.4 Empty String and Single-Character Strings | Construct | Output | Description | |-----------|--------|-------------| | `""` | Empty string | Zero-length string, `len("") == 0` | | `"a"` | Single char string | A string of length 1, NOT a `char` type (Python has no `char`) | | `str(123)` | `"123"` | Type conversion from `int` to `str` | | `str(3.14)` | `"3.14"` | Type conversion from `float` to `str` | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** Python does **not** have a separate `char` data type. Even a single character `'A'` is still a string of length 1. Examiners frequently test this distinction. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 String Indexing ŌĆö The Mathematical Foundation String indexing in Python follows **zero-based indexing** for forward access and **negative-based indexing** for reverse access. The indexing formula is governed by: $$I_{valid} = \{-n, -n+1, \ldots, -1, 0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$$ Where $n$ is the length of the string. Formally, for a string $S$ of length $n = \text{len}(S)$: | Index Range | Direction | First Element | Last Element | |-------------|-----------|---------------|--------------| | $0 \leq i \leq n-1$ | Forward (Left $\to$ Right) | $S[0]$ | $S[n-1]$ | | $-n \leq i \leq -1$ | Backward (Right $\to$ Left) | $S[-n]$ | $S[-1]$ | The relationship between positive and negative indices is given by: $$S[-k] \equiv S[n - k], \quad \text{where } 1 \leq k \leq n$$ ### Worked Example: `S = "PYTHON"` | Index $i$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |-----------|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Character | `P` | `Y` | `T` | `H` | `O` | `N` | | Index $i$ | -6 | -5 | -4 | -3 | -2 | -1 | |-----------|----|----|----|----|----|----| | Character | `P` | `Y` | `T` | `H` | `O` | `N` | Here $n = 6$, so $S[-1] = S[6-1] = S[5] = \text{'N'}$ (verified). ## 2.2 String Slicing ŌĆö The `[start:stop:step]` Paradigm Slicing extracts a **substring** using the syntax `S[start:stop:step]`. The result is a new string formed by characters at indices $i$ satisfying: $$i \in \{start, start+step, start+2\cdot step, \ldots\}$$ subject to the **half-open interval constraint**: $$start \leq i < stop$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `stop` index is EXCLUSIVE** ŌĆö the character at position `stop` is **not** included. This is the single most common source of off-by-one errors in KTU exams. ### Boundary Condition Rules for Slicing | Omitted Parameter | Default Value | Rationale | |-------------------|---------------|-----------| | `start` omitted | $0$ (string beginning) | Forward traversal starts at index 0 | | `stop` omitted | $n$ (string length) | Traversal continues to the end | | `step` omitted | $1$ (sequential access) | Natural left-to-right reading order | | Negative `step` | N/A | Reverses traversal direction | ### The Clamping Theorem When an index falls **outside** the valid range, Python silently **clamps** it to the nearest valid boundary rather than raising an `IndexError` (this behavior applies **only to slicing**, not to direct indexing): $$\text{clamp}(i) = \max(0, \min(i, n-1))$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Operation | Syntax | Result Type | Example | Output | |-----------|--------|-------------|---------|--------| | Length | `len(S)` | `int` | `len("Hello")` | `5` | | Concatenation | `S1 + S2` | `str` | `"Py" + "thon"` | `"Python"` | | Repetition | `S * k` | `str` | `"ab" * 3` | `"ababab"` | | Membership (in) | `c in S` | `bool` | `'P' in "Python"` | `True` | | Membership (not in) | `c not in S` | `bool` | `'z' not in "Python"` | `True` | | Forward Indexing | `S[i]` | `str` (len 1) | `"Python"[2]` | `'t'` | | Reverse Indexing | `S[-i]` | `str` (len 1) | `"Python"[-1]` | `'n'` | | Basic Slice | `S[a:b]` | `str` | `"Python"[1:4]` | `"yth"` | | Full Slice | `S[:]` | `str` (copy) | `"Python"[:]` | `"Python"` | | Step Slice | `S[a:b:c]` | `str` | `"Python"[::2]` | `"Pto"` | | Reverse Slice | `S[::-1]` | `str` (reversed) | `"Python"[::-1]` | `"nohtyP"` | | Type Conversion | `str(x)` | `str` | `str(2024)` | `"2024"` | | Character $\to$ Ordinal | `ord(c)` | `int` | `ord('A')` | `65` | | Ordinal $\to$ Character | `chr(n)` | `str` | `chr(65)` | `'A'` | ## 2.4 String Immutability ŌĆö The "Cannot Mutate" Theorem > [!NOTE] > **Theorem (String Immutability):** For any string object $S$ and any valid index $i$, the assignment operation $S[i] = c$ is **illegal** and raises a `TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignment`. This means that any operation that appears to "modify" a string actually **creates a new string object** and rebinds the variable name. The original object is left untouched and is eventually garbage-collected. ### Immutability Consequence: String Methods Return New Objects All string transformation methods (`.upper()`, `.replace()`, `.strip()`, etc.) **do not modify** the original string; they **return a new string**: $$S.\text{method}() \rightarrow S_{new}, \quad S = S_{old} \text{ (unchanged)}$$ To capture the result, explicit reassignment is required: $$S \leftarrow S.\text{method}()$$ ## 2.5 Escape Characters and Raw Strings Python uses the **backslash `\`** as an escape character. KTU 2024 frequently tests the following escape sequences: | Escape Sequence | Meaning | ASCII/Unicode Reference | |-----------------|---------|-------------------------| | `\n` | Newline (Line Feed) | U+000A | | `\t` | Horizontal Tab | U+0009 | | `\\` | Literal Backslash | U+005C | | `\'` | Single Quote | U+0027 | | `\"` | Double Quote | U+0022 | | `\r` | Carriage Return | U+000D | | `\0` | Null Character | U+0000 | | `\x41` | Hexadecimal Unicode | U+0041 (`'A'`) | | `\u0041` | 4-digit Unicode | U+0041 (`'A'`) | A **raw string** prefixed with `r` or `R` disables escape processing, treating backslashes as literal characters ŌĆö essential for **regex patterns** and **Windows file paths**: ```python path = r"C:\Users\Admin\new_folder" # Backslashes preserved literally ``` ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Machine Learning In **ML pipelines**, strings are omnipresent: 1. **Data Ingestion:** Reading CSV headers (`pd.read_csv`) produces `Index` objects of strings. 2. **Label Encoding:** Class labels like `"cat"`, `"dog"` are stored as strings before numeric encoding. 3. **NLP Tokenization:** Raw text is split into lists of words (substrings). 4. **Regular Expressions:** `re` module operates exclusively on strings. 5. **File I/O:** `open(path, 'r').read()` returns a string. 6. **Hyperparameter Logging:** MLflow/W&B logs hyperparameters as key-value string pairs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Reverse a String Using Slicing **Problem Statement:** Given a string $S$ of length $n$, derive the slicing expression that produces the reversed string $S_{rev}$. ### Mathematical Derivation We want to find indices $i_0, i_1, \ldots, i_{n-1}$ such that: $$S_{rev}[k] = S[n - 1 - k], \quad \text{for } 0 \leq k \leq n-1$$ The slicing syntax `S[start:stop:step]` visits indices in the arithmetic progression: $$i_j = start + j \cdot step, \quad j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$ **Step 1:** To traverse in **reverse direction**, we need a **negative step**: $$step = -1$$ **Step 2:** To **include the first character** $S[0]$, we start at index $0$: $$start = 0$$ **Step 3:** To traverse until we cross the **last character** $S[-1] = S[n-1]$, we let the slice continue "past the end" (Python handles out-of-bounds gracefully in slicing): $$stop = \text{None} \quad (\text{which defaults to "go all the way"})$$ **Step 4:** Combining the three parameters: $$S_{rev} = S[0:\text{None}:-1] = S[::-1]$$ ### Verification with $S = \text{"Kerala"}$ ($n = 6$) | $j$ | $i_j = 0 + j \cdot (-1) = -j$ | $S[i_j]$ | |-----|------------------------------|----------| | 0 | 0 | `'K'` | | 1 | -1 | `'a'` | | 2 | -2 | `'l'` | | 3 | -3 | `'a'` | | 4 | -4 | `'r'` | | 5 | -5 | `'e'` | | 6 | -6 | (stop: out of range, loop terminates) | Concatenated result: `"alaerK"` Ō£ō ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation ŌĆö Slice to Extract Every N-th Character **Problem Statement:** For a string $S$ of length $n$, extract characters at positions $0, k, 2k, 3k, \ldots$ ### Mathematical Derivation We need indices satisfying: $$i_j = j \cdot k, \quad 0 \leq j \cdot k < n$$ This is precisely the arithmetic progression generated by slice syntax with `step = k`. The largest valid $j$ satisfies $j \cdot k \leq n - 1$, so: $$j_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{n-1}{k} \right\rfloor$$ The **stop** index must be set to $n$ (or `None`) to ensure the last element is included: $$S_{\text{step}} = S[0:n:k] = S[::k]$$ ### Verification with $S = \text{"Programming"}$ ($n = 11$), $k = 3$ The indices visited are $0, 3, 6, 9$. The last valid index is $9 < 11$ Ō£ō. | Index | Character | |-------|-----------| | 0 | `'P'` | | 3 | `'g'` | | 6 | `'m'` | | 9 | `'i'` | Result: `"Pgmi"` ## 3.3 Comprehensive Method Reference Matrix The following table enumerates **all essential string methods** testable in KTU 2024 examinations. Always remember that **none of these methods modify the original string** ŌĆö they return new string objects. ## 3.4 String Formatting ŌĆö Three KTU-Approved Techniques ### Technique 1: f-Strings (PEP 498, Python 3.6+) ŌĆö **Preferred Modern Method** ```python name = "ML" year = 2024 msg = f"Welcome to {name} Basics, {year}!" # Output: "Welcome to ML Basics, 2024!" ``` ### Technique 2: `.format()` Method (PEP 3101) ŌĆö **Positional/Keyword Arguments** ```python msg = "Welcome to {} Basics, {}!".format("ML", 2024) msg2 = "{course} in {yr}".format(course="Python", yr=2024) ``` ### Technique 3: `%` Operator (C-style) ŌĆö **Legacy Method** ```python msg = "Welcome to %s Basics, %d!" % ("ML", 2024) ``` > [!TIP] > **Format Specifiers (commonly tested):** > > | Specifier | Meaning | Example | > |-----------|---------|---------| > | `%s` | String | `"%s" % "hi"` ŌåÆ `"hi"` | > | `%d` | Integer (decimal) | `"%d" % 42` ŌåÆ `"42"` | > | `%f` | Float | `"%.2f" % 3.14159` ŌåÆ `"3.14"` | > | `%x` | Hexadecimal | `"%x" % 255` ŌåÆ `"ff"` | > | `%e` | Scientific notation | `"%.2e" % 1234.5` ŌåÆ `"1.23e+03"` | ## 3.5 Comparative Analysis ŌĆö `find()` vs `index()` These two methods are **functionally identical** for successful searches but differ in **failure behavior** ŌĆö a frequent KTU question: $$\text{find}(sub) = \begin{cases} \text{lowest index where } sub \text{ occurs} & \text{if found} \\ -1 & \text{if not found} \end{cases}$$ $$\text{index}(sub) = \begin{cases} \text{lowest index where } sub \text{ occurs} & \text{if found} \\ \text{raise ValueError} & \text{if not found} \end{cases}$$ **Engineering recommendation:** Use `find()` when testing for existence (avoids exception overhead); use `index()` when the substring **must** exist and a missing case is a programming error. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 String Indexing Memory Map The following diagram visualizes the dual-indexing system of a Python string, showing how positive and negative indices map to the same character positions. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Positive_Index_Forward ["Positive Index (Left to Right)"] A0["S[0]
