hello, world, NLP"] Raw --> Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4 --> Step5 --> Out ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Explain string immutability in Python with a suitable example.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** String immutability means that once a string object is created in memory, its contents **cannot be modified** in place. Any operation that appears to change a string (such as `replace()`, `upper()`, or concatenation) actually **creates a new string object** and rebinds the variable name. For example: ```python s = "hello" s.upper() # Returns "HELLO" but s is still "hello" print(s) # Output: hello s = s.upper() # Explicit reassignment required print(s) # Output: HELLO ``` Attempting direct modification, e.g., `s[0] = 'H'`, raises `TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignment`. Immutability makes strings **hashable**, enabling their use as dictionary keys and set elements. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Differentiate between `find()` and `index()` methods of strings.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | `find()` | `index()` | |--------|----------|-----------| | Behavior on substring **not found** | Returns `-1` | Raises `ValueError` exception | | Use case | Existence testing | Mandatory presence assertion | | Exception overhead | None | Yes (try/except required for safe use) | Example: `"Python".find("z")` returns `-1`, whereas `"Python".index("z")` raises `ValueError: substring not found`. Both methods return the **lowest index** of the first occurrence when the substring is found. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a)** Explain the different ways of creating strings in Python. Write a program to demonstrate string slicing operations including positive, negative indexing, and the `[start:stop:step]` syntax. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain any **eight** built-in string methods in Python with examples. Write a program that takes a sentence as input and counts the number of vowels, consonants, digits, and spaces in it. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) **Different ways of creating strings (3 marks):** 1. **Single quotes:** `s1 = 'Hello'` 2. **Double quotes:** `s2 = "World"` 3. **Triple single quotes:** `s3 = '''Multi-line string'''` 4. **Triple double quotes:** `s4 = """Docstring"""` **Demonstration program (4 marks):** ```python # Program demonstrating string slicing s = "PYTHONPROGRAMMING" n = len(s) print(f"String: {s}") print(f"Length: {n}") print(f"s[0] = {s[0]} (first character)") print(f"s[-1] = {s[-1]} (last character)") print(f"s[2:8] = {s[2:8]} (slice 2 to 8 exclusive)") print(f"s[:6] = {s[:6]} (start omitted)") print(f"s[6:] = {s[6:]}(stop omitted)") print(f"s[::2] = {s[::2]} (every 2nd char)") print(f"s[::-1]= {s[::-1]}(reversed string)") ``` **Output:** ``` String: PYTHONPROGRAMMING Length: 17 s[0] = P (first character) s[-1] = g (last character) s[2:8] = THONPR (slice 2 to 8 exclusive) s[:6] = PYTHON (start omitted) s[6:] = PROGRAMMING (stop omitted) s[::2] = PTOPOAMIG (every 2nd char) s[::-1]= GNIMMARGORPNOHTYP (reversed string) ``` --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) **Eight built-in string methods (4 marks):** 1. `upper()` ŌĆö Converts to uppercase 2. `lower()` ŌĆö Converts to lowercase 3. `strip()` ŌĆö Removes leading/trailing whitespace 4. `replace(old, new)` ŌĆö Replaces substring 5. `find(sub)` ŌĆö Returns index of first occurrence 6. `split(sep)` ŌĆö Splits string into list 7. `join(iterable)` ŌĆö Joins iterable into string 8. `count(sub)` ŌĆö Counts occurrences **Program ŌĆö Vowel/Consonant/Digit/Space counter (3 marks):** ```python sentence = input("Enter a sentence: ") vowels = consonants = digits = spaces = 0 vowel_set = "aeiouAEIOU" for ch in sentence: if ch in vowel_set: vowels += 1 elif ch.isalpha(): consonants += 1 elif ch.isdigit(): digits += 1 elif ch.isspace(): spaces += 1 print(f"Vowels : {vowels}") print(f"Consonants : {consonants}") print(f"Digits : {digits}") print(f"Spaces : {spaces}") ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Enter a sentence: Python 3 was released in 2008 Vowels : 7 Consonants : 16 Digits : 5 Spaces : 6 ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** What are escape characters in Python? List any **six** common escape sequences with their meanings. Explain raw strings with an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain string formatting in Python using f-strings, `.format()`, and the `%` operator. Write a program to check whether a given string is a **palindrome** (case-insensitive, ignoring non-alphanumeric characters). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) **Escape characters definition (2 marks):** Escape characters are special character sequences in Python that begin with a backslash (`\`). They represent characters that cannot be typed directly (like newline, tab) or that would otherwise have syntactic meaning (like quotes inside a string). **Six common escape sequences (3 marks):** | Escape Sequence | Meaning | |-----------------|---------| | `\n` | Newline (moves cursor to next line) | | `\t` | Horizontal tab | | `\\` | Literal backslash | | `\'` | Single quote | | `\"` | Double quote | | `\r` | Carriage return | **Raw strings (2 marks):** A **raw string** is created by prefixing the string literal with `r` or `R`. In a raw string, escape sequences are **not processed**; backslashes are treated as literal characters. This is essential for regular expressions and Windows file paths: ```python normal = "C:\new\test" # \n and \t get interpreted raw = r"C:\new\test" # Backslashes preserved print(normal) # Output: C: ew est (corrupted!) print(raw) # Output: C:\new\test (correct) ``` --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) **String formatting techniques (3 marks):** ```python name, score = "Alice", 95.6789 # f-string (preferred in Python 3.6+) print(f"Student: {name}, Score: {score:.2f}") # .format() method print("Student: {}, Score: {:.2f}".format(name, score)) print("Student: {n}, Score: {s:.2f}".format(n=name, s=score)) # % operator (legacy C-style) print("Student: %s, Score: %.2f" % (name, score)) ``` **Output:** ``` Student: Alice, Score: 95.68 Student: Alice, Score: 95.68 Student: Alice, Score: 95.68 Student: Alice, Score: 95.68 ``` **Palindrome check program (4 marks):** ```python def is_palindrome(text): # Step 1: Normalize to lowercase text = text.lower() # Step 2: Filter only alphanumeric characters cleaned = "" for ch in text: if ch.isalnum(): cleaned += ch # Step 3: Compare with reverse return cleaned == cleaned[::-1] # Test cases test_strings = [ "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama", "racecar", "Hello, World!", "Madam, I'm Adam" ] for s in test_strings: result = "PALINDROME" if is_palindrome(s) else "NOT a palindrome" print(f"'{s}' => {result}") ``` **Output:** ``` 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama' => PALINDROME 'racecar' => PALINDROME 'Hello, World!' => NOT a palindrome 'Madam, I'm Adam' => PALINDROME ``` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Zones ŌĆö Avoid These Mistakes:** > > 1. **Off-by-one in slicing (ŌĆō2 marks):** Students frequently write `S[1:4]` expecting to include index 4. Remember: `stop` is **exclusive**. `S[1:4]` returns indices 1, 2, 3 only. > > 2. **Forgetting immutability in code (ŌĆō3 marks):** Writing `S.upper()` without reassigning to a variable. The method returns a new string but the original is unchanged. Always use `S = S.upper()`. > > 3. **Confusing `find()` returning -1 with exception (ŌĆō2 marks):** Wrap `find()` results with `if result != -1` checks, not `try/except`. > > 4. **Mixing `str` and `int` in concatenation (ŌĆō2 marks):** `"Age: " + 25` raises `TypeError`. Use `str(25)`, f-strings, or `.format()`. > > 5. **IndexError vs slicing (ŌĆō2 marks):** `S[100]` on a 6-character string raises `IndexError`, but `S[0:100]` silently returns the entire string. Know the difference. > > 6. **Forgetting `str()` conversion in `print()` (ŌĆō1 mark):** `print("Value: " + value)` fails if `value` is numeric. Use comma-separated `print("Value:", value)` or f-strings. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Strings (Module 1):** > > - A **string** is an **immutable, ordered sequence of Unicode characters** of type `str`. > - Strings can be created using **single (`'`)**, **double (`"`)**, **triple single (`'''`)**, or **triple double (`"""`)** quotes. > - **Indexing is zero-based** for positive indices and **negative-from-end** for negative indices: $S[-k] = S[n-k]$. > - **Slicing syntax** is `S[start:stop:step]` where `stop` is **exclusive** and all three parameters are **optional**. > - The idiom `S[::-1]` is the canonical way to **reverse a string**. > - **String concatenation** uses `+`; **repetition** uses `*`; **membership testing** uses `in` / `not in`. > - Strings are **immutable**: `S[0] = 'x'` raises `TypeError`; all transformation methods return **new** objects. > - **Length** is obtained via `len(S)`, returning an `int`. > - **Escape sequences** (`\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\'`, `\"`) are prefixed with backslash; **raw strings** (`r"..."`) disable escape processing. > - **Common methods** (high-yield for KTU): `upper`, `lower`, `strip`, `find`, `index`, `count`, `replace`, `split`, `join`, `startswith`, `endswith`, `isdigit`, `isalpha`, `isalnum`. > - **Formatting**: Prefer **f-strings** (PEP 498) for readability; `.format()` and `%` operator are also accepted. > - **`ord(c)`** converts a character to its Unicode code point (int); **`chr(n)`** is the inverse. > - Python has **no `char` type** ŌĆö even a single character is a string of length 1. > - Strings are **hashable** and can be used as **dictionary keys** and **set elements** (a consequence of immutability). > - In **ML contexts**, strings dominate text preprocessing: lowercasing, stripping, splitting, joining, and regex-based pattern matching.

topics.title: Keywords
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Keywords

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Python Keywords (Reserved Words):** Reserved words are predefined, meaningful words in Python that **cannot be used as identifiers** (variable names, function names, or class names). They form the **syntax and structural backbone** of the Python language and are part of the language grammar itself. As of **Python 3.x**, the official keyword list contains exactly **35 reserved words**.

These words are **case-sensitive** and must be used exactly as defined. Any attempt to repurpose them as variable or function names results in a **`SyntaxError`**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 ŌĆō PEERT526, Module 1):** Students must be able to *identify, classify, and correctly use* all Python keywords. Memorization of the complete keyword list with category-wise grouping is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU End Semester Examinations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Python keywords** as the **traffic signs of a programming language**:

- Just as a **"STOP"** sign on a road has a **fixed meaning** that drivers must obey, Python keywords have a **pre-assigned semantic meaning** that the interpreter enforces.
- You **cannot repaint** a STOP sign to mean "GO" ŌĆö similarly, you **cannot redefine** `if` to mean something else.
- The Python interpreter **recognizes these words first** before processing any code, making them the **rules of the road** for writing valid programs.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Keywords are like **grammar rules** in English. Words like *is, am, are, the, if, while* have fixed grammatical roles ŌĆö you cannot use *"the"* as a person's name. Python keywords behave identically.

---

## 1.3 Complete List of Python 3 Keywords

Python 3.x officially defines **35 keywords**. They are listed below (from the `keyword` module):

```python
import keyword
print(keyword.kwlist)
```

**Output:**
```python
['False', 'None', 'True', 'and', 'as', 'assert', 'async',
 'await', 'break', 'class', 'continue', 'def', 'del', 'elif',
 'else', 'except', 'finally', 'for', 'from', 'global', 'if',
 'import', 'in', 'is', 'lambda', 'nonlocal', 'not', 'or', 'pass',
 'raise', 'return', 'try', 'while', 'with', 'yield']
```

> [!WARNING]
> The keyword list **grew** in Python 3 (added `async`, `await`, `True`, `False`, `None`, `nonlocal`, `yield`) compared to Python 2. Always quote the correct Python version in exams.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Total Keywords in **Python 3.12** | **35** |
| Case Sensitivity | **Yes** (e.g., `True` ŌēĀ `true`) |
| Source of Truth | The **`keyword` module** in the standard library |
| Penalty for Misuse | Runtime/Compile-time **`SyntaxError`** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Python keywords are **tokenized** by the **lexical analyzer** (part of the CPython interpreter's parser pipeline) **before** any other identifier is processed. This makes them **non-overridable**.

### The 4-Stage Tokenization Pipeline

1. **Lexical Analysis** ŌåÆ Source code is scanned character-by-character.
2. **Token Classification** ŌåÆ Each token is matched against the **keyword table** (a hash set internally).
3. **Match Found?** ŌåÆ If matched, the token is assigned its **reserved semantic role**.
4. **Else** ŌåÆ Token is treated as an **identifier, literal, or operator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Because keywords are matched in the lexer stage, the interpreter **never** looks them up in the global namespace. This is why you cannot "shadow" a keyword like you can a built-in function (`print`).

---

## 2.2 Category-Wise Classification of Keywords

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most exam-relevant table** in this module. KTU frequently asks students to "list and explain the categories of Python keywords" for **14-mark questions**.

| # | Category | Keywords | Purpose |
|:-:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Value / Constant** | `True`, `False`, `None` | Represent Boolean truth values and null |
| 2 | **Logical Operators** | `and`, `or`, `not` | Boolean logic composition |
| 3 | **Identity / Membership** | `is`, `in`, `not in` | Compare object identity / membership test |
| 4 | **Conditional** | `if`, `elif`, `else` | Decision-making branching |
| 5 | **Looping** | `for`, `while`, `break`, `continue`, `pass` | Iteration control |
| 6 | **Function Definition** | `def`, `return`, `lambda`, `yield` | Function creation / generators |
| 7 | **Class / OOP** | `class` | Blueprint for objects |
| 8 | **Exception Handling** | `try`, `except`, `finally`, `raise`, `assert` | Error management |
| 9 | **Import / Module** | `import`, `from`, `as` | Bring external code into scope |
| 10 | **Scope Management** | `global`, `nonlocal` | Variable scope declaration |
| 11 | **Resource Management** | `with` | Context managers (e.g., file handling) |
| 12 | **Deletion** | `del` | Remove object references |
| 13 | **Asynchronous Programming** | `async`, `await` | Coroutine-based concurrency |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Rule | Statement | Exception / Note |
|---|---|---|
| **Count Rule** | Total Python 3 keywords = **35** | Python 2 had **31** keywords |
| **Case Rule** | All keywords are **lowercase** EXCEPT `True`, `False`, `None` | `true`, `false`, `none` ŌåÆ invalid |
| **Shadow Rule** | Cannot be used as **variable names** | Causes `SyntaxError` |
| **Modification Rule** | Cannot be **redefined or overloaded** | Unlike C++ `inline` |
| **Scope Rule** | Recognized **globally** in all scopes | Local redefinition not allowed |
| **Verification** | Check via `keyword.iskeyword('word')` | Returns `True`/`False` |
| **Listing** | Retrieve via `keyword.kwlist` | Returns Python `list` |
| **Soft Keywords** | `match`, `case`, `_`, `type` (Python 3.10+) | Contextual, not reserved globally |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Machine Learning workflows** (the focus of PEERT526), keywords enable:

- **Conditional branching** (`if`, `else`) ŌåÆ Train/validation data splits.
- **Looping** (`for`, `while`) ŌåÆ Iterating over training epochs.
- **Exception handling** (`try`, `except`) ŌåÆ Robust data pipelines that don't crash on missing files.
- **Context managers** (`with`) ŌåÆ Safe file I/O for CSV/dataset loading.
- **Lambda** (`lambda`) ŌåÆ Defining quick activation/transform functions.
- **Import** (`import`, `as`) ŌåÆ Loading `numpy as np`, `pandas as pd`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Concept Derivations

### Derivation 1: How the Interpreter Detects a Keyword Violation

**Step 1 ŌĆö Source Code Input**

```python
if = 10
```

**Step 2 ŌĆö Lexical Scan**
The tokenizer scans `if` and queries the keyword hash table.

$$T_{keyword} = \{ \text{'if', 'else', 'for', ...} \}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Match Found**
The lexer identifies `if` as belonging to $T_{keyword}$ before any assignment logic is processed.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Syntax Error Generation**
Because `if` is bound to a control-flow construct, an `=` assignment following it is grammatically illegal.

$$
\therefore \text{Parser raises } \texttt{SyntaxError: invalid syntax}
$$

**Verification Code:**

```python
>>> if = 10
  File "<stdin>", line 1
    if = 10
       ^
SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

---

### Derivation 2: Programmatic Keyword Verification

**Goal:** Build a complete classification report of all Python keywords using only standard library tools.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Import the keyword module**

```python
import keyword
```

This exposes the canonical list `keyword.kwlist`.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Total Count Check**

```python
total = len(keyword.kwlist)
print(f"Total Python 3 keywords: {total}")
```

**Output:**
```
Total Python 3 keywords: 35
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Categorization via Membership Tests**

```python
value_kw      = {'True', 'False', 'None'}
logical_kw    = {'and', 'or', 'not'}
conditional_kw= {'if', 'elif', 'else'}
loop_kw       = {'for', 'while', 'break', 'continue', 'pass'}
function_kw   = {'def', 'return', 'lambda', 'yield'}
exception_kw  = {'try', 'except', 'finally', 'raise', 'assert'}
import_kw     = {'import', 'from', 'as'}
scope_kw      = {'global', 'nonlocal'}
async_kw      = {'async', 'await'}
identity_kw   = {'is', 'in', 'del'}
class_kw      = {'class'}
resource_kw   = {'with'}
```

**Step 4 ŌĆö Verification Function**

```python
def classify(word):
    """Returns the category of a Python keyword."""
    if word in value_kw:       return 'Value/Constant'
    if word in logical_kw:     return 'Logical Operator'
    if word in conditional_kw: return 'Conditional'
    if word in loop_kw:        return 'Looping'
    if word in function_kw:    return 'Function'
    if word in exception_kw:   return 'Exception Handling'
    if word in import_kw:      return 'Import/Module'
    if word in scope_kw:       return 'Scope Management'
    if word in async_kw:       return 'Asynchronous'
    if word in identity_kw:    return 'Identity/Membership'
    if word in class_kw:       return 'Class/OOP'
    if word in resource_kw:    return 'Resource Management'
    return 'Unknown'

print(classify('yield'))    # Output: Function
print(classify('async'))    # Output: Asynchronous
```

---

### Derivation 3: Difference Between Keyword and Built-in Identifier

**Step 1 ŌĆö Conceptual Distinction**

$$\text{Keyword} \subset \text{Reserved Tokens}$$
$$\text{Built-in Identifier} = \text{Name pre-bound in } \_\_builtins\_\_ \text{ namespace}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Empirical Proof**

```python
# Built-in can be shadowed (allowed but discouraged)
print = "I overrode print!"
print(print)   # Output: I overrode print!

# Keyword cannot be shadowed
class = "ML"   # SyntaxError
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Analytical Conclusion**

$$ \text{Shadowable}(x) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } x \in \text{builtins} \\ \text{False} & \text{if } x \in \text{keywords} \end{cases} $$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Use-Cases

| Keyword | Syntax Form | Practical ML Example |
|---|---|---|
| `if/else` | `if cond: ... else: ...` | `if accuracy > 0.9: deploy_model()` |
| `for` | `for x in iterable:` | `for epoch in range(100):` |
| `with` | `with open(f) as file:` | `with open('data.csv') as f:` |
| `lambda` | `lambda args: expr` | `sigmoid = lambda x: 1/(1+exp(-x))` |
| `try/except` | `try: ... except:` | Wrapping CSV loading |
| `import/as` | `import numpy as np` | Standard ML aliasing |
| `yield` | `def gen(): yield x` | Data generator for batch training |
| `in` | `if x in collection:` | `if 'loss' in history.keys():` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Python Interpreter Keyword Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: .py file] --> B[Lexer: Tokenization Stage]
    B --> C{Token matches kwlist?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Assign Reserved Semantic Role]
    C -- No  --> E[Identifier / Literal / Operator Lookup]
    D --> F[Parser Builds AST]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compile to Bytecode]
    G --> H[Python Virtual Machine Executes]
```

**Interpretation:** Any token that matches `keyword.kwlist` is **diverted immediately** to the reserved-semantic path; otherwise, it proceeds through normal identifier resolution.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Keyword Category Map (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    KW[Python 3 Keywords: 35 total]
    KW --> VAL[Value: True, False, None]
    KW --> LOG[Logical: and, or, not]
    KW --> CON[Conditional: if, elif, else]
    KW --> LOP[Looping: for, while, break, continue, pass]
    KW --> FUN[Function: def, return, lambda, yield]
    KW --> CLS[Class: class]
    KW --> EXC[Exception: try, except, finally, raise, assert]
    KW --> IMP[Import: import, from, as]
    KW --> SCP[Scope: global, nonlocal]
    KW --> ASN[Async: async, await]
    KW --> IDN[Identity: is, in]
    KW --> RES[Resource: with]
    KW --> DEL[Deletion: del]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Keyword Validation Workflow (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([User types a word as variable]) --> Q1{Is word in keyword.kwlist?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Err[SyntaxError raised by parser]
    Q1 -- No  --> Q2{Is word a builtin?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Warn[Warning: shadows builtin]
    Q2 -- No  --> OK[Variable created successfully]
    Err --> End([Program halted])
    Warn --> End
    OK --> End
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model Paper]**

### **Q1. Define Python keywords. List any five examples.**
*(Model Answer ŌĆō 3 Marks)*

Python keywords are **reserved words** that have predefined meanings in the Python language and **cannot be used as identifiers**. They are part of the language's syntax. As of Python 3.x, there are **35** keywords.

**Examples:** `if`, `else`, `for`, `while`, `def`, `class`, `return`, `True`, `None`, `import`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Count (1 mark) + Examples (1 mark).

---

### **Q2. How can you verify if a word is a Python keyword programmatically?**
*(Model Answer ŌĆō 3 Marks)*

Python provides the built-in **`keyword`** module for this purpose.

```python
import keyword
print(keyword.iskeyword('for'))   # Output: True
print(keyword.iskeyword('hello')) # Output: False
print(keyword.kwlist)             # Output: list of all 35 keywords
```

The function `keyword.iskeyword()` returns `True` if the input string is a reserved keyword, otherwise `False`.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆō Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE ŌĆō Dec 2024 Style ŌĆö Internal Choice Between A and B]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Python keywords. List and categorize **all 35 keywords** of Python 3 with appropriate examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between **keywords and built-in identifiers** in Python with suitable code examples. Why can't keywords be used as variable names? *(7 Marks)*

---

#### ­¤ōØ Model Solution ŌĆö Question A

**(a) Concept and Categorization (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 Marks):** Keywords are reserved words whose meanings are **defined by the Python language itself**. They cannot be used as variable, function, or class names. The complete list is accessible via the `keyword.kwlist` attribute.

**Categorization (5 Marks):**

| Category | Keywords (Count) |
|---|---|
| Value/Constant | `True, False, None` (3) |
| Logical | `and, or, not` (3) |
| Conditional | `if, elif, else` (3) |
| Looping | `for, while, break, continue, pass` (5) |
| Function | `def, return, lambda, yield` (4) |
| Class | `class` (1) |
| Exception | `try, except, finally, raise, assert` (5) |
| Import | `import, from, as` (3) |
| Scope | `global, nonlocal` (2) |
| Async | `async, await` (2) |
| Identity/Membership | `is, in` (2) |
| Resource | `with` (1) |
| Deletion | `del` (1) |
| **Total** | **35** |

**Code Examples:**

```python
# Conditional
if 5 > 2:
    print("Greater")   # Uses: if

# Looping
for i in range(3):     # Uses: for, in
    print(i)

# Function with return
def add(a, b):         # Uses: def
    return a + b       # Uses: return
```

---

**(b) Keywords vs Built-in Identifiers (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Keywords | Built-in Identifiers |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Part of language syntax | Names pre-bound in `__builtins__` |
| **Count (Py 3)** | Exactly **35** | ~150+ (e.g., `print`, `len`, `int`) |
| **Shadowable?** | **No** ŌåÆ SyntaxError | **Yes** (allowed but discouraged) |
| **Namespace** | Handled by lexer/parser | Stored in `builtins` module |
| **Examples** | `if`, `for`, `class` | `print`, `list`, `dict`, `range` |

**Code Demonstration (3 Marks):**

```python
# Built-in can be overridden (BAD practice)
print("Hello")     # Works
print = 100        # Overrides print
print(print)       # Output: 100

# Restoring it
del print
print("Back!")     # Works again

# Keyword cannot be overridden
for = 10           # SyntaxError: invalid syntax
```

**Why keywords cannot be variables (2 Marks):**
Keywords are recognized at the **lexical analysis stage** before any namespace lookup. If Python allowed them to be reassigned, the parser would lose its ability to unambiguously distinguish control flow from assignment, breaking the grammar. Hence they are **strictly reserved**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What are Python keywords? Write a Python program to **display all keywords** and **count** them. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the following keywords with one example each: `with`, `lambda`, `yield`, `global`, `assert`, `is`, `in`. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### ­¤ōØ Model Solution ŌĆö Question B

**(a) Program to Display & Count Keywords (7 Marks)**

```python
# Program: list_all_keywords.py
import keyword

print("=" * 45)
print("  PYTHON 3 KEYWORD REFERENCE")
print("=" * 45)

# Display in formatted columns
for i, kw in enumerate(keyword.kwlist, start=1):
    print(f"{i:>3}. {kw:<10}", end="\t")
    if i % 5 == 0:
        print()   # Newline after every 5 keywords

print("\n" + "=" * 45)
print(f"Total Keywords in Python 3: {len(keyword.kwlist)}")
print("=" * 45)

# Verification helper
test_words = ['for', 'hello', 'True', 'data_science']
print("\nKeyword Verification:")
for w in test_words:
    status = "KEYWORD" if keyword.iskeyword(w) else "Not a keyword"
    print(f"  {w:<12} ŌåÆ {status}")
```

**Sample Output (truncated):**
```
=============================================
  PYTHON 3 KEYWORD REFERENCE
=============================================
  1. False      	 2. None       	 3. True       	 4. and        	 5. as
  6. assert     	 7. async      	 8. await      	 9. break      	10. class
 ...
=============================================
Total Keywords in Python 3: 35
=============================================

Keyword Verification:
  hello        ŌåÆ Not a keyword
  data_science ŌåÆ Not a keyword
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Code (4) + Output explanation (2) + Concept (1).

---

**(b) Explanation of Specific Keywords (7 Marks ŌĆö 1 mark each)**

| Keyword | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **`with`** | Context manager for safe resource handling | `with open('f.txt') as f: data = f.read()` |
| **`lambda`** | Anonymous single-expression function | `square = lambda x: x*x; print(square(5))  # 25` |
| **`yield`** | Pauses function, returns generator | `def gen(): yield 1; yield 2` |
| **`global`** | Declares a variable as module-level inside a function | `global counter; counter += 1` |
| **`assert`** | Debugging check; raises `AssertionError` if false | `assert x > 0, "x must be positive"` |
| **`is`** | Identity comparison (same memory object) | `a is b` ŌåÆ True if `id(a)==id(b)` |
| **`in`** | Membership test in a collection | `if 'a' in ['a','b']: print("found")` |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤øæ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout ŌĆö Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
>
> 1. **Wrong count** ŌåÆ Writing *33, 34, or 36* instead of **35** ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**
> 2. **Case mistake** ŌåÆ Writing `true`, `false`, `none` as keywords ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ0.5 mark each**
> 3. **Confusing built-ins with keywords** ŌåÆ Listing `print`, `len`, `int` as keywords ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks**
> 4. **Missing `async/await`** ŌåÆ Often forgotten in Python 3 lists ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**
> 5. **No examples** ŌåÆ Definitions alone without code samples ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks in 14-mark Q**
> 6. **Mixing Python 2 & 3** ŌåÆ Listing `print` as a keyword (it's a built-in in Py3, a keyword in Py2) ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö Print This Before Exam!**

Ō£ģ Python 3 has **exactly 35 keywords** (memorize the count!).

Ō£ģ Keywords are **case-sensitive**: `True` Ō£ģ vs `true` ŌØī.

Ō£ģ Three keywords are **capitalized**: `True`, `False`, `None` ŌĆö all others are **lowercase**.

Ō£ģ Verify using **`keyword.iskeyword(word)`** and list using **`keyword.kwlist`**.

Ō£ģ Keywords **cannot** be variable names ŌĆö doing so raises **`SyntaxError`**.

Ō£ģ **Built-ins are NOT keywords** ŌĆö `print`, `len`, `list`, `dict` are built-in identifiers, not reserved words.

Ō£ģ **`async`, `await`** were introduced in Python 3.5+; **`nonlocal`** in 3.0.

Ō£ģ **`yield`** makes a function a **generator function** (lazy iterator).

Ō£ģ **`with`** is the preferred way to handle files, locks, and database connections.

Ō£ģ **`lambda`** creates anonymous functions limited to a **single expression** (no statements).

Ō£ģ **`is` vs `==`**: `is` checks **identity** (same object), `==` checks **equality** (same value).

Ō£ģ **`assert`** is a **debugging aid**, not a runtime validation tool ŌĆö can be disabled with `python -O`.

Ō£ģ Keywords are recognized at the **lexer stage**, making them un-shadowable.

Ō£ģ Total keywords vary by version: Python 2 = 31, Python 3.0 = 33, Python 3.5+ = 35.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variables and Assignment Statement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Variables and Assignment Statement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable (KTU Definition):** A *variable* in Python is a symbolic name (identifier) that refers to an object stored in the computer's memory. It acts as a **reference** (or pointer) to a memory location rather than the memory location itself. Variables in Python are **dynamically typed**, meaning their type is determined at runtime by the object they currently reference, not declared explicitly by the programmer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assignment Statement (KTU Definition):** An *assignment statement* is the fundamental Python statement of the form `variable_name = expression`, which binds (associates) the name on the left-hand side (LHS) to the object produced by evaluating the expression on the right-hand side (RHS). It does **not** copy values ŌĆö it creates a reference binding.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **variable as a sticky label pasted on a box**, and the **object as the actual content inside the box**.

- The **box** is a memory location holding a real Python object (e.g., the integer `42`, the string `"Hello"`).
- The **label** is your variable name (e.g., `score`, `name`).
- The **assignment statement** is the act of pasting that label onto the box.
- Re-assignment is simply *peeling off the label* and pasting it onto a different box.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for Machine Learning:** In ML, every dataset column, weight, bias, or hyperparameter is stored in a variable. Mistakenly understanding variables as "containers holding values" (instead of *labels pointing to objects*) is the #1 reason beginners get confused by Python's mutable vs. immutable behavior ŌĆö a classic KTU exam pitfall.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Identifier** | The textual name given to a variable. |
| **Object** | The actual data entity stored in memory (has a type, id, value). |
| **Binding** | The link created between a name and an object via assignment. |
| **Reference** | The relationship a variable holds to its object. |
| **`id()`** | Built-in Python function returning the unique memory address identifier. |
| **`type()`** | Built-in Python function returning the class/type of the object. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Assignment Statement

An assignment statement in Python has the structure:

```
target = expression
```

It is evaluated in **two strict phases**:
1. **Evaluate the RHS:** Compute the object produced by `expression` (creates a new object in memory if needed).
2. **Bind to the LHS:** Bind the name `target` to that resulting object. Any previous binding of `target` is discarded (garbage-collected if unreferenced).

### 2.2 Rules for Naming Variables (KTU Frequently Asked)

- Must **start** with a letter (AŌĆōZ, aŌĆōz) or an **underscore** `_`.
- Subsequent characters may include letters, digits (0ŌĆō9), or underscores.
- **Cannot** start with a digit (e.g., `2var` is illegal).
- **Cannot** be a Python reserved keyword (e.g., `for`, `if`, `class`, `True`, `None`).
- **Case-sensitive:** `Score` and `score` are two different variables.
- Convention: use **snake_case** (e.g., `learning_rate`, `num_epochs`) for ordinary variables and **UPPER_SNAKE_CASE** for constants.

### 2.3 Types of Assignment Statements (KTU High-Yield Table)

| # | Type | Syntax | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple | `x = 10` | Binds `x` to integer `10`. |
| 2 | Chained | `a = b = c = 5` | All three names refer to the **same object** `5`. |
| 3 | Tuple / Unpacking | `x, y, z = 1, 2, 3` | Positional binding of RHS tuple elements to LHS names. |
| 4 | Extended Unpacking | `a, *b, c = [1,2,3,4,5]` | `a=1`, `b=[2,3,4]`, `c=5`. |
| 5 | Augmented | `x += 5` | Equivalent to `x = x + 5` but modifies in-place for mutable objects. |
| 6 | Annotation | `x: int = 10` | Adds type hint (PEP 484); does NOT enforce type at runtime. |

### 2.4 Dynamic Typing vs. Static Typing

Python is **dynamically typed**:
- A variable does not have a fixed type ŌĆö it can be rebound to any object at any time.
- The type "lives with the object," not with the name.

```python
x = 10          # x refers to an int object
x = "hello"     # now x refers to a str object ŌĆö perfectly legal
x = [1, 2, 3]   # now x refers to a list object
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Dynamic typing accelerates prototyping in ML workflows (scikit-learn, pandas) where variable types frequently change between NumPy arrays, pandas DataFrames, and PyTorch tensors. However, it sacrifices compile-time safety ŌĆö hence the rise of *type hints* (`x: int`) and tools like `mypy` in production ML pipelines.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Address Identity | $\texttt{id}(x) == \texttt{id}(y)$ iff $x$ and $y$ reference the **same object** | `x = [1]; y = x; id(x)==id(y)` ŌåÆ `True` |
| Augmented Addition | $x \mathrel{+}= a \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x = x + a$ | `x = 5; x += 3` ŌåÆ `x == 8` |
| Augmented Multiplication | $x \mathrel{*}= a \;\Longleftrightarrow\; x = x \cdot a$ | `x = 4; x *= 2` ŌåÆ `x == 8` |
| Chained Binding Order | $a = b = c = v \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{one object } v \text{ with three names}$ | `id(a) == id(b) == id(c)` |
| Identity vs Equality | $x \text{ is } y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \texttt{id}(x) = \texttt{id}(y)$ | `a = 256; b = 256; a is b` ŌåÆ `True` (small int cache) |
| Equality of Value | $x == y \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \texttt{value}(x) = \texttt{value}(y)$ | `[1] == [1]` ŌåÆ `True` (same content) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identity vs Equality ŌĆö A KTU Favorite:** Always remember ŌĆö `==` compares **values**; `is` compares **memory identities (references)**. Two different list objects with the same content are `==` but **not** `is`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Memory Model of an Assignment

Consider the sequence:

```python
a = 10
b = a
a = 20
print(b)
```

**Step 1 ŌĆö Evaluate the literal `10`:**
Python's interpreter creates a new integer object with value `10` at some memory address (say `0x7FAA1001`). Integer objects are immutable.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Bind the name `a`:**
The assignment statement binds the identifier `a` to the object at `0x7FAA1001`.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate `a`:**
When `b = a` is executed, Python first evaluates the RHS. The expression `a` yields the object currently referenced by `a` ŌĆö which is the integer `10` at `0x7FAA1001`.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Bind `b`:**
The name `b` is now also bound to the *same* object at `0x7FAA1001`. There is now **one object with two names** ŌĆö `a` and `b`.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Re-assign `a`:**
`a = 20` evaluates `20` (creates a new integer object at, say, `0x7FAA1002`) and rebinds `a` to it. The name `b` still points to the original `10`.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final state:**
- `a` ŌåÆ object `20` at `0x7FAA1002`
- `b` ŌåÆ object `10` at `0x7FAA1001`

So `print(b)` outputs **`10`**, not `20`.

The formal "proof" in symbolic terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After Line 1: } & \texttt{a} \xrightarrow{\text{bind}} \text{Object}(10) \\
\text{After Line 2: } & \texttt{a} \xrightarrow{\text{bind}} \text{Object}(10) \xleftarrow{\text{bind}} \texttt{b} \\
\text{After Line 3: } & \texttt{a} \xrightarrow{\text{bind}} \text{Object}(20) \quad ; \quad \texttt{b} \xrightarrow{\text{bind}} \text{Object}(10) \\
\text{Therefore: } & \text{Output of } \texttt{print(b)} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mutable vs Immutable Re-Assignment

| Initial Code | Object Type | Behavior on `b = a` then `a += 1` | Final Value of `b` |
|---|---|---|---|
| `a = 5; ...` | `int` (immutable) | New object created for `a`; `b` unchanged | Original value |
| `a = "hi"; ...` | `str` (immutable) | New string created for `a`; `b` unchanged | Original value |
| `a = [1,2]; ...` | `list` (mutable) | `a += [3]` modifies list in-place; both see change | Modified value |
| `a = (1,2); ...` | `tuple` (immutable) | New tuple created for `a`; `b` unchanged | Original value |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The difference between `a = a + [3]` (creates new list, `b` unaffected) and `a += [3]` (in-place `__iadd__` for lists, both affected) is a guaranteed KTU question ŌĆö memorize it!

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Chained Assignment Trace

Code: `x = y = z = [1, 2]`

Step-by-step internal evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1. Evaluate RHS: } & \text{Create a single list object } L = [1, 2] \text{ at address } 0x1000. \\
\text{2. Bind target list left-to-right: } & \texttt{x} \to L,\ \texttt{y} \to L,\ \texttt{z} \to L. \\
\text{3. Identity check: } & \texttt{id(x)} == \texttt{id(y)} == \texttt{id(z)} \quad \Rightarrow \text{True}. \\
\text{4. Mutation effect: } & \text{If } \texttt{x.append(3)} \text{ is called, then } \texttt{y} \text{ and } \texttt{z} \text{ also show } [1,2,3].
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the "shared reference" nature of chained assignment ŌĆö critical for ML where mutating a model weight list affects every alias.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Memory Binding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Assignment Statement] --> B[Parse LHS Identifier Name]
    A --> C[Parse and Evaluate RHS Expression]
    C --> D{Object already exists in memory?}
    D -- No --> E[Create New Object in Heap Memory]
    D -- Yes --> F[Locate Existing Object via id]
    E --> G[Assign Memory Address]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Bind LHS Name to Object Reference]
    H --> I[Discard Previous Binding if Any]
    I --> J[Run Garbage Collector on Orphaned Object]
    J --> K[Statement Complete]
    B --> H
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Model: Variable as a Label on a Memory Box

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MemoryHeap["PYTHON HEAP MEMORY"]
        Box1["Box containing int 10"]
        Box2["Box containing str HELLO"]
        Box3["Box containing list 1,2,3"]
    end
    subgraph Namespace["VARIABLE NAMESPACE"]
        L1["Variable x"]
        L2["Variable name"]
        L3["Variable data"]
    end
    L1 -. points to .-> Box1
    L2 -. points to .-> Box2
    L3 -. points to .-> Box3
```

### 4.3 Re-Assignment Trace Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Code as Python Code
    participant Mem as Memory Heap
    participant Var as Variable Namespace
    Code->>Mem: Create int object 10 at id=1001
    Code->>Var: Bind name 'a' to id=1001
    Code->>Mem: Create int object 20 at id=1002
    Code->>Var: Re-bind 'a' to id=1002
    Note over Var: 'a' no longer references id=1001
    Code->>Mem: Garbage collect object at id=1001
```

### 4.4 Augmented vs Standard Assignment Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Statement Encountered] --> Check{Operator is Augmented?}
    Check -- No / x = x + 5 --> Std[Evaluate RHS x + 5]
    Std --> CreateNew[Create New Object with Result]
    CreateNew --> RebindStd[Rebind x to New Object]
    Check -- Yes / x += 5 --> LookUp[Look up object referenced by x]
    LookUp --> Method{Object supports __iadd__?}
    Method -- Yes and Mutable --> InPlace[Modify Object In-Place]
    InPlace --> SameRef[Reference id remains same]
    Method -- No / Immutable --> Fallback[Treat as x = x + 5]
    Fallback --> Std
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between **`is`** operator and **`==`** operator in Python with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The `==` operator checks **value equality** ŌĆö whether two objects have the same content. *(1 mark)*
- The `is` operator checks **identity equality** ŌĆö whether two names refer to the **same memory object** (same `id()`). *(1 mark)*
- Example: `a = [1, 2]; b = [1, 2]` ŌåÆ `a == b` is `True`, but `a is b` is `False` because they are two distinct list objects in memory. *(1 mark)*

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024]`** What is **dynamic typing** in Python? How does it differ from static typing?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Dynamic typing means the type of a variable is determined at **runtime** by the object it currently references, not declared in advance by the programmer. *(1 mark)*
- A single variable can be reassigned to objects of different types during execution (`x = 5; x = "hi"`). *(1 mark)*
- In contrast, static typing (C, Java) requires explicit type declaration and a variable cannot change its declared type ŌĆö providing compile-time safety but less flexibility. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **variables and assignment statements** in Python with a suitable example. Discuss the rules for naming identifiers. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Variable definition (1 mark):** A variable in Python is a symbolic name that serves as a reference to an object stored in memory. It is *not* a "container" of data but a *label* pointing to a data object.

2. **Assignment statement syntax (1 mark):** `variable_name = expression` ŌĆö Python first evaluates the RHS expression to produce an object, then binds the LHS name to that object.

3. **Example trace (2 marks):**
   ```python
   price = 99.99       # binds 'price' to a float object 99.99
   price = price + 10  # creates new float 109.99, rebinds 'price'
   ```

4. **Naming rules (3 marks):**
   - Must begin with a letter (AŌĆōZ, aŌĆōz) or underscore `_`.
   - Can contain letters, digits, and underscores afterwards.
   - Cannot begin with a digit (`2var` invalid).
   - Cannot be a reserved keyword (`class`, `for`, `if`, `True`, `None`).
   - Case-sensitive (`Score` ŌēĀ `score`).
   - Convention: snake_case for variables, UPPER_CASE for constants.

**(b)** With an example, explain **chained assignment** and **augmented assignment**. Show how identity (`is`) behaves in each case. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Chained assignment definition (1.5 marks):** Multiple names bound to the same object in a single statement: `a = b = c = 5`. All three names refer to the **same integer object** ŌĆö verified by `a is b` returning `True`.

2. **Example with identity check (1.5 marks):**
   ```python
   a = b = c = [1, 2]
   print(a is b)   # True ŌĆö same object
   print(id(a) == id(b))  # True ŌĆö same memory id
   ```

3. **Augmented assignment definition (1.5 marks):** Combines a binary operation with assignment: `x += y`, `x *= y`, `x //= y`, etc. Equivalent to `x = x + y` for immutable types, but **modifies in-place** (`__iadd__`) for mutable types like lists.

4. **Comparative example (2.5 marks):**
   ```python
   a = [1, 2]
   b = a
   a += [3]           # in-place modification for lists
   print(b)           # [1, 2, 3] ŌĆö b sees the change!
   
   a = [1, 2]
   b = a
   a = a + [3]        # creates new list, a rebinds
   print(b)           # [1, 2] ŌĆö b unaffected
   ```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is **dynamic typing** in Python? Explain with an example how the same variable can refer to objects of different types. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** Dynamic typing means the type is associated with the **object**, not the variable name. Python determines the type at runtime, and a single variable can be reassigned to any type of object without prior declaration.

2. **Example demonstration (3 marks):**
   ```python
   data = 42              # data refers to int object
   print(type(data))      # <class 'int'>
   
   data = 3.14            # now refers to float
   print(type(data))      # <class 'float'>
   
   data = "Machine Learning"  # now refers to str
   print(type(data))      # <class 'str'>
   
   data = [1, 2, 3]       # now refers to list
   print(type(data))      # <class 'list'>
   ```
   Each time, the previous object becomes unreferenced and is eventually garbage-collected.

3. **Comparison with static typing (2 marks):** In C/Java, `int x = 10;` declares `x` as permanently int. Reassigning `x = "hello"` would be a compile-time error. Python's flexibility accelerates ML prototyping but loses compile-time guarantees ŌĆö which is why modern Python supports optional **type hints** (`x: int = 10`).

**(b)** Explain **tuple unpacking** and **extended unpacking** assignment with examples. State one practical ML use case. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Tuple unpacking (2 marks):** Assigns RHS iterable elements to LHS names positionally:
   ```python
   x, y, z = (10, 20, 30)
   # x=10, y=20, z=30
   ```

2. **Extended unpacking with `*` (2 marks):** Captures multiple middle elements into a list:
   ```python
   first, *middle, last = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
   # first=1, middle=[2,3,4], last=5
   ```

3. **Swapping variables trick (1 mark):**
   ```python
   a, b = b, a     # elegant tuple-unpacking swap
   ```

4. **ML use case (2 marks):** Unpacking model evaluation results:
   ```python
   accuracy, precision, recall, f1 = evaluate_model(model, X_test, y_test)
   # unpacks returned tuple of metrics directly into named variables
   ```

---

### 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss zones for this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing `=` with `==`:** `=` is assignment, `==` is comparison. Writing `if x = 5:` (instead of `if x == 5:`) is a `SyntaxError`. **Loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks.**
> 2. **Believing assignment copies values:** Many students write `b = a` and expect `b` to be an independent copy. For mutable objects (lists, dicts), this creates an alias ŌĆö mutating one mutates both. **Loss: 2ŌĆō3 marks.**
> 3. **Mixing `is` with `==`:** Using `is` for value comparison (e.g., `if x is 5:`) works for small ints due to caching but fails for large numbers or floats. Always use `==` for value comparison. **Loss: 1ŌĆō2 marks.**
> 4. **Augmented assignment on tuples:** `t = (1,2); t += (3,4)` creates a **new** tuple (immutable) ŌĆö students expecting in-place mutation lose marks.

---

### 5.4 ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **variable** in Python is a *name that references an object* ŌĆö not a "box" holding a value.
- An **assignment statement** binds a name to an object created/evaluated on the RHS.
- Python uses **dynamic typing**: types belong to objects, not variable names.
- Naming rules: start with letter/underscore, case-sensitive, no keywords, snake_case convention.
- **Chained assignment** (`a = b = c = v`) creates **one shared object** referenced by all names.
- **Tuple unpacking** binds positional RHS values to LHS names.
- **Augmented assignment** (`+=`, `*=`, etc.) is equivalent to `x = x op y` for immutables but performs **in-place modification** for mutables.
- `==` tests **value equality**; `is` tests **identity (same memory object)**.
- Re-assigning a variable does **not** modify the original object ŌĆö it rebinds the name to a new object.
- Mutable types (`list`, `dict`, `set`) share state across aliases; immutable types (`int`, `str`, `tuple`) cannot be mutated ŌĆö rebinding creates new objects.
- For ML: variables store tensors, weights, datasets ŌĆö understanding reference semantics prevents accidental mutation bugs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OPERATORS (KTU Definition)**
> In Python, an **Operator** is a *special symbol* or *reserved keyword* that performs a specific operation on one or more operands (values/variables) and returns a result. Python is a dynamically-typed, high-level language where operators form the fundamental building blocks for expression evaluation, data manipulation, and control flow.

Mathematically, any Python expression of the form `operand_1 <operator> operand_2` is interpreted as a function call to an internally defined *dunder method* (e.g., `a + b` ŌåÆ `a.__add__(b)`).

The classification hierarchy in Python follows the **PTABLM** model:
- **P** - Precedence (binding tightness)
- **T** - Type-coercion (implicit conversion rules)
- **A** - Associativity (left/right binding)
- **B** - Boolean short-circuit behavior
- **L** - Left-to-right evaluation order
- **M** - Method-dispatch (dunder protocols)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen Recipe**
> Think of Python operators as **kitchen utensils**:
> - The **plus sign (`+`)** is a *mixer* ŌĆö it combines two ingredients.
> - The **double slash (`//`)** is a *knife* ŌĆö it chops a pizza into equal slices (integer division).
> - The **modulo (`%`)** is a *timer buzzer* ŌĆö it tells you what remains after dividing into equal time slots.
> - The **logical AND (`and`)** is a *safety interlock* ŌĆö both switches must be ON for the machine to start.
> - The **bitwise AND (`&`)** is a *mask* ŌĆö it only lets specific bits of information pass through.

When you write `x = 5 + 3`, you are essentially telling Python: *"Take two values (5 and 3), apply the 'addition' utensil, and store the result (8) in the box labeled x."*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Properties

- **Operator Overloading**: Python's ability to redefine operator behavior for user-defined classes.
- **Operand Count**: **Unary** (1 operand), **Binary** (2 operands), **Ternary** (3 operands).
- **Standard constant**: Boolean truth values are `True = 1` and `False = 0` in numeric contexts.
- **PEP 8 Convention**: Operators should be surrounded by **exactly one space** on either side (e.g., `a + b`, not `a+b`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Python Operators

Python's standard library defines **9 major operator categories**, listed in the order of importance for KTU 2024 evaluation:

#### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Operators
Used for mathematical computations. They support **operator overloading** for custom objects.

#### 2.1.2 Comparison (Relational) Operators
Return a `bool` value (`True` or `False`) after comparing two operands.

#### 2.1.3 Logical Operators
Used to combine conditional statements. Python uses *short-circuit evaluation*:
- `A and B` ŌåÆ If `A` is `False`, `B` is **not evaluated**.
- `A or B`  ŌåÆ If `A` is `True`,  `B` is **not evaluated**.

#### 2.1.4 Bitwise Operators
Operate on the **binary representation** of integers. The standard **word size for pedagogical purposes is 8 bits** unless otherwise stated.

#### 2.1.5 Assignment Operators
Compound assignment combines a binary operator with `=`. Semantically, `x op= y` is equivalent to `x = x op y`.

#### 2.1.6 Identity Operators (`is`, `is not`)
Test whether two variables point to the **same memory object** (not the same value).

#### 2.1.7 Membership Operators (`in`, `not in`)
Test whether a value is a member of a sequence (string, list, tuple, set, dict).

#### 2.1.8 Ternary (Conditional) Operator
The only ternary operator in Python: `value_if_true if condition else value_if_false`.

### 2.3 Operator Precedence Pyramid (Highest to Lowest)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precedence determines binding tightness.** Higher-priority operators "bind" first. For binary operators of equal precedence, associativity rules apply.

| Precedence Level | Operators | Description | Associativity |
|------------------|-----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 (Highest) | `**` | Exponentiation | Right-to-Left |
| 2 | `+x`, `-x`, `~x` | Unary plus, minus, bitwise NOT | Right-to-Left |
| 3 | `*`, `/`, `//`, `\%` | Multiplicative | Left-to-Right |
| 4 | `+`, `-` | Additive | Left-to-Right |
| 5 | `\ll`, `\gg` | Bitwise shifts | Left-to-Right |
| 6 | `&` | Bitwise AND | Left-to-Right |
| 7 | `^` | Bitwise XOR | Left-to-Right |
| 8 | $\vert$ | Bitwise OR | Left-to-Right |
| 9 | `\lt`, `\le`, `\gt`, `\ge`, `==`, `!=` | Comparisons | Left-to-Right |
| 10 | `not` | Logical NOT | Right-to-Left |
| 11 | `and` | Logical AND | Left-to-Right |
| 12 | `or` | Logical OR | Left-to-Right |
| 13 | `=`, `+=`, `-=`, ... | Assignments | Right-to-Left |
| 14 (Lowest) | Ternary `x if c else y` | Conditional | Right-to-Left |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ML engineers MUST master operators:**
> 1. **NumPy / Tensor** vectorization uses operator overloading to broadcast operations across millions of elements: `tensor + bias`.
> 2. **Masking** in image processing uses bitwise AND to isolate color channels: `cv2.bitwise_and(img, mask)`.
> 3. **Boolean indexing** in pandas uses comparison + bitwise operators: `df[(df.age \ge 18) & (df.score \gt 50)]`.
> 4. **Floor division** in data bucketing: `age_bucket = age // 10` creates decade bins.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Examples with Full Binary Trace

> [!NOTE]
> Every Python integer operation can be **traced bit-by-bit**. The standard convention in KTU examinations is to use **8-bit two's complement** representation for negative numbers. For positive values, plain binary is used.

---

#### 3.1.1 Derivation 1: Floor Division & Modulus Relationship

The **Division Algorithm** states that for any integers `a` and `b \neq 0`:

$$a = (a \, // \, b) \cdot b + (a \, \% \, b)$$

**Numerical Trace:** Compute `17 // 5` and `17 % 5` step-by-step.

Step 1 ŌĆö Identify dividend (`a = 17`) and divisor (`b = 5`).

Step 2 ŌĆö Perform true division: 
$$17 \, / \, 5 = 3.4$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Apply floor function to the quotient:
$$\lfloor 3.4 \rfloor = 3$$
Therefore, `17 // 5 = 3`.

Step 4 ŌĆö Compute remainder using the identity:
$$17 \, \% \, 5 = 17 - (3 \cdot 5) = 17 - 15 = 2$$

Step 5 ŌĆö Verification using the Division Algorithm:
$$a = q \cdot b + r \quad \Rightarrow \quad 17 = (3)(5) + 2 = 15 + 2 = 17 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### 3.1.2 Derivation 2: Modulo with Negative Operands (Python-Specific)

Python's modulo follows the **sign of the divisor** (mathematical modulo), unlike C/C++ which follow the sign of the dividend.

**Numerical Trace:** Compute `-17 % 5`.

Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the floor-division formula to find `q`:
$$q = -17 \, // \, 5$$

Step 2 ŌĆö True division: 
$$-17 \, / \, 5 = -3.4$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Apply floor (round toward negative infinity):
$$\lfloor -3.4 \rfloor = -4$$
Therefore, `q = -4`.

Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the remainder:
$$r = -17 - (-4)(5) = -17 + 20 = 3$$
So `-17 % 5 = 3` (positive result, matching divisor's sign).

---

#### 3.1.3 Derivation 3: Bitwise NOT (`~`) and Two's Complement

The operator `~x` computes `-x - 1` (one's complement plus one).

**Formula:**
$$\sim x = -(x + 1)$$

**Numerical Trace:** Compute `~5` in 8-bit two's complement.

Step 1 ŌĆö Write `5` in 8-bit binary: 
$$0000\,0101$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Invert every bit (one's complement): 
$$1111\,1010$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Interpret as two's complement. The MSB is `1`, so the value is negative.
$$\text{Value} = -128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 0 + 2 + 0 = -6$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Cross-check using the formula:
$$\sim 5 = -(5 + 1) = -6 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### 3.1.4 Derivation 4: Bitwise Left Shift as Multiplication

The formula for left shift is:
$$x \, \ll \, n \; = \; x \cdot 2^{n}$$

**Numerical Trace:** Compute `5 << 2`.

Step 1 ŌĆö Write `5` in 8-bit binary: 
$$0000\,0101$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Shift all bits **2 positions to the left**, padding right with zeros: 
$$0001\,0100$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Convert back to decimal: 
$$0001\,0100_{2} = 16 + 4 = 20$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Verify with the formula:
$$5 \cdot 2^{2} = 5 \cdot 4 = 20 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### 3.1.5 Derivation 5: Bitwise Right Shift as Floor Division

The formula for right shift is:
$$x \, \gg \, n \; = \; \lfloor x \, / \, 2^{n} \rfloor$$

**Numerical Trace:** Compute `25 >> 2`.

Step 1 ŌĆö Write `25` in 8-bit binary: 
$$0001\,1001$$

Step 2 ŌĆö Shift all bits **2 positions to the right**, padding left with zeros: 
$$0000\,0110$$

Step 3 ŌĆö Convert back to decimal: 
$$0000\,0110_{2} = 4 + 2 = 6$$

Step 4 ŌĆö Verify with the formula:
$$\lfloor 25 \, / \, 4 \rfloor = \lfloor 6.25 \rfloor = 6 \;\; \checkmark$$

---

#### 3.1.6 Derivation 6: Ternary Operator Evaluation

**Formula:**
$$\text{result} \; = \; \text{value\_true} \; \text{if} \; \text{condition} \; \text{else} \; \text{value\_false}$$

**Numerical Trace:** Evaluate `x = 10 if 7 \gt 5 else 20`.

Step 1 ŌĆö Evaluate the condition `7 > 5`: returns `True`.

Step 2 ŌĆö Select the "true" branch: `value_true = 10`.

Step 3 ŌĆö Assign to `x`: `x = 10`.

---

#### 3.1.7 Derivation 7: Identity vs. Equality ŌĆö A Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `==` compares **values**; `is` compares **memory identity** (object id).

**Numerical Trace:** Compare two equal lists.

Step 1 ŌĆö `list1 = [1, 2, 3]` and `list2 = [1, 2, 3]`.

Step 2 ŌĆö Both lists have **identical values**, so:
$$\text{list1} == \text{list2} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{True}$$

Step 3 ŌĆö However, they are **two separate objects** stored at different memory addresses:
$$\text{id(list1)} \neq \text{id(list2)} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{list1 is list2} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{False}$$

Step 4 ŌĆö This matters in ML: small integers (-5 to 256) and short strings are **interned** by CPython, so `x is y` may unexpectedly return `True`. Always use `==` for value comparison.

---

#### 3.1.8 Derivation 8: Short-Circuit Evaluation of `and`

**Numerical Trace:** Evaluate `(0) and (5/0)`.

Step 1 ŌĆö Python evaluates `0` first. In Boolean context, `0` is `False`.

Step 2 ŌĆö Since `A and B` short-circuits on `A == False`, the right operand `(5/0)` is **never evaluated**.

Step 3 ŌĆö Result returned: `0` (the falsy left operand itself, not a `bool`!).

Step 4 ŌĆö This is why short-circuit `and` is used in ML pipelines for **safe defaulting**:
$$\text{value} = \text{config.get("lr")} \; \text{and} \; 0.01$$
If the key is missing (`None`), the default `0.01` is used without a `KeyError`.

---

#### 3.1.9 Derivation 9: Chained Comparisons (Python-Specific Feature)

Python supports **mathematical chain notation**:
$$a \, < \, b \, < \, c \;\; \equiv \;\; (a < b) \; \text{and} \, (b < c)$$

**Numerical Trace:** Evaluate `3 < 5 < 7`.

Step 1 ŌĆö First comparison: `3 < 5` ŌåÆ `True`.

Step 2 ŌĆö Second comparison: `5 < 7` ŌåÆ `True`.

Step 3 ŌĆö Both must be `True`, so the chain returns `True`. (Note: the middle value `5` is evaluated only **once**, unlike `a < b and b < c` which evaluates `b` twice.)

---

#### 3.1.10 Derivation 10: Operator Precedence Resolution

**Numerical Trace:** Evaluate `2 + 3 * 4 ** 2`.

Step 1 ŌĆö Highest precedence is `**`:
$$4^{2} = 16$$
Expression becomes: `2 + 3 * 16`.

Step 2 ŌĆö Next precedence is `*`:
$$3 \cdot 16 = 48$$
Expression becomes: `2 + 48`.

Step 3 ŌĆö Finally, `+`:
$$2 + 48 = 50$$

Final answer: `50`.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A QUESTIONS (2 ├Ś 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)** *Differentiate between the `==` operator and the `is` operator in Python. Provide one example for each.*

**Model Answer:**
- The `==` operator (Equality Operator) compares the **values** of two operands and returns `True` if they are equal, regardless of whether they are the same object in memory. It is implemented via the `__eq__()` dunder method.
- The `is` operator (Identity Operator) checks whether two variables point to the **exact same object** in memory (i.e., the same memory address). It is implemented by comparing the `id()` of both operands.

**Example:**
- Equality: `print([1,2] == [1,2])` ŌåÆ `True` (same value, different objects).
- Identity: `print([1,2] is [1,2])` ŌåÆ `False` (different memory locations).

---

**Q2. (3 Marks)** *Explain the difference between `/` and `//` operators in Python with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**
- The `/` operator performs **true (floating-point) division** and **always returns a `float`**, even if the division is exact.
- The `//` operator performs **floor (integer) division** ŌĆö it divides and then applies the mathematical floor function to the result.

**Examples:**
- `print(7 / 2)`  ŌåÆ `3.5`  (float)
- `print(7 // 2)` ŌåÆ `3`    (floor of 3.5)
- `print(-7 // 2)` ŌåÆ `-4`  (floor of -3.5, NOT -3)
- `print(7.0 // 2)` ŌåÆ `3.0` (still returns float when either operand is float)

---

### ­¤ōĢ PART B QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### ­¤öĘ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]`

**Q (a) (7 Marks)** *Explain the various types of operators available in Python with appropriate examples for each category.*

**Model Solution:**

Python provides a rich set of operators categorized as follows:

**1. Arithmetic Operators (7 symbols):**

```python
a, b = 10, 3
print(a + b)   # 13  Addition
print(a - b)   # 7   Subtraction
print(a * b)   # 30  Multiplication
print(a / b)   # 3.333... True Division (always float)
print(a // b)  # 3   Floor Division
print(a % b)   # 1   Modulus (remainder)
print(a ** b)  # 1000 Exponentiation
```

**2. Comparison (Relational) Operators (6 symbols):**

```python
x, y = 5, 7
print(x == y)  # False  Equal to
print(x != y)  # True   Not equal
print(x > y)   # False  Greater than
print(x < y)   # True   Less than
print(x >= 5)  # True   Greater or equal
print(x <= 4)  # False  Less or equal
```

**3. Logical Operators (3 keywords):**

```python
p, q = True, False
print(p and q)  # False
print(p or q)   # True
print(not p)    # False
```

**4. Bitwise Operators (6 symbols):**

```python
m, n = 12, 10      # 12 = 1100, 10 = 1010
print(m & n)       # 8    (1000) Bitwise AND
print(m | n)       # 14   (1110) Bitwise OR
print(m ^ n)       # 6    (0110) Bitwise XOR
print(~m)          # -13       Bitwise NOT
print(m << 1)      # 24        Left shift
print(m >> 1)      # 6         Right shift
```

**5. Assignment & Compound Operators:**

```python
x = 5
x += 3   # 8   Same as x = x + 3
x -= 2   # 6
x *= 4   # 24
x //= 5  # 4
x **= 2  # 16
```

**6. Identity Operators:**

```python
a = [1, 2]
b = a
print(a is b)      # True  (same object)
c = [1, 2]
print(a is not c)  # True  (different objects)
```

**7. Membership Operators:**

```python
nums = [1, 3, 5, 7]
print(3 in nums)       # True
print(4 not in nums)   # True
```

**8. Ternary (Conditional) Operator:**

```python
age = 20
status = "Adult" if age >= 18 else "Minor"
print(status)  # "Adult"
```

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks)** *Demonstrate the use of bitwise operators in Python. Show the step-by-step evaluation of the expression `(25 & 14) | (10 ^ 6) << 1`.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Convert each operand to 8-bit binary:**
- $25 = 0001\,1001$
- $14 = 0000\,1110$
- $10 = 0000\,1010$
- $6  = 0000\,0110$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Evaluate the AND operation `25 & 14`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0001\,1001 \\
\land \; 0000\,1110 \\
\hline
0000\,1000
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: $0000\,1000_2 = 8$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the XOR operation `10 ^ 6`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0000\,1010 \\
\oplus \; 0000\,0110 \\
\hline
0000\,1100
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: $0000\,1100_2 = 12$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply the left shift `12 << 1`:**

$$12 \cdot 2^{1} = 24$$

Binary: $0001\,1000$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Apply the OR operation between the two sub-results `8 | 24`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0000\,1000 \\
\lor \; 0001\,1000 \\
\hline
0001\,1000
\end{aligned}
$$

Result: $0001\,1000_2 = 24$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Final Verification in Python:**

```python
print((25 & 14) | (10 ^ 6) << 1)  # Output: 24
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on precedence:** In the original expression, `<<` (precedence 5) binds **tighter** than `|` (precedence 8), so Python first computes `(10 ^ 6) << 1` and then performs the `&` and `|` evaluations. The grouping Python uses is: `(25 & 14) | ((10 ^ 6) << 1)`.

**Final Answer:** `24`

---

#### ­¤öĘ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) (7 Marks)** *Discuss operator precedence and associativity in Python. Evaluate the following expression with proper justification: `4 + 2 * 3 - 6 / 2 ** 2 + (3 \lt 5 and 7 \gt 4)`. What will be the result?*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply Parentheses First (Precedence 1 logic):** The sub-expression `(3 < 5 and 7 > 4)` is evaluated as a self-contained unit.

- $3 < 5$ ŌåÆ `True`
- $7 > 4$ ŌåÆ `True`
- `True and True` ŌåÆ `True`
- In numeric context: `True = 1`

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Exponentiation `**` (Precedence 1):**

$$2^{2} = 4$$

Expression becomes: `4 + 2 * 3 - 6 / 4 + 1`

**Step 3 ŌĆö Apply Multiplicative operators `*` and `/` (Precedence 3, left-to-right):**

$$2 \cdot 3 = 6$$
$$6 \, / \, 4 = 1.5$$

Expression becomes: `4 + 6 - 1.5 + 1`

**Step 4 ŌĆö Apply Additive operators `+` and `-` (Precedence 4, left-to-right):**

$$4 + 6 = 10$$
$$10 - 1.5 = 8.5$$
$$8.5 + 1 = 9.5$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Final Verification:**

```python
print(4 + 2 * 3 - 6 / 2 ** 2 + (3 < 5 and 7 > 4))  # 9.5
```

**Final Answer:** `9.5`

**Associativity Rule Used:** All arithmetic operators (`*`, `/`, `+`, `-`) are **left-associative**, except `**` which is **right-associative**. Hence `2 ** 2` is computed as `2 ** (2)` and not `(2 ** 2)`.

---

**Q (b) (7 Marks)** *Write a Python program that demonstrates the use of the ternary operator, identity operators, and membership operators. Explain the output of each segment.*

**Model Solution:**

```python
# --- SEGMENT 1: Ternary Operator ---
age = 17
status = "Eligible to vote" if age >= 18 else "Not eligible"
print(status)     # Output: "Not eligible"
```

**Explanation:** The ternary operator `value_if_true if condition else value_if_false` evaluates the condition `age >= 18`. Since `17 >= 18` is `False`, the `else` branch is selected.

```python
# --- SEGMENT 2: Identity Operators ---
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a             # b points to the SAME object as a
c = [1, 2, 3]     # c is a NEW object with the same value

print(a is b)     # True  (same memory id)
print(a is c)     # False (different memory id)
print(a is not c) # True
```

**Explanation:** The `is` operator checks memory identity. Even though `a` and `c` have the same value, they occupy different memory locations, so `a is c` returns `False`.

```python
# --- SEGMENT 3: Membership Operators ---
sentence = "Machine Learning with Python"
vowels = ['a', 'e', 'i', 'o', 'u']

print('M' in sentence)        # True
print('z' in sentence)        # False
print('z' not in sentence)    # True
print('a' in vowels)          # True
print('x' not in vowels)      # True
```

**Explanation:** The `in` operator performs a sequential search through the container (string or list). For strings, it checks substring presence; for lists, it checks element presence. All operations return `bool` values.

---

### ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing `/` with `//`:** Many students mark `7/2 = 3` instead of `3.5`. Remember: `/` ALWAYS returns a float; `//` returns the FLOOR of the division.
> 2. **Negative Modulo Sign Error:** Writing `-17 % 5 = -2` (C-style) instead of the correct `3` (Python-style). Python's modulo follows the divisor's sign.
> 3. **Precedence of Shift vs. OR:** `a | b << c` is parsed as `a | (b << c)`, NOT as `(a | b) << c`. This reverses results in 60% of attempted questions.
> 4. **`is` vs `==`:** Using `is` to compare list values is a logic error. Reserve `is` for `None`, `True`, `False` checks.
> 5. **Bitwise vs Logical on Booleans:** Writing `True & False` works but returns a boolean bitwise result, not the `and` short-circuit. The convention is to use `and`/`or` for boolean logic, `&`/`|` for bitwise/integer logic.
> 6. **Integer Division with Negatives:** `-7 // 2 = -4` (floor of -3.5), NOT `-3` (truncation).
> 7. **Right-shift of negatives:** In Python, `>>` is **arithmetic** (sign-preserving), not logical.

---

### ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Operators (KTU 2024 Module 1):**

- ŌŁÉ Python has **9 major categories** of operators; know the symbol and category for every operator on the Master Table.
- ŌŁÉ The `//` (floor division) operator rounds toward **negative infinity**, not toward zero.
- ŌŁÉ The `%` (modulo) operator in Python returns a result with the **same sign as the divisor** (mathematical convention).
- ŌŁÉ The `**` (exponentiation) operator is **right-associative**: `2 ** 3 ** 2 = 2 ** 9 = 512`, NOT `8 ** 2 = 64`.
- ŌŁÉ The `~` (bitwise NOT) operator satisfies the identity $\sim x = -(x + 1)$.
- ŌŁÉ Left shift is multiplication by $2^{n}$; right shift is floor division by $2^{n}$.
- ŌŁÉ `==` checks **value equality**; `is` checks **memory identity** (same `id()`).
- ŌŁÉ Python supports **chained comparisons**: `a < b < c` evaluates `b` only once.
- ŌŁÉ `and` and `or` perform **short-circuit evaluation** and return the actual operand value (not necessarily a `bool`).
- ŌŁÉ Operator precedence (highest ŌåÆ lowest): `**` ŌåÆ unary ŌåÆ `* / // %` ŌåÆ `+ -` ŌåÆ `<< >>` ŌåÆ `&` ŌåÆ `^` ŌåÆ `|` ŌåÆ comparisons ŌåÆ `not` ŌåÆ `and` ŌåÆ `or` ŌåÆ ternary ŌåÆ assignment.
- ŌŁÉ Boolean `True` is equivalent to integer `1`; `False` is equivalent to integer `0` in numeric contexts.
- ŌŁÉ The `is` operator works reliably only for `None`, `True`, `False`, and small integers (-5 to 256) due to **integer interning** in CPython.
- ŌŁÉ PEP 8 mandates a **single space** around binary operators for readability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expressions. Type Conversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Expressions. Type Conversions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Expressions

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **expression** in Python is a valid combination of *operands* (values/variables) and *operators* that the Python interpreter evaluates to produce a single resultant value. Every expression evaluates to (returns) a Python object and has an associated data type.

In simpler terms, an expression is anything you can put on the right-hand side of an assignment that **computes a value**.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Recipe Kitchen" ­¤Ź│

Imagine you are a chef in a kitchen:
- **Ingredients** (operands) are your raw materials ŌåÆ *flour, sugar, eggs* ŌåÆ these are your values and variables.
- **Cooking actions** (operators) are the verbs that act on ingredients ŌåÆ *mix, beat, heat* ŌåÆ these are your `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` operators.
- **The finished dish** is the final result that comes out of the oven ŌåÆ this is the **evaluated value** of the expression.

A Python expression is exactly this: take ingredients (operands), apply actions (operators), and the oven (interpreter) gives you a finished dish (a single value with a type).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> An expression is different from a **statement**. A *statement* does something (performs an action), while an *expression* **produces a value**. Python has *expression statements* (like `x = 5 + 3`), where the whole line is a statement but contains an expression.

## 1.2 Type Conversion

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Type Conversion** (also called *Type Casting*) is the process of converting a Python object from one data type to another. Python supports two mechanisms:
> 1. **Implicit Type Conversion (Coercion)** ŌĆö performed automatically by the Python interpreter.
> 2. **Explicit Type Conversion (Casting)** ŌĆö performed manually by the programmer using built-in functions like `int()`, `float()`, `str()`, `bool()`.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Currency Exchange Counter" ­¤Æ▒

Picture yourself at a foreign exchange counter at an airport:
- **Implicit conversion** is like the bank automatically converting your USD to INR when you swipe your card at an Indian merchant. You did not ask ŌĆö the system handled it intelligently because it knows the merchant only accepts INR.
- **Explicit conversion** is like you walking up to the counter and **explicitly saying**: "Please convert my 100 USD to Japanese Yen." You are forcing the conversion even if it loses context (you cannot buy ramen with yen designed for sushi shops!).

Both produce a converted value, but the difference is **who initiates the conversion** ŌĆö the system (implicit) or you (explicit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Type Conversion Matters in Machine Learning (KTU Context):**
> In ML pipelines (NumPy, Pandas, scikit-learn), datasets often contain mixed types ŌĆö string labels, float features, integer counts. **Type conversion is the first preprocessing step** before feeding data into ML models, because scikit-learn estimators strictly require numeric arrays (`float64`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Python Expression

An expression in Python is composed of three fundamental building blocks:

| Building Block | Symbol/Form | Role | KTU Board Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operands** | Literals, Variables, Function calls | The "data" being operated on | `x`, `5`, `len(s)` |
| **Operators** | Symbols like `+ - * /` | The "action" applied to operands | `+`, `>`, `and` |
| **Result** | Single Python object | The evaluated output | `8`, `True`, `"hello"` |

## 2.2 Classification of Python Operators (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **most-tested topic** in Module 1 board exams. Memorize every row.

### A. Arithmetic Operators

| Operator | Name | Operation | Example (x=10, y=3) | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `+` | Addition | `x + y` | `10 + 3` | `13` |
| `-` | Subtraction | `x - y` | `10 - 3` | `7` |
| `*` | Multiplication | `x * y` | `10 * 3` | `30` |
| `/` | True Division | `x / y` | `10 / 3` | `3.3333...` (**always returns `float`**) |
| `//` | Floor Division | `x // y` | `10 // 3` | `3` |
| `%` | Modulus | `x % y` | `10 % 3` | `1` |
| `**` | Exponentiation | `x ** y` | `10 ** 3` | `1000` |

### B. Comparison (Relational) Operators

> Return a **Boolean** value: `True` or `False`.

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `==` | Equal to | `5 == 5.0` | `True` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `5 != 3` | `True` |
| `>` | Greater than | `7 > 5` | `True` |
| `<` | Less than | `7 < 5` | `False` |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal to | `5 >= 5` | `True` |
| `<=` | Less than or equal to | `4 <= 5` | `True` |

### C. Logical Operators

| Operator | Description | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `and` | Logical AND (both true) | `(5 > 3) and (8 > 6)` | `True` |
| `or` | Logical OR (at least one true) | `(5 > 3) or (8 < 6)` | `True` |
| `not` | Logical NOT (negation) | `not (5 > 3)` | `False` |

### D. Assignment & Compound Assignment Operators

| Operator | Equivalent To | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `=` | Simple assignment | `x = 5` | Assigns `5` to `x` |
| `+=` | `x = x + a` | `x = 5; x += 3` | `x` becomes `8` |
| `-=` | `x = x - a` | `x = 5; x -= 3` | `x` becomes `2` |
| `*=` | `x = x * a` | `x = 5; x *= 3` | `x` becomes `15` |
| `/=` | `x = x / a` | `x = 10; x /= 4` | `x` becomes `2.5` |
| `//=` | `x = x // a` | `x = 10; x //= 4` | `x` becomes `2` |
| `%=` | `x = x % a` | `x = 10; x %= 4` | `x` becomes `2` |
| `**=` | `x = x ** a` | `x = 2; x **= 3` | `x` becomes `8` |

### E. Bitwise Operators (operate on binary representations)

| Operator | Name | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
