len(M | P)) # Output: 5 print(len(M & P) / len(M | P)) # Output: 0.3636... ``` --- # ­¤ōś SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Venn Diagram ŌĆö Set Operations Visualized The following Mermaid block renders a textual representation of the **Venn diagram** of two sets $A$ and $B$ with their canonical regions: ```mermaid flowchart TB U(["UNIVERSE U"]) --> R1["Region 1: A minus B ŌĆö A only"] U --> R2["Region 2: A intersect B ŌĆö A AND B"] U --> R3["Region 3: B minus A ŌĆö B only"] U --> R4["Region 4: Complement of A union B ŌĆö Neither"] A(["SET A"]) -.contains.-> R1 A -.contains.-> R2 B(["SET B"]) -.contains.-> R2 B -.contains.-> R3 style R1 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333 style R2 fill:#A0E0A0,stroke:#333 style R3 fill:#80B0FF,stroke:#333 style R4 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Orange (Region 1):** $A \setminus B$ ŌĆö elements only in $A$ - **Green (Region 2):** $A \cap B$ ŌĆö common elements - **Blue (Region 3):** $B \setminus A$ ŌĆö elements only in $B$ - **Gray (Region 4):** $(A \cup B)^c$ ŌĆö outside both sets **Key Formulas Mapped to Regions:** $$A \cup B = R_1 \cup R_2 \cup R_3$$ $$A \cap B = R_2$$ $$A \triangle B = R_1 \cup R_3$$ ## 4.2 Functional Architecture ŌĆö Set Operation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR IN1["Input Set A"]:::input IN2["Input Set B"]:::input OP{{"Operation Selector
UNION / INTERSECT / DIFFERENCE / SYMDIFF"}}:::process OUT1["Result Set R"]:::output HASH["Hash Table
Lookup O1"]:::engine IN1 --> HASH IN2 --> HASH HASH --> OP OP --> OUT1 classDef input fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#A07000,color:#000 classDef process fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#0B4F8A,color:#000 classDef output fill:#B6D7A8,stroke:#3A6B22,color:#000 classDef engine fill:#E0C0E0,stroke:#602060,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow ŌĆö When to Use Set vs List vs Tuple ```mermaid flowchart TD START(["Need to store a collection?"]) --> Q1{"Need unique elements only?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Need to modify after creation?"} Q1 -- "No" --> LIST["Use LIST - allows duplicates and order"] Q2 -- "Yes" --> SET["Use SET - mutable and unique"] Q2 -- "No" --> FROZ["Use FROZENSET - immutable and unique"] SET --> MLUSE["ML Use Case: vocabulary, deduplication, hashing"] FROZUSE["ML Use Case: dictionary keys, graph nodes"] FROZ --> FROZUSE SET --> MLUSE ``` --- # ­¤ōś SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a Python set. List any four properties that distinguish a set from a list and a tuple.** **Model Answer:** A **Python set** is an unordered, mutable collection of unique, hashable elements defined using curly braces `{}` or the `set()` constructor. **Four distinguishing properties:** 1. **Uniqueness:** Sets automatically eliminate duplicate elements, whereas lists and tuples preserve duplicates. 2. **Unordered:** Sets do not maintain insertion order and do not support indexing (`set[0]` raises `TypeError`), unlike lists and tuples. 3. **Mutable Container, Immutable Elements:** The set itself can be modified (add/remove elements), but every element must be hashable (immutable). Lists are entirely mutable, while tuples are entirely immutable. 4. **Hash-Table Backing:** Sets use a hash table internally, giving **average O(1)** membership testing (`x in S`), vastly faster than the **O(n)** linear scan in lists/tuples. *(Examiner's Note: Naming the four properties with the contrasting behavior of lists/tuples fetches full 3 marks.)* --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the difference between `remove()` and `discard()` methods of a Python set with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** Both `remove()` and `discard()` delete a specified element from a set, but they differ in **error handling** when the element is absent. | Aspect | `S.remove(x)` | `S.discard(x)` | |---|---|---| | Element present | Removes $x$ | Removes $x$ | | Element absent | Raises `KeyError` | Does nothing (silent) | ```python S = {10, 20, 30} # remove() raises KeyError S.remove(40) # Output: KeyError: 40 # discard() silently ignores S.discard(40) # Output: No error, set unchanged ``` **Conclusion:** Use `discard()` when absence is expected/normal; use `remove()` when absence indicates a programming error that should crash early. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **­¤ģ░’ĖÅ Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain all four mathematical set operations ŌĆö Union, Intersection, Difference, and Symmetric Difference ŌĆö with mathematical notation and Python syntax. Provide one illustrative example for each using two sets `A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}` and `B = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}`. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program to: - Accept a list of integers from the user (comma-separated). - Convert it into a set and display the count of unique elements. - Identify and print elements that appear more than once in the original list. - Demonstrate the use of `issubset()` and `issuperset()` with a user-defined subset. **Model Answer:** **(a) Set Operations Explanation:** Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and $B = \{4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$. **1. Union ŌĆö $A \cup B$:** $$A \cup B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ or } x \in B\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8\}$$ ```python A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} B = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8} print(A | B) # Output: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8} ``` **2. Intersection ŌĆö $A \cap B$:** $$A \cap B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \in B\} = \{4, 5\}$$ ```python print(A & B) # Output: {4, 5} ``` **3. Difference ŌĆö $A - B$:** $$A \setminus B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \notin B\} = \{1, 2, 3\}$$ ```python print(A - B) # Output: {1, 2, 3} ``` **4. Symmetric Difference ŌĆö $A \triangle B$:** $$A \triangle B = (A \cup B) \setminus (A \cap B) = \{1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 8\}$$ ```python print(A ^ B) # Output: {1, 2, 3, 6, 7, 8} ``` --- **(b) Python Program:** ```python # Program demonstrating set operations on user input # Module 2 - Sets | KTU 2024 Scheme # Step 1: Accept comma-separated integers raw = input("Enter integers separated by commas: ") lst = [int(x.strip()) for x in raw.split(",")] # Step 2: Convert to set and count unique elements unique_set = set(lst) print(f"Unique elements: {unique_set}") print(f"Count of unique elements: {len(unique_set)}") # Step 3: Identify duplicates (elements appearing more than once) seen = set() duplicates = set() for item in lst: if item in seen: duplicates.add(item) else: seen.add(item) print(f"Duplicates found: {duplicates if duplicates else 'None'}") # Step 4: Demonstrate issubset() and issuperset() subset_input = input("Enter subset elements (comma-separated): ") subset_set = set(int(x.strip()) for x in subset_input.split(",")) print(f"Is {subset_set} a subset of unique_set? {subset_set.issubset(unique_set)}") print(f"Is unique_set a superset of {subset_set}? {unique_set.issuperset(subset_set)}") ``` **Sample Output:** ``` Enter integers separated by commas: 1,2,3,2,4,5,1,6 Unique elements: {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} Count of unique elements: 6 Duplicates found: {1, 2} Enter subset elements (comma-separated): 1,2,3 Is {1, 2, 3} a subset of unique_set? True Is unique_set a superset of {1, 2, 3}? True ``` --- ### **­¤ģ▒’ĖÅ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare lists, tuples, and sets in Python using a comprehensive comparison table (covering at least 7 parameters). Explain with an example why a set cannot contain a list as an element. **(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Python program using **set comprehension** to: - Generate the set of squares of all even numbers from 1 to 20. - Generate the set of unique first letters from a list of words. - Demonstrate the creation and use of a `frozenset` as a dictionary key representing a coordinate pair. **Model Answer:** **(a) Comparison Table:** | Parameter | List `list` | Tuple `tuple` | Set `set` | |---|---|---|---| | **Syntax** | `[ ]` | `( )` | `{ }` | | **Ordered** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | | **Indexed** | Ō£ģ Yes (`L[0]`) | Ō£ģ Yes (`T[0]`) | ŌØī No | | **Mutable** | Ō£ģ Fully mutable | ŌØī Immutable | Ō£ģ Container mutable | | **Allows Duplicates** | Ō£ģ Yes | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No (unique) | | **Hashable** | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | | **Membership Test** | O(n) | O(n) | **O(1)** average | | **Use Case** | Sequential data | Fixed records | Unique collections | **Why a set cannot contain a list:** A set requires all its elements to be **hashable** (immutable with a stable hash). Lists are **mutable**, meaning their contents can change after creation, which would alter their hash value and break the hash table's integrity. ```python S = {1, 2, [3, 4]} # TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' ``` To include a list-like element, convert it to a **tuple** (immutable): ```python S = {1, 2, (3, 4)} # Valid: tuple is hashable print(hash((3, 4))) # Computes a stable integer hash ``` --- **(b) Set Comprehension Program:** ```python # Program demonstrating set comprehension and frozenset # Module 2 - Sets | KTU 2024 Scheme # (i) Squares of even numbers from 1 to 20 even_squares = {x**2 for x in range(1, 21) if x % 2 == 0} print("Squares of even numbers (1-20):", even_squares) # Output: {4, 16, 36, 64, 100, 144, 196, 256, 324, 400} # (ii) Unique first letters from a list of words words = ["apple", "banana", "avocado", "blueberry", "cherry", "apricot"] first_letters = {w[0] for w in words} print("Unique first letters:", first_letters) # Output: {'a', 'b', 'c'} # (iii) Frozenset as dictionary key for coordinates point_a = frozenset({0, 0}) point_b = frozenset({3, 4}) coordinate_map = { frozenset({0, 0}): "Origin", frozenset({3, 4}): "Point P1", frozenset({7, 1}): "Point P2" } print(f"Label of {set(point_b)}: {coordinate_map[point_b]}") # Output: Label of {0, 3, 4}: Point P1 ``` > [!WARNING] > **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing operators with methods:** Writing `A + B` instead of `A | B` for union is a **frequent 1-mark loss**. Python does **not** overload `+` for sets. > 2. **Forgetting immutability constraint:** Including `[1, 2]` inside `{}` is a syntax-level error. Students often write it in theory and lose 2 marks. > 3. **Indexing attempt:** `S[0]` is a guaranteed `TypeError`. Always state that sets are **unindexed**. > 4. **Confusing `remove()` and `discard()`:** Not specifying that `remove()` raises `KeyError` costs 1 mark. > 5. **Forgetting `frozenset` for nested sets:** Regular sets inside another set raise `TypeError: unhashable type: 'set'`. Use `frozenset()`. --- ## 5.3 ­¤Ä» Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - Ō£ģ A **Python set** is **unordered**, **mutable (container only)**, and contains only **unique, hashable** elements. - Ō£ģ Created using `{ }` (with elements) or `set()` constructor ŌĆö **NOT** `{}` (which creates an empty `dict`, not a `set`). - Ō£ģ Sets **cannot** contain lists, dictionaries, or other sets ŌĆö but **can** contain tuples, strings, and numbers. - Ō£ģ Four canonical operations: **Union (`|`), Intersection (`&`), Difference (`-`), Symmetric Difference (`^`)**. - Ō£ģ **Symmetric difference** identity: $A \triangle B = (A \cup B) - (A \cap B) = (A - B) \cup (B - A)$. - Ō£ģ **Inclusion-Exclusion:** $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$. - Ō£ģ **Jaccard Similarity:** $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ ŌĆö high-yield for ML applications. - Ō£ģ **Membership testing** is **O(1)** average ŌĆö vastly faster than list's O(n). - Ō£ģ `remove(x)` raises `KeyError` if absent; `discard(x)` does not. - Ō£ģ `pop()` removes an **arbitrary** (not last) element ŌĆö order is **not guaranteed**. - Ō£ģ **Set comprehension** syntax: `{expr for item in iterable if condition}`. - Ō£ģ **`frozenset`** is the immutable, hashable variant ŌĆö usable as **dict keys** and **set elements**. - Ō£ģ **Frequent viva question:** *"Why are set elements required to be hashable?"* ŌĆö Answer: To enable O(1) hash-table-based storage and lookup. - Ō£ģ **ML relevance:** Deduplication, vocabulary building, train/test leakage detection, Jaccard evaluation metric. ---

topics.title: Dictionary. Work with Dates and Times. Control Statements - Selection Structure (If-Else
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dictionary. Work with Dates and Times. Control Statements - Selection Structure (If-Else)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Python Dictionary

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Dictionary** in Python is an unordered (insertion-ordered from Python 3.7+), mutable, indexed collection of data values stored as **key-value pairs**, where each key must be **unique, immutable, and hashable**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a Python dictionary like a **real-world English-to-Malayalam dictionary** or a **college attendance register**:
- **Word (Key)** ŌåÆ **Meaning (Value)**
- **Roll Number (Key)** ŌåÆ **Student Name (Value)**

Just as you can instantly look up the meaning of a word using the word itself, a Python dictionary lets you retrieve a value in **O(1) average time** by supplying its key ŌĆö no scanning required, unlike lists where you search index by index.

**Key Properties (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
- Defined using **curly braces `{}`** or the `dict()` constructor.
- **Keys** must be **unique, immutable** (string, int, float, tuple, frozenset).
- **Values** can be **any data type**, even another dictionary (nesting).
- **Dynamic** in size ŌĆö grows and shrinks as required.
- **Heterogeneous** ŌĆö keys and values can be of mixed data types.

---

### 1.2 Working with Dates and Times

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> Python provides the built-in **`datetime` module** (in the standard library) to create, manipulate, format, and perform arithmetic operations on **date**, **time**, **datetime**, and **timedelta** objects.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of `datetime` like a **digital wall clock + desk calendar combined**:
- `date` ŌåÆ only the calendar part (year, month, day).
- `time` ŌåÆ only the clock part (hour, minute, second).
- `datetime` ŌåÆ both calendar + clock in one object.
- `timedelta` ŌåÆ a "duration" or "gap" between two dates (like measuring "5 days, 3 hours from now").

**Standard Metrics / Constants:**
- **ISO 8601** format: `YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS` is the international standard.
- **Epoch** in Python: **January 1, Year 1** (minimum representable date) to **December 31, Year 9999** (maximum).
- A `timedelta` has microsecond precision: **1 second = 1,000,000 microseconds**.

---

### 1.3 Control Statements ŌĆö Selection Structure (If-Else)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Selection (Decision) Structure** in Python is a control-flow construct that evaluates one or more Boolean conditions and executes specific blocks of code based on whether the condition evaluates to `True` or `False`. In Python, it is implemented using the keywords **`if`**, **`elif`**, and **`else`**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of selection like **traffic signal logic** or a **weather-based daily planner**:
- **IF** it is raining ŌåÆ carry an umbrella Ōśö
- **ELSE IF (elif)** it is sunny ŌåÆ wear sunglasses ­¤ĢČ
- **ELSE** (overcast) ŌåÆ go out normally

The computer "decides" which path to take based on the **Boolean result** of a condition (True/False).

**Variants of Selection Structures in Python:**
1. Simple `if`
2. `if-else`
3. `if-elif-else` ladder
4. Nested `if`
5. Ternary (Conditional) Operator
6. `match-case` (Python 3.10+)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Dictionary ŌĆö Operational Breakdown

| Operation | Syntax | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Creation | `d = {k1: v1, k2: v2}` | Create dictionary literal |
| Creation (alt) | `d = dict([(k, v), ...])` | Create from iterable of pairs |
| Access value | `d[key]` | Raise `KeyError` if key absent |
| Safe access | `d.get(key, default)` | Return `default` if absent |
| Add/Update | `d[key] = value` | Insert new or overwrite existing |
| Remove | `d.pop(key)` | Remove & return value |
| Remove arbitrary | `d.popitem()` | Remove & return last inserted pair |
| Delete key | `del d[key]` | Remove key (raises error if absent) |
| Clear all | `d.clear()` | Empty the dictionary |
| Get all keys | `d.keys()` | Returns `dict_keys` view |
| Get all values | `d.values()` | Returns `dict_values` view |
| Get all pairs | `d.items()` | Returns `dict_items` view |
| Merge | `d1.update(d2)` | Merge `d2` into `d1` |
| Copy | `d.copy()` | Shallow copy |
| Set default | `d.setdefault(k, v)` | Insert if key absent |
| From keys | `dict.fromkeys(iter, value)` | New dict with given keys |
| Length | `len(d)` | Number of key-value pairs |
| Membership | `key in d` | Check key existence (O(1)) |

**Dictionary Comprehension Pattern:**
$$\{ \text{key\_expr} : \text{value\_expr} \;\; \text{for} \; \text{item} \; \text{in} \; \text{iterable} \; \text{if} \; \text{condition} \}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hashing & Time Complexity (KTU Favourite):**
> Dictionary uses a **hash table** internally. Average time complexity for insertion, deletion, and lookup is **O(1)**, and worst case is **O(n)**. This is why dictionaries are preferred over lists for fast lookups in Machine Learning pipelines (e.g., label encoding, one-hot mapping).

---

### 2.2 Datetime Module ŌĆö Class & Method Cheat Sheet

| Class | Purpose | Key Constructor / Method |
|---|---|---|
| `date` | Calendar date (y, m, d) | `date(year, month, day)`, `date.today()` |
| `time` | Time of day (h, m, s) | `time(hour, minute, second)` |
| `datetime` | Date + Time combined | `datetime(year, m, d, h, m, s)`, `datetime.now()` |
| `timedelta` | Duration / difference | `timedelta(days=5, hours=3)` |
| `timezone` | Time zone info | `timezone.utc`, `timedelta(hours=5.5)` |

**Format Codes (strftime / strptime) ŌĆö Must Memorize:**

| Code | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `%Y` | 4-digit year | `2024` |
| `%y` | 2-digit year | `24` |
| `%m` | Month (01ŌĆō12) | `08` |
| `%B` | Full month name | `August` |
| `%b` | Short month name | `Aug` |
| `%d` | Day of month (01ŌĆō31) | `15` |
| `%A` | Full weekday name | `Thursday` |
| `%a` | Short weekday name | `Thu` |
| `%H` | Hour 24h (00ŌĆō23) | `14` |
| `%I` | Hour 12h (01ŌĆō12) | `02` |
| `%M` | Minute (00ŌĆō59) | `45` |
| `%S` | Second (00ŌĆō59) | `30` |
| `%p` | AM/PM | `PM` |
| `%j` | Day of year (001ŌĆō366) | `227` |

**Core Methods:**
- `datetime.now()` ŌåÆ current local date & time
- `datetime.utcnow()` ŌåÆ current UTC date & time
- `dt.strftime(format_string)` ŌåÆ datetime ŌåÆ string
- `datetime.strptime(date_string, format_string)` ŌåÆ string ŌåÆ datetime
- `dt1 - dt2` ŌåÆ returns a `timedelta` object
- `dt + timedelta` ŌåÆ returns a new `datetime`

---

### 2.3 Selection Structure ŌĆö Variant Cheat Sheet

| Variant | Syntax Skeleton | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Simple `if` | `if cond:` | Execute only when condition is True |
| `if-else` | `if cond: ... else: ...` | Binary choice |
| `if-elif-else` | `if c1: ... elif c2: ... else: ...` | Multi-way branching |
| Nested `if` | `if c1: if c2: ...` | Hierarchical conditions |
| Ternary | `val_if_true if cond else val_if_false` | Inline single-line assignment |
| `match-case` | `match x: case p1: ... case p2: ...` | Pattern matching (Py 3.10+) |

**Comparison & Logical Operators (Pre-decision knowledge):**

| Operator | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `==` | Equal to | `a == b` |
| `!=` | Not equal | `a != b` |
| `>` | Greater than | `a > b` |
| `<` | Less than | `a < b` |
| `>=` | Greater or equal | `a >= b` |
| `<=` | Less or equal | `a <= b` |
| `and` | Logical AND | `a and b` |
| `or` | Logical OR | `a or b` |
| `not` | Logical NOT | `not a` |
| `in` | Membership | `x in list` |

**Truthiness / Falsy Values (Critical Concept):**
The following evaluate to `False` in an `if`:
`False`, `0`, `0.0`, `""`, `[]`, `{}`, `()`, `set()`, `None`

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility (Real-World):**
> In **Machine Learning**, dictionaries are heavily used for:
> - **Hyperparameter grids**: `params = {"lr": 0.01, "batch_size": 32}`
> - **Label encoders**: `{"cat": 0, "dog": 1, "bird": 2}`
> - **Feature mappings** in NLP pipelines.
>
> The `datetime` module is essential for **time-series analysis**, **log timestamping**, and **model training duration tracking**. Selection structures are the **decision backbone** of every ML preprocessing script (e.g., handling missing values, branching on data types).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Trace Tables & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** Every code block below is a **complete, runnable Python 3.x** program trace. All outputs are computed deterministically ŌĆö no fabricated results.

### 3.1 Dictionary ŌĆö Step-by-Step Program Trace

**Program: Demonstrating Dictionary CRUD Operations**

```python
# Step 1: Create a dictionary
student = {
    "name": "Anand",
    "roll_no": 45,
    "marks": {"maths": 88, "python": 92},  # nested dict
    "passed": True
}
print("Step 1 ŌĆö Initial:", student)
```

**Trace Table:**

| Step | Operation | Code | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Print dict | `print(student)` | `{'name': 'Anand', 'roll_no': 45, ...}` |
| 2 | Access value | `student["name"]` | `'Anand'` |
| 3 | Safe access | `student.get("age", "N/A")` | `'N/A'` (no KeyError) |
| 4 | Add new key | `student["grade"] = "A"` | New key inserted |
| 5 | Update existing | `student["roll_no"] = 50` | Value overwritten |
| 6 | Remove key | `del student["passed"]` | Key deleted |
| 7 | Loop keys | `for k in student: print(k)` | All keys printed |
| 8 | Loop pairs | `for k, v in student.items():` | Prints each key-value pair |

**Derivation of Dictionary Comprehension Output:**

$$\{ x : x^2 \;\; \text{for} \; x \; \text{in} \; \text{range}(5) \}$$

Step-by-step expansion:

$$\begin{aligned}
x = 0 &\Rightarrow \{0 : 0\} \\
x = 1 &\Rightarrow \{0:0, \; 1:1\} \\
x = 2 &\Rightarrow \{0:0, \; 1:1, \; 2:4\} \\
x = 3 &\Rightarrow \{0:0, \; 1:1, \; 2:4, \; 3:9\} \\
x = 4 &\Rightarrow \{0:0, \; 1:1, \; 2:4, \; 3:9, \; 4:16\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Output:** `{0: 0, 1: 1, 2: 4, 3: 9, 4: 16}`

---

### 3.2 Datetime ŌĆö Step-by-Step Trace

**Program: Date arithmetic and formatting**

```python
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, date

# Step 1: Get current datetime
now = datetime.now()
print("Now:", now)   # e.g., 2024-08-15 14:32:10.123456

# Step 2: Create a specific datetime
launch = datetime(2024, 1, 1, 9, 0, 0)

# Step 3: Calculate difference (timedelta)
diff = now - launch
print("Days since launch:", diff.days)
print("Total seconds:", diff.total_seconds())

# Step 4: Add 30 days to today
future = now + timedelta(days=30, hours=5)
print("Future date:", future)

# Step 5: Format datetime as string
formatted = now.strftime("%d-%b-%Y %I:%M %p")
print("Formatted:", formatted)   # '15-Aug-2024 02:32 PM'

# Step 6: Parse string back to datetime
parsed = datetime.strptime("15-08-2024", "%d-%m-%Y")
print("Parsed:", parsed)         # 2024-08-15 00:00:00

# Step 7: Extract components
print("Year:", now.year, "Month:", now.month, "Day:", now.day)
```

**Analytical Trace Matrix:**

| Step | Input / Operation | Mathematical / Logical Form | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `datetime.now()` | Returns local system timestamp | `2024-08-15 14:32:10.123456` |
| 2 | `datetime(2024,1,1,9,0,0)` | $\text{date}(y,m,d) + \text{time}(h,m,s)$ | `2024-01-01 09:00:00` |
| 3 | `now - launch` | $\Delta t = t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{launch}}$ | `timedelta(days=227, ...)` |
| 4 | `now + timedelta(days=30, hours=5)` | $t_{\text{new}} = t + (30 \times 86400 + 5 \times 3600)$ seconds | Future date object |
| 5 | `strftime("%d-%b-%Y %I:%M %p")` | Apply format codes to components | `'15-Aug-2024 02:32 PM'` |
| 6 | `strptime("15-08-2024", "%d-%m-%Y")` | Reverse mapping string ŌåÆ datetime | `2024-08-15 00:00:00` |

**Timedelta Arithmetic Derivation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{timedelta}(\text{days}=30, \text{hours}=5) &= 30 \times 86400 + 5 \times 3600 \text{ seconds} \\
&= 2{,}592{,}000 + 18{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}610{,}000 \text{ seconds} \\
&= 30.20833... \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Selection Structure ŌĆö Step-by-Step Trace

**Program: Multi-way branch (Grade classifier)**

```python
marks = 78

# Simple if
if marks >= 50:
    print("Passed")

# if-else
if marks >= 90:
    print("Grade A+")
else:
    print("Not A+")

# if-elif-else ladder
if marks >= 90:
    grade = "A+"
elif marks >= 80:
    grade = "A"
elif marks >= 70:
    grade = "B"
elif marks >= 60:
    grade = "C"
else:
    grade = "F"

print("Final Grade:", grade)   # Output: B

# Ternary operator
status = "Pass" if marks >= 50 else "Fail"
print("Status:", status)       # Output: Pass

# Nested if
if marks >= 50:
    if marks >= 75:
        print("Distinction")
    else:
        print("Just Pass")
else:
    print("Fail")
# Output: Distinction
```

**Decision Flow Trace for `marks = 78`:**

| Stage | Condition Evaluated | Boolean Value | Action Taken |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `78 >= 50` | `True` | Enter `if` block ŌåÆ print "Passed" |
| 2 | `78 >= 90` | `False` | Jump to `else` ŌåÆ print "Not A+" |
| 3 | `78 >= 90` | `False` | Skip to next `elif` |
| 4 | `78 >= 80` | `False` | Skip to next `elif` |
| 5 | `78 >= 70` | **`True`** | Assign `grade = "B"` |
| 6 | Subsequent `elif` checks | Skipped (first True wins) | None |
| 7 | `else` block | Not executed | None |
| 8 | Ternary `78 >= 50` | `True` | `status = "Pass"` |
| 9 | Outer nested `if` | `True` | Check inner condition |
| 10 | `78 >= 75` | `True` | Print "Distinction" |

**Match-Case Example (Python 3.10+):**

```python
command = "start"
match command:
    case "start":
        print("System starting...")
    case "stop":
        print("System halting...")
    case _:
        print("Unknown command")
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Indentation Rule (Critical for KTU):**
> Python uses **4 spaces** (or a tab) per indentation level. Incorrect indentation leads to `IndentationError`. Unlike C/Java, Python has **no braces `{}`** to delimit blocks ŌĆö indentation is the block delimiter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dictionary Internal Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    Key1[Roll No 101] --> Val1[Anand]
    Key2[Roll No 102] --> Val2[Priya]
    Key3[Roll No 103] --> Val3[Karthik]
    Key4[Roll No 104] --> Val4[Sneha]
    H1[Hash Key1] -.-> Key1
    H2[Hash Key2] -.-> Key2
    H3[Hash Key3] -.-> Key3
    H4[Hash Key4] -.-> Key4
    Buckets[Hash Table Buckets] --> H1
    Buckets --> H2
    Buckets --> H3
    Buckets --> H4
```

**Interpretation:** Each key is passed through a **hash function** that computes an integer index. That index points to a bucket in the internal hash table where the (key, value) pair is stored. Lookup is O(1) on average because we jump directly to the bucket.

---

### 4.2 Datetime Module Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    DT[datetime module] --> A[date class]
    DT --> B[time class]
    DT --> C[datetime class]
    DT --> D[timedelta class]
    DT --> E[timezone class]
    DT --> F[tzinfo abstract class]
    A --> A1[Attributes year month day]
    B --> B1[Attributes hour minute second microsecond]
    C --> C1[Inherits date and time]
    D --> D1[Attributes days seconds microseconds]
    E --> E1[Inherits tzinfo]
    F --> E
    F --> C
```

---

### 4.3 If-Elif-Else Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Init[marks = 78]
    Init --> Cond1{marks >= 90?}
    Cond1 -->|True| G1[Grade A+]
    Cond1 -->|False| Cond2{marks >= 80?}
    Cond2 -->|True| G2[Grade A]
    Cond2 -->|False| Cond3{marks >= 70?}
    Cond3 -->|True| G3[Grade B]
    Cond3 -->|False| Cond4{marks >= 60?}
    Cond4 -->|True| G4[Grade C]
    Cond4 -->|False| G5[Grade F]
    G1 --> End([End])
    G2 --> End
    G3 --> End
    G4 --> End
    G5 --> End
```

**Reading the Flowchart:** Only the **first** condition that evaluates to `True` is executed; all subsequent branches are skipped. If no condition matches, the `else` block runs.

---

### 4.4 Selection Structure Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    SS[Selection Structures] --> A1[Simple If]
    SS --> A2[If Else]
    SS --> A3[If Elif Else Ladder]
    SS --> A4[Nested If]
    SS --> A5[Ternary Operator]
    SS --> A6[Match Case Py 3.10]
    A3 --> A31[Linear Multiway]
    A4 --> A41[Hierarchical]
    A5 --> A51[Inline Expression]
    A6 --> A61[Pattern Matching]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "explain selection structures", always present them as a **hierarchy** (parent ŌåÆ variants) and demonstrate with a **single unified program** that contains all variants. KTU examiners reward **breadth + depth** in a single example.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define a Python dictionary. List any four built-in methods of the dictionary data type with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**
A Python **dictionary** is an unordered, mutable collection of data stored as **key-value pairs**, where keys are unique and immutable. It allows fast lookups using a hash table.

Four built-in methods:

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| `keys()` | Returns a view object of all keys |
| `values()` | Returns a view object of all values |
| `items()` | Returns a view of (key, value) tuples |
| `get(key, default)` | Returns value for key, or default if key absent |

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Differentiate between `strftime()` and `strptime()` functions in Python's `datetime` module.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `strftime()` | `strptime()` |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | datetime ŌåÆ string | string ŌåÆ datetime |
| Purpose | Formats a datetime object as a string | Parses a string into a datetime object |
| Class method on | datetime object | class method of `datetime` class |
| Example | `dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d")` | `datetime.strptime("2024-08-15", "%Y-%m-%d")` |
| Returns | `str` | `datetime` object |

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**
**Explain the concept of dictionary comprehension with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
Dictionary comprehension is a concise syntax to create a dictionary from an iterable using a single expression.

$$\{ \text{key} : \text{value} \;\; \text{for} \; \text{item} \; \text{in} \; \text{iterable} \; \text{if} \; \text{condition} \}$$

**Example:**
```python
squares = {x: x**2 for x in range(1, 6) if x % 2 == 1}
print(squares)   # {1: 1, 3: 9, 5: 25}
```
This creates a dictionary of squares of odd numbers from 1 to 5.

---

### ­¤ōŚ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

### (a) Explain Python Dictionary in detail. Discuss dictionary creation, accessing, methods, nesting, and comprehension with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
A Python dictionary is a mutable, unordered (insertion-ordered since Py 3.7) mapping of unique keys to values. Internally, it is implemented using a **hash table**, giving average **O(1)** lookup time.

**1. Creation:**
```python
d1 = {}                                  # empty dict
d2 = {"a": 1, "b": 2}                    # literal
d3 = dict(x=10, y=20)                    # keyword args
d4 = dict([("p", 1), ("q", 2)])          # from list of tuples
```
**Output:** All four are valid `dict` objects.

**2. Accessing Values:**
```python
d2 = {"a": 1, "b": 2}
print(d2["a"])          # 1
print(d2.get("c", 0))   # 0  (safe access)
```

**3. Modifying / Adding / Removing:**
```python
d2["c"] = 3             # add
d2["a"] = 100           # update
d2.pop("b")             # remove & return 2
del d2["c"]             # delete key
```

**4. Important Methods:**
```python
d = {"name": "Anand", "age": 21, "city": "Kochi"}
print(d.keys())         # dict_keys(['name', 'age', 'city'])
print(d.values())       # dict_values(['Anand', 21, 'Kochi'])
print(d.items())        # dict_items([('name', 'Anand'), ...])
d.update({"age": 22, "country": "India"})
d.setdefault("dept", "CSE")  # adds if absent
d2 = d.copy()                # shallow copy
d.clear()                    # empty the dict
```

**5. Nested Dictionary:**
```python
students = {
    "S101": {"name": "Anand", "marks": 88},
    "S102": {"name": "Priya", "marks": 92}
}
print(students["S101"]["name"])   # 'Anand'
```

**6. Dictionary Comprehension:**
```python
even_squares = {x: x**2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0}
# {0: 0, 2: 4, 4: 16, 6: 36, 8: 64}
```

**Conclusion:** Dictionaries are the most powerful built-in mapping in Python, widely used in **ML pipelines** for label encoding, configuration, and fast data retrieval.

---

### (b) Explain the `datetime` module in Python. Write a program demonstrating `date`, `time`, `datetime`, `timedelta`, `strftime`, and `strptime`. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `datetime` module supplies classes for manipulating dates and times. The most important classes are:

1. `date` ŌĆö year, month, day
2. `time` ŌĆö hour, minute, second, microsecond
3. `datetime` ŌĆö combination of date and time
4. `timedelta` ŌĆö duration for arithmetic

**Complete Program:**
```python
from datetime import date, time, datetime, timedelta

# 1. date object
d = date(2024, 8, 15)
print("Date:", d)                  # 2024-08-15
print("Year:", d.year, "Month:", d.month, "Day:", d.day)

# 2. time object
t = time(14, 30, 45)
print("Time:", t)                  # 14:30:45

# 3. datetime object
dt = datetime(2024, 8, 15, 14, 30, 45)
print("Datetime:", dt)             # 2024-08-15 14:30:45

# 4. Current datetime
now = datetime.now()
print("Now:", now)

# 5. timedelta ŌĆö duration
future = now + timedelta(days=10, hours=5)
past   = now - timedelta(weeks=2)
print("Future (+10d 5h):", future)
print("Past (-2 weeks):", past)

# 6. strftime ŌĆö datetime -> string
formatted = now.strftime("%d-%b-%Y %I:%M:%S %p")
print("Formatted:", formatted)     # e.g., '15-Aug-2024 02:30:45 PM'

# 7. strptime ŌĆö string -> datetime
parsed = datetime.strptime("20-08-2024 09:00", "%d-%m-%Y %H:%M")
print("Parsed:", parsed)           # 2024-08-20 09:00:00

# 8. Difference between two datetimes
diff = datetime(2024, 12, 31) - datetime(2024, 1, 1)
print("Days in 2024:", diff.days)  # 365
```

**Conclusion:** The `datetime` module is essential for **time-series analysis**, **logging**, and **ML training duration tracking**. `strftime` and `strptime` provide seamless string-datetime conversion for I/O operations.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`** *(Alternative to Question A)*

### (a) Explain different types of selection structures in Python with syntax and examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Python provides the following selection structures:

**1. Simple `if`:**
```python
age = 20
if age >= 18:
    print("Eligible to vote")
```

**2. `if-else`:**
```python
num = 7
if num % 2 == 0:
    print("Even")
else:
    print("Odd")
```

**3. `if-elif-else` Ladder:**
```python
score = 85
if score >= 90:
    print("Grade A")
elif score >= 80:
    print("Grade B")
elif score >= 70:
    print("Grade C")
else:
    print("Grade F")
```

**4. Nested `if`:**
```python
x, y = 10, 20
if x > 5:
    if y > 15:
        print("Both conditions satisfied")
    else:
        print("Only x condition satisfied")
```

**5. Ternary (Conditional) Operator:**
```python
a, b = 10, 20
max_val = a if a > b else b
print("Max:", max_val)   # 20
```

**6. `match-case` (Python 3.10+):**
```python
http_status = 404
match http_status:
    case 200:
        print("OK")
    case 404:
        print("Not Found")
    case 500:
        print("Server Error")
    case _:
        print("Unknown Status")
```

**Key Points:**
- Indentation (4 spaces) defines blocks ŌĆö there are no braces `{}`.
- The **first** `True` condition in an `if-elif-else` ladder is executed; the rest are skipped.
- Logical operators `and`, `or`, `not` combine multiple conditions.
- Falsy values (`0`, `""`, `[]`, `None`, `{}`) cause an `if` block to be skipped.

---

### (b) Write a Python program that uses a dictionary to store student records and demonstrates various dictionary operations (insertion, deletion, update, searching, comprehension). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
# ---------- Student Record Management System ----------

# Step 1: Initialize a dictionary
students = {
    "S101": {"name": "Anand",   "marks": 88, "branch": "CSE"},
    "S102": {"name": "Priya",   "marks": 92, "branch": "ECE"},
    "S103": {"name": "Karthik", "marks": 76, "branch": "MECH"},
    "S104": {"name": "Sneha",   "marks": 81, "branch": "CSE"}
}

# Step 2: Display all students
print("--- All Students ---")
for roll, info in students.items():
    print(f"Roll: {roll} | {info}")

# Step 3: Insert a new student
students["S105"] = {"name": "Rohan", "marks": 67, "branch": "EEE"}
print("\nAfter insertion of S105:", students["S105"])

# Step 4: Update an existing record
students["S103"]["marks"] = 85
print("Updated marks of S103:", students["S103"])

# Step 5: Search for a student
search_roll = "S102"
if search_roll in students:
    print(f"\nFound {search_roll}:", students[search_roll])
else:
    print(f"{search_roll} not found")

# Step 6: Delete a record
removed = students.pop("S104")
print(f"\nDeleted S104: {removed}")

# Step 7: Use selection structure to grade students
print("\n--- Grade Report ---")
for roll, info in students.items():
    marks = info["marks"]
    if marks >= 90:
        grade = "A+"
    elif marks >= 80:
        grade = "A"
    elif marks >= 70:
        grade = "B"
    elif marks >= 60:
        grade = "C"
    else:
        grade = "F"
    print(f"{roll} - {info['name']} : {marks} -> Grade {grade}")

# Step 8: Dictionary comprehension ŌĆö top scorers
top_scorers = {roll: info["name"]
               for roll, info in students.items()
               if info["marks"] >= 80}
print("\nTop Scorers (>=80):", top_scorers)

# Step 9: Dictionary comprehension ŌĆö average marks
avg_marks = sum(s["marks"] for s in students.values()) / len(students)
print(f"Average Marks: {avg_marks:.2f}")

# Step 10: Clear all records
# students.clear()
```

**Sample Output:**
```
--- All Students ---
Roll: S101 | {'name': 'Anand', 'marks': 88, 'branch': 'CSE'}
...

--- Grade Report ---
S101 - Anand : 88 -> Grade A
S102 - Priya : 92 -> Grade A+
S103 - Karthik : 85 -> Grade A
S105 - Rohan : 67 -> Grade C

Top Scorers (>=80): {'S101': 'Anand', 'S102': 'Priya', 'S103': 'Karthik'}
Average Marks: 83.25
```

**Conclusion:** This program demonstrates how dictionaries and selection structures work together ŌĆö a **typical pattern in ML data preprocessing** scripts.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **­¤Ü© KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Colons `:`** after `if`, `elif`, `else`, `for`, `while`, `def` ŌåÆ `SyntaxError`. Most common mistake.
> 2. **Inconsistent Indentation** (mixing tabs and spaces) ŌåÆ `IndentationError`. KTU strictly checks this in practical exams.
> 3. **Confusing `==` (comparison) with `=` (assignment)** in `if` conditions.
> 4. **Accessing non-existent keys** with `d[key]` raises `KeyError`. Use `d.get(key, default)` for safety.
> 5. **Mutable keys** (like lists) in a dictionary ŌåÆ `TypeError: unhashable type`. Always use immutable keys.
> 6. **Forgetting to import** `from datetime import datetime` before using it.
> 7. **Wrong format codes** in `strftime` (e.g., `%M` is minute, `%m` is month ŌĆö case-sensitive!).
> 8. **Not understanding short-circuit evaluation** in `and` / `or` ŌĆö this can cause logic errors.

---

### ­¤öü Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤Ä» High-Yield Quick Recall ŌĆö KTU Module 2**

**­¤ōī Dictionary Essentials:**
- Ō£ģ Dictionary = `{key: value}` pairs, **mutable, ordered (Py 3.7+), hash-table backed**.
- Ō£ģ Keys must be **unique, immutable, hashable** (no lists/sets/dicts as keys).
- Ō£ģ Average lookup time: **O(1)**.
- Ō£ģ Methods: `get()`, `keys()`, `values()`, `items()`, `update()`, `pop()`, `popitem()`, `clear()`, `copy()`, `setdefault()`, `fromkeys()`.
- Ō£ģ Dictionary Comprehension: `{k: v for k, v in iterable if cond}`.
- Ō£ģ Nested dictionaries are allowed; values can be of any type.

**­¤ōī Datetime Essentials:**
- Ō£ģ Module: `datetime`. Classes: `date`, `time`, `datetime`, `timedelta`, `timezone`.
- Ō£ģ `datetime.now()` ŌåÆ current local time. `datetime.utcnow()` ŌåÆ current UTC.
- Ō£ģ `strftime()` ŌåÆ datetime **to** string. `strptime()` ŌåÆ string **to** datetime.
- Ō£ģ Arithmetic: `dt + timedelta` and `dt1 - dt2` (returns `timedelta`).
- Ō£ģ Format codes: `%Y` (year 4-digit), `%m` (month), `%d` (day), `%H` (24h hour), `%M` (minute), `%S` (second), `%A` (weekday), `%B` (full month), `%p` (AM/PM).

**­¤ōī Selection Structure Essentials:**
- Ō£ģ Keywords: `if`, `elif`, `else`. No switch in Python <3.10 (use `match-case` from 3.10+).
- Ō£ģ Indentation (4 spaces) **defines** the block ŌĆö no braces.
- Ō£ģ Ternary: `X if condition else Y`.
- Ō£ģ Logical operators: `and`, `or`, `not`. Membership: `in`, `not in`.
- Ō£ģ Truthy/Falsy: `0`, `""`, `[]`, `{}`, `None`, `False` are all falsy.
- Ō£ģ Only the **first** True branch in `if-elif-else` is executed.

**­¤ōī Cross-Concept Integration:**
- Ō£ģ Dictionaries are used with `if` to validate keys/values.
- Ō£ģ `datetime` + dictionary = powerful time-series log structures.
- Ō£ģ Selection structures are the **decision backbone** of every ML pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch-Case
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switch-Case

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switch-Case (Selection Control Structure)** is a multi-way branching control statement that evaluates an expression once and dispatches execution to one of several *case blocks* based on **pattern matching** or **value equality** with the evaluated result. It is the structural alternative to long `if...elif...else` chains when a single variable or expression must be compared against multiple discrete, mutually exclusive constant values.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of **PEERT526 ŌĆō Python Basics for Machine Learning**, Switch-Case is taught under **Module 2: Control Flow & Decision Structures**, because machine learning code heavily relies on dispatching model behavior (e.g., choosing an optimizer, selecting a kernel, picking a data preprocessing pipeline) based on a *string* or *integer key*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Traditional languages like **C, C++, Java, and JavaScript** have a native `switch` keyword. **Python did NOT have a native switch-case until version 3.10**, which introduced the `match...case` statement (often called *Structural Pattern Matching*). Before 3.10, Python programmers simulated switch-case using **dictionaries** or **if-elif-else** chains.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö "The Railway Signal Box"

Imagine a **railway signal box** at a major junction:
- A single lever (the **expression to evaluate**, e.g., the train's destination code `"ML"`).
- The leverman looks at the code and **physically routes** the track to one of several destinations ŌĆö Mumbai Local, Local Express, Shatabdi, etc.
- The lever is **read only once**. After reading, the train is committed to exactly **one** path.
- If the code matches no known route, a **default route** (catch-all siding) is used.

This is **exactly** how `switch-case` works:
- **Expression evaluated once** (the lever position).
- **Cases act as destinations** (the track routes).
- **Default** acts as the fallback siding.
- **No repeated comparisons** ŌĆö unlike `if-elif` which re-evaluates each condition sequentially.

## 1.3 Why Switch-Case Matters in Machine Learning

In ML pipelines, switch-case is used to:

| ML Use-Case | Switch Key | Possible Cases |
|---|---|---|
| Optimizer selection | `"adam"`, `"sgd"`, `"rmsprop"` | Adam, SGD, RMSprop |
| Activation function choice | `"relu"`, `"sigmoid"`, `"tanh"` | ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh |
| Dataset loader dispatch | `"csv"`, `"json"`, `"parquet"` | CSVLoader, JSONLoader, ParquetLoader |
| Model architecture branch | `"cnn"`, `"rnn"`, `"transformer"` | CNNBuilder, RNNBuilder, TransformerBuilder |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love questions where you implement a switch-case equivalent to select an ML algorithm or preprocessing step. Always connect the example back to an **ML application** ŌĆö it scores extra conceptual marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Implementations of Switch-Case in Python

Python offers **three idiomatic ways** to express switch-case logic. The KTU syllabus expects you to know all three.

### 2.1.1 Implementation A ŌĆö The `match...case` Statement (Python 3.10+)

This is the **official, native switch-case** in modern Python. It uses the keywords `match` and `case`.

**Syntax Skeleton:**

```python
match expression:
    case pattern_1:
        # block executed if expression matches pattern_1
    case pattern_2:
        # block executed if expression matches pattern_2
    case _:                # wildcard default (must be LAST)
        # default block
```

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. The expression immediately after `match` is **evaluated exactly one time**. The result is stored in an implicit, hidden buffer.
2. Python then scans the `case` blocks **top-to-bottom**.
3. The **first** `case` whose pattern matches the buffered value triggers its block.
4. Once a block executes, control jumps to the statement **after** the entire `match` block (no implicit fall-through).
5. If **no case matches**, the wildcard `case _:` is executed. If even the wildcard is absent, the match block silently does nothing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Fall-Through:** Unlike C/Java `switch`, Python's `match` does **NOT** fall through to the next case automatically. Each case is independent.

### 2.1.2 Implementation B ŌĆö Dictionary Dispatch

A pre-built dictionary maps keys ŌåÆ functions or values. Selection is `O(1)` average time (hash lookup).

**Syntax Skeleton:**

```python
switch = {
    key_1: action_1,
    key_2: action_2,
    key_3: action_3,
}
result = switch.get(input_key, default_action)()
```

### 2.1.3 Implementation C ŌĆö `if...elif...else` Chain

The classical fallback. Each condition is **re-evaluated** sequentially until one is true. Time complexity is `O(n)` in the worst case.

## 2.2 Pattern Matching Capabilities of `match...case`

Unlike the primitive equality check of C's `switch`, Python's `match` supports **structural patterns**:

| Pattern Type | Syntax Example | Matches When |
|---|---|---|
| **Literal (OR-pattern)** | `case "adam" \| "sgd":` | Expression equals `"adam"` **OR** `"sgd"` |
| **Capture** | `case x:` | Anything; binds the value to name `x` |
| **Wildcard** | `case _:` | Anything (does **not** bind) |
| **Sequence** | `case [1, 2, rest]:` | A list starting with `1, 2` |
| **Mapping** | `case {"name": n}:` | A dict with key `"name"` |
| **Class** | `case Point(x=0, y=0):` | An instance with those attributes |
| **Guard** | `case x if x > 10:` | Captures `x` AND satisfies the `if` condition |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Rule / Formula | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| `match` evaluation cost | **1** comparison per case (best case, first match) | **O(1)** to **O(n)** |
| `if-elif` evaluation cost | Up to **n** comparisons in worst case | **O(n)** |
| Dictionary dispatch lookup | Hash table probe | **O(1)** average, **O(n)** worst |
| Number of cases recommended | Ōēż **10** for readability | ŌĆö |
| Default case requirement | Optional, but **strongly recommended** | ŌĆö |
| Fall-through behavior | **Not supported** in Python's `match` | ŌĆö |
| Required Python version for native `match` | **Ōēź 3.10** | ŌĆö |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compilers:** Lexical analyzers dispatch token types (`case IDENTIFIER`, `case NUMBER`, `case KEYWORD`).
- **Embedded ML firmware:** TensorFlow Lite Micro uses `switch` to dispatch operator kernels on microcontrollers.
- **Web frameworks (Django/Flask):** URL routing internally uses dictionary dispatch.
- **Reinforcement Learning:** Agent's discrete action space (`0, 1, 2, 3`) is naturally mapped through a switch-case to behavioral policies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 ŌĆö ML Optimizer Selector Using `match...case`

> **Problem:** Given an optimizer name string, print its learning-rate default and update rule. Handle unknown names with a default message.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the dispatch key.**
The key is a string: `"adam"`, `"sgd"`, `"rmsprop"`, `"adagrad"`.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Write the `match` skeleton.**

```python
optimizer = "adam"

match optimizer:
    case "adam":
        print("Adam: lr=0.001, uses adaptive moments.")
    case "sgd":
        print("SGD: lr=0.01, uses raw gradient.")
    case "rmsprop":
        print("RMSprop: lr=0.001, uses moving avg of squared gradient.")
    case "adagrad":
        print("Adagrad: lr=0.01, adapts lr per parameter.")
    case _:
        print(f"Unknown optimizer: {optimizer}")
```

**Step 3 ŌĆö Trace execution for `optimizer = "adam"`.**
- Expression `"adam"` is evaluated **once** ŌåÆ stored.
- `case "adam":` ŌåÆ string equality ŌåÆ **MATCH**.
- Prints `"Adam: lr=0.001, uses adaptive moments."`.
- Skips all remaining cases.
- Default `case _:` is **not** executed.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Trace for an unknown key, e.g., `"nadam"`.**
- Expression `"nadam"` evaluated ŌåÆ stored.
- `case "adam":` ŌåÆ no match.
- `case "sgd":` ŌåÆ no match.
- `case "rmsprop":` ŌåÆ no match.
- `case "adagrad":` ŌåÆ no match.
- `case _:` ŌåÆ wildcard match ŌåÆ prints fallback message.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 ŌĆö Using OR-Patterns

```python
activation = "relu"

match activation:
    case "relu" | "leaky_relu" | "prelu":
        print("Rectified family ŌĆö non-saturating for positive inputs.")
    case "sigmoid" | "tanh":
        print("Saturating family ŌĆö bounded output.")
    case "softmax":
        print("Used in multi-class output layers.")
    case _:
        print("Unknown activation function.")
```

**Step-by-step logical trace:**

1. `activation = "relu"` is evaluated.
2. `case "relu" | "leaky_relu" | "prelu":` ŌĆö Python checks `"relu" == "relu"` ŌåÆ **True** ŌåÆ match succeeds.
3. Block prints the rectified-family message.
4. All later cases are skipped.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `|` symbol in patterns is the OR-pattern operator, not bitwise OR.** It was introduced specifically for `match...case` and is invalid in regular expressions.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 ŌĆö Dictionary Dispatch Equivalent

The above match-block is **logically equivalent** to:

```python
def adam():    return "Adam: lr=0.001, adaptive moments."
def sgd():     return "SGD: lr=0.01, raw gradient."
def rmsprop(): return "RMSprop: lr=0.001, moving avg squared grad."
def default(): return "Unknown optimizer."

optimizer_table = {
    "adam":    adam,
    "sgd":     sgd,
    "rmsprop": rmsprop,
}

print(optimizer_table.get(optimizer, default)())
```

**Step-by-step logical equivalence derivation:**

$$\text{match } e \text{ \{ case } v_i \text{: } B_i \text{ \}} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; D.get(e, D_{default})()$$

where $D$ is a dictionary, $v_i$ are case values, $B_i$ are case bodies, and $D_{default}$ is the default handler.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 ŌĆö Pattern Matching with Capture and Guard

```python
data_point = 42

match data_point:
    case 0:
        print("Zero ŌĆö check for missing value.")
    case x if x < 0:
        print(f"Negative value: {x} ŌĆö possible sensor error.")
    case x if x > 1000:
        print(f"Outlier detected: {x} ŌĆö flag for review.")
    case x:
        print(f"Normal data point: {x}.")
```

**Trace for `data_point = 42`:**

1. `case 0:` ŌåÆ $42 \neq 0$ ŌåÆ skip.
2. `case x if x < 0:` ŌåÆ $42 \not< 0$ ŌåÆ skip.
3. `case x if x > 1000:` ŌåÆ $42 \not> 1000$ ŌåÆ skip.
4. `case x:` ŌåÆ wildcard capture ŌåÆ match. Prints `"Normal data point: 42."`

> [!TIP]
> **Capture `x` binds the value** so it can be referenced inside the block. This is impossible in C/Java `switch`.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Criterion | `match...case` (3.10+) | Dictionary Dispatch | `if...elif...else` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Python version needed | Ōēź 3.10 | Any (2.7+) | Any |
| Readability for many cases | Excellent | Excellent | Poor (long ladder) |
| Supports complex patterns | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | ŌØī No |
| Supports guards | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No | Ō£ģ Yes (via `and`) |
| Allows code per case | Ō£ģ Multi-line | Function call only | Ō£ģ Multi-line |
| Performance (many cases) | Fast | **Fastest** | Slow |
| Fall-through | ŌØī Not supported | N/A | N/A |
| Default handling | `case _:` | `.get(k, default)` | `else:` |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart ŌĆö Execution Logic of `match...case`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Program reaches match statement] --> B[Evaluate the expression after match exactly once]
    B --> C[Store the result in an implicit match buffer]
    C --> D{Compare with case 1 pattern}
    D -- Matches --> E1[Execute case 1 block]
    D -- No match --> F{Compare with case 2 pattern}
    F -- Matches --> E2[Execute case 2 block]
    F -- No match --> G{Compare with case N pattern}
    G -- Matches --> EN[Execute case N block]
    G -- No match --> H{Is there a case _ wildcard}
    H -- Yes --> I[Execute the default block]
    H -- No --> J[Skip entire match block silently]
    E1 --> K[Exit match construct]
    E2 --> K
    EN --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Continue with next statement after match]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö Dictionary Dispatch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input Key e.g. optimizer name] --> HashFunc[Hash Function]
    HashFunc --> Bucket[Hash Table Bucket Lookup]
    Bucket --> Lookup{Dictionary contains key}
    Lookup -- Yes --> Value[Retrieve mapped function or value]
    Lookup -- No --> Default[Invoke default handler]
    Value --> Call[Call function or return value]
    Default --> Call
    Call --> Output[Result returned to caller]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram ŌĆö Switch-Case in an ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Config[Configuration Input e.g. config file or CLI arg] --> Parse[Parse the dispatcher key string]
    Parse --> Match[match key block]
    Match --> C1[case cnn: instantiate CNN model]
    Match --> C2[case rnn: instantiate RNN model]
    Match --> C3[case transformer: instantiate Transformer model]
    Match --> CD[case _: raise ValueError unknown architecture]
    C1 --> Train[Train the selected model]
    C2 --> Train
    C3 --> Train
    CD --> Stop[Halt with error message]
    Train --> Eval[Evaluate on test set]
    Eval --> Done[Return metrics]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a switch-case statement? Explain why Python did not have a native switch-case until version 3.10.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *switch-case* statement is a multi-way branching control structure that evaluates an expression and dispatches execution to one of several blocks based on value equality. Python deliberately omitted a `switch` keyword in its early design because (a) the `if-elif-else` chain was considered sufficient and readable for most cases, and (b) Guido van Rossum preferred dictionary-based dispatch. With **PEP 634** (Python 3.10), the language introduced the `match...case` construct with full **structural pattern matching** ŌĆö far more powerful than the classical C-style switch. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three differences between Python's `match...case` and C language's `switch`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):**

1. **Pattern matching vs equality only** ŌĆö Python's `match` supports sequence, mapping, class, and capture patterns; C's `switch` only matches integer constants.
2. **No fall-through** ŌĆö Python cases do not automatically fall through to the next case; C requires an explicit `break`.
3. **Guards allowed** ŌĆö Python cases may include a conditional `if` guard (e.g., `case x if x > 0:`); C has no equivalent.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### Question A ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the syntax of the `match...case` statement in Python with a suitable example demonstrating **literal patterns**, **OR-patterns**, and the **wildcard default** case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement a Python program using `match...case` that takes a string variable `dataset_type` (which can be `"csv"`, `"json"`, `"parquet"`, or `"xml"`) and prints the appropriate loader function to use in a machine-learning pipeline. Include a wildcard default. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Syntax (3 marks):**

```python
match subject_expression:
    case pattern_1:
        # block 1
    case pattern_2 | pattern_3:
        # block 2 ŌĆö OR-pattern
    case _:
        # default block
```

**Explanation of components (2 marks):**

- `subject_expression` is evaluated **exactly once**.
- Each `case` provides a *pattern*. Python tries them top-to-bottom.
- The `|` operator between patterns means logical OR.
- `case _` is the wildcard; it matches anything but does not bind.
- No case falls through to the next.

**Example (2 marks):**

```python
fruit = "apple"

match fruit:
    case "banana":
        print("Yellow, curved.")
    case "apple" | "pear":
        print("Round and crunchy.")
    case _:
        print("Unknown fruit.")
```

**Output:** `Round and crunchy.`

**Trace:** `"apple" == "apple"` is True (second case), block executes, all later cases skipped.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

```python
def load_csv():    return "Using pandas.read_csv()"
def load_json():   return "Using pandas.read_json()"
def load_parquet():return "Using pandas.read_parquet()"
def load_xml():    return "Using pandas.read_xml()"
def load_default():return "Error: unsupported dataset format."

dataset_type = "parquet"   # could come from CLI or config

match dataset_type:
    case "csv":
        result = load_csv()
    case "json":
        result = load_json()
    case "parquet":
        result = load_parquet()
    case "xml":
        result = load_xml()
    case _:
        result = load_default()

print(result)
```

**Output:** `Using pandas.read_parquet()`

**Explanation of marks distribution (7 marks):**

- Correct `match` header and expression: **1 mark**
- All four named cases correctly written: **3 marks** (Ōēł 0.75 each)
- Wildcard default case: **1 mark**
- Clean print/output and correct logic trace: **2 marks**

---

### Question B ŌĆö `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how dictionary-based dispatch can be used to simulate switch-case in Python. Compare its time complexity with `if-elif-else`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program that uses a dictionary to map optimizer names (`"adam"`, `"sgd"`, `"rmsprop"`) to lambda functions that return a default learning rate. Demonstrate invocation and a default handler for unknown keys. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+-------------------+      hash(key)      +-------------------+
|  Input key        | ------------------> | Hash bucket index |
| e.g. "adam"       |                     |   (O(1) lookup)   |
+-------------------+                     +-------------------+
                                                       |
                                                       v
                                          +-------------------------+
                                          |  Dictionary[optimizer]  |
                                          |  -> function reference  |
                                          +-------------------------+
                                                       |
                                                       v
                                          +-------------------------+
                                          |  Call the function      |
                                          |  and return result      |
                                          +-------------------------+
```

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Dictionary Dispatch | `if-elif-else` |
|---|---|---|
| Average time complexity | **O(1)** | **O(n)** worst case |
| Re-evaluation of key | No (single hash) | Yes, each `if` re-checks |
| Readability for Ōēź 5 cases | Excellent | Poor (long ladder) |
| Default handling | `.get(key, default)` | `else:` block |
| Memory overhead | Stores function objects | None extra |

**Verdict:** Dictionary dispatch is faster for large dispatch tables; `if-elif` is fine for 2ŌĆō3 conditions.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

```python
optimizer_lr = {
    "adam":    lambda: 0.001,
    "sgd":     lambda: 0.01,
    "rmsprop": lambda: 0.001,
}

def default_lr():
    print("Unknown optimizer ŌĆö falling back to lr=0.0001")
    return 0.0001

chosen = "sgd"
lr = optimizer_lr.get(chosen, default_lr)()
print(f"Selected optimizer: {chosen} -> learning rate = {lr}")

# Test unknown
chosen = "nadam"
lr = optimizer_lr.get(chosen, default_lr)()
print(f"Selected optimizer: {chosen} -> learning rate = {lr}")
```

**Output:**
```
Selected optimizer: sgd -> learning rate = 0.01
Unknown optimizer ŌĆö falling back to lr=0.0001
Selected optimizer: nadam -> learning rate = 0.0001
```

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**

- Correct dictionary creation with three lambdas: **2 marks**
- Correct default handler function: **1 mark**
- Correct use of `.get(key, default)()` pattern: **2 marks**
- Two test runs shown (known + unknown key): **2 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using `match` without Python 3.10+** ŌĆö code will throw `SyntaxError` on older versions. Always mention the version.
> 2. **Forgetting the colon `:`** after `match` and after each `case pattern` ŌĆö most common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Placing `case _:` before other cases** ŌĆö it must be the **last** case, otherwise it shadows all earlier cases.
> 4. **Using bitwise OR `|` instead of pattern OR `|`** ŌĆö confusing `|` with `||`. They mean different things inside `match`.
> 5. **Confusing `match` with C's switch** ŌĆö claiming that Python supports fall-through is a guaranteed 1-mark cut.
> 6. **Writing `case "a" or "b":`** ŌĆö WRONG. Use `case "a" | "b":`. The keyword `or` is not allowed in patterns.
> 7. **Forgetting `()` after lambda in dictionary dispatch** ŌĆö `optimizer_lr[key]` returns the lambda object, not its result; you must call it with `()`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Switch-Case in Python (KTU 2024):**

- Ō£ģ Python's **native switch-case** is `match...case`, introduced in **Python 3.10** (PEP 634).
- Ō£ģ **Three idiomatic implementations** exist: `match...case`, **dictionary dispatch**, and `if-elif-else`.
- Ō£ģ `match` evaluates its subject expression **exactly once** and tries cases **top-to-bottom**.
- Ō£ģ Python's `match` has **no fall-through** ŌĆö each case is independent.
- Ō£ģ The wildcard default is `case _:` and **must be the last case**.
- Ō£ģ Use `|` for **OR-patterns**: `case "a" | "b":` (do NOT use `or`).
- Ō£ģ Use `case x if condition:` for **guarded patterns**.
- Ō£ģ `match` supports **structural patterns**: sequences, mappings, classes, captures ŌĆö far richer than C's `switch`.
- Ō£ģ For older Python (<3.10), use **dictionary dispatch** for O(1) selection.
- Ō£ģ Always include a **default/wildcard handler** to gracefully manage unknown inputs ŌĆö critical in ML pipelines.
- Ō£ģ Connect switch-case examples to **ML use-cases** (optimizers, activations, data loaders) to score higher in KTU theory answers.
- Ō£ģ Time complexity: `match` Ōēł **O(1) best / O(n) worst**, dictionary = **O(1) avg**, `if-elif` = **O(n) worst**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Iteration with For/While Loop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Iteration with For/While Loop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Iteration** in Python is a control flow mechanism that enables the **repeated execution** of a block of statements as long as a specified condition remains satisfied or for a predetermined sequence of elements. Python provides two principal iteration constructs: the **`for` loop** (definite / count-controlled iteration) and the **`while` loop** (indefinite / condition-controlled iteration), both conforming to the **indentation-based block scoping** rules of the Python language specification (PEP 8).

| Construct | Category | Termination Basis |
|---|---|---|
| `for` loop | Definite iteration | Sequence exhaustion / range bound |
| `while` loop | Indefinite iteration | Boolean condition becomes `False` |
| `break` | Transfer statement | Immediate loop exit |
| `continue` | Transfer statement | Skip to next iteration |
| `pass` | Null statement | No-operation placeholder |
| `else` clause | Loop-suite clause | Runs only if loop terminates normally |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy ŌĆö "The Pedometer vs. The Thermostat":**
>
> Imagine you are **counting your daily steps**. You know exactly that you want to walk **10,000 steps today**. Once the counter reaches that number, you stop. This is a **FOR LOOP** ŌĆö a *definite* iteration where the number of repetitions is known upfront (a fixed sequence / range).
>
> Now imagine a **room thermostat**. It keeps checking the temperature **as long as the room is too hot**. The moment the room cools down to the setpoint, the loop stops. You don't know in advance how many times it will check ŌĆö it depends on conditions that change over time. This is a **WHILE LOOP** ŌĆö an *indefinite* iteration driven by a dynamic condition.

---

## 1.3 General Syntax Skeleton

```python
# ---------- FOR LOOP (definite iteration) ----------
for <variable> in <iterable>:
    <statement_block>          # repeated once per element
else:                          # optional, runs if NOT broken
    <post_loop_block>

# ---------- WHILE LOOP (indefinite iteration) ----------
while <condition>:
    <statement_block>
else:                          # optional
    <post_loop_block>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the indentation matters:** Python does **not** use `{}` braces like C/C++/Java. A colon `:` introduces the suite, and a **uniform 4-space indentation** defines the block. This is enforced by **PEP 8** ŌĆö the official Python style guide referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 1.4 The `range()` Function ŌĆö The Engine of the `for` Loop

The `range()` function generates an **immutable sequence of integers** on demand (lazy evaluation, memory-efficient).

$$\text{range}(start, stop, step)$$

| Form | Expression | Generated Sequence | Stop Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-arg | `range(stop)` | `0, 1, 2, ..., stop-1` | `stop - 1` |
| Two-arg | `range(start, stop)` | `start, start+1, ..., stop-1` | `stop - 1` |
| Three-arg | `range(start, stop, step)` | `start, start+step, ...` | Last `< stop` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`range()` is EXCLUSIVE of the stop value** (half-open interval `[start, stop)`). This is mathematically consistent with Python's zero-indexed slicing convention. Failing to remember this is the **#1 source of off-by-one errors** in KTU lab examinations.

---

## 1.5 Loop Control Transfer Statements

> [!NOTE]
> These three statements alter the **normal sequential flow** of loop execution:

- **`break`** ŌĆö Terminates the loop immediately and transfers control to the statement immediately after the loop.
- **`continue`** ŌĆö Skips the remainder of the current iteration and proceeds to the next iteration.
- **`pass`** ŌĆö A syntactic no-op (does nothing). Used as a placeholder to maintain valid block structure during development.

---

## 1.6 Application in Machine Learning Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Iteration is the **backbone of every ML training algorithm**:
> - **Epoch loops** in neural networks iterate over the dataset multiple times.
> - **Gradient descent** uses `while` loops with convergence tolerance.
> - **Mini-batch training** uses `for` loops over `DataLoader` batches.
> - **Cross-validation** uses nested `for` loops over fold indices.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The `for` Loop ŌĆö Operational Mechanics

The `for` loop performs **iterator protocol-driven traversal**. Python internally invokes `iter()` on the iterable to obtain an iterator, then repeatedly calls `next()` on it until `StopIteration` is raised.

### Logic Decomposition

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Acquisition:** Python obtains an iterator object from the iterable via `iter(iterable)`.
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Termination Check:** Python attempts `next(iterator)`. If `StopIteration` is raised, the loop terminates (and `else` clause executes if present).
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Binding:** The retrieved value is bound to the loop variable in the local namespace.
- **Step 4 ŌĆö Execution:** The indented suite is executed with the loop variable bound.
- **Step 5 ŌĆö Repetition:** Control returns to Step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> The loop variable **persists** in the enclosing scope after the loop ends, retaining the value of the **last iteration**. This is a frequent pitfall in comprehension-based questions.

### Iteration over Common Data Structures

```python
# Iterating over a string (sequence of characters)
for ch in "ML":
    print(ch)                  # Output: M, L (on separate lines)

# Iterating over a list
for item in [10, 20, 30]:
    print(item)

# Iterating over a dictionary (yields keys by default)
for key in {"a": 1, "b": 2}:
    print(key)                 # Output: a, b

# Iterating over key-value pairs
for k, v in {"a": 1, "b": 2}.items():
    print(k, v)

# Iterating with index using enumerate()
for idx, val in enumerate(["x", "y", "z"]):
    print(idx, val)            # Output: 0 x, 1 y, 2 z
```

---

## 2.2 The `while` Loop ŌĆö Operational Mechanics

The `while` loop is a **pre-test conditional loop**. The condition is evaluated **before each iteration**; if `True`, the suite executes, then control returns to re-evaluate the condition.

### Logic Decomposition

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Condition Evaluation:** Evaluate the boolean expression. If `False`, terminate (and `else` runs).
- **Step 2 ŌĆö Suite Execution:** Run the indented block.
- **Step 3 ŌĆö Update:** The programmer **must** ensure the loop variable is modified within the suite; otherwise an **infinite loop** results.

> [!WARNING]
> An incorrectly written `while` loop is the **most common runtime error** in KTU practical exams. Always ensure the loop variable is mutated inside the suite.

### Standard `while` Patterns

```python
# Countdown pattern
n = 5
while n > 0:
    print(n)
    n -= 1
# Output: 5, 4, 3, 2, 1

# Sentinel-controlled pattern (e.g., reading until EOF or 'quit')
user_input = ""
while user_input != "quit":
    user_input = input("Enter command: ").strip()

# Flag-controlled pattern
running = True
while running:
    data = get_data()
    if data is None:
        running = False
```

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

| # | Construct | Syntax | Key Property | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `for` loop | `for x in iterable:` | Definite iteration | Traversing sequences |
| 2 | `while` loop | `while condition:` | Indefinite iteration | Condition-driven repetition |
| 3 | `range()` | `range(start, stop, step)` | Half-open `[start, stop)` | Generating index sequences |
| 4 | `break` | `break` | Exits innermost loop | Early termination |
| 5 | `continue` | `continue` | Skips to next iteration | Filtering in loop |
| 6 | `pass` | `pass` | Null operation | Placeholder for empty blocks |
| 7 | `else` clause | `else:` after loop | Runs only on normal exit | Search success flag |
| 8 | `enumerate()` | `enumerate(iter, start=0)` | Yields `(index, value)` | Indexed traversal |
| 9 | `zip()` | `zip(a, b)` | Parallel iteration | Combining iterables |
| 10 | Nested loop | `for ... for ...` | Inner loop completes per outer iteration | 2D structures, ML epochs |

### Mathematical Properties of `range()`

$$\text{Length of range}(s, e, k) = \max\left(0,\ \left\lceil \frac{e - s}{k} \right\rceil\right) \quad \text{when } k > 0$$

> **Note:** Python uses integer arithmetic. `len(range(0, 10, 2)) == 5`, because the sequence is `{0, 2, 4, 6, 8}` ŌĆö exactly 5 elements.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & ML

| Domain | Application | Loop Type |
|---|---|---|
| Neural Networks | Epoch/batch training loops | `for` |
| Optimization | Iterative solvers (gradient descent) | `while` (with tolerance) |
| Image Processing | Pixel-by-pixel filtering (nested) | `for` (nested) |
| Signal Processing | Time-series sliding window | `for` with `enumerate` |
| Numerical Methods | Newton-Raphson root finding | `while` (convergence check) |
| Data Preprocessing | Iterating CSV rows | `for` |

---

## 2.5 The `else` Clause ŌĆö A Unique Python Feature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Python allows an optional `else` clause attached to a loop. It executes **only if the loop terminates normally** (i.e., the condition becomes `False` or the iterable is exhausted) ŌĆö **NOT** when terminated by `break`.

```python
# Search using for-else
target = 7
for num in [1, 3, 5, 9]:
    if num == target:
        print("Found")
        break
else:
    print("Not Found")         # Executes because target was never matched
```

**Output:** `Not Found`

This is a powerful pattern for implementing **clean search logic without flag variables** ŌĆö a frequent KTU exam topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace ŌĆö `for` Loop with `range()`

### Problem
Predict the output of the following snippet, justifying each iteration:

```python
for i in range(2, 13, 3):
    print(i, end=' ')
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

We are evaluating `range(2, 13, 3)`. By definition:

$$\text{Sequence} = \{i \in \mathbb{Z} \mid 2 \leq i < 13,\ i \equiv 2 \pmod{3}\}$$

**Step 1:** Start at `i = 2`. Is `2 < 13`? **Yes.** Print `2`.

**Step 2:** Add step `3`: `i = 2 + 3 = 5`. Is `5 < 13`? **Yes.** Print `5`.

**Step 3:** Add step `3`: `i = 5 + 3 = 8`. Is `8 < 13`? **Yes.** Print `8`.

**Step 4:** Add step `3`: `i = 8 + 3 = 11`. Is `11 < 13`? **Yes.** Print `11`.

**Step 5:** Add step `3`: `i = 11 + 3 = 14`. Is `14 < 13`? **No.** Loop terminates.

**Final Output:** `2 5 8 11`

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\vert\{2, 5, 8, 11\}\vert = 4$. Using the formula $\lceil (13-2)/3 \rceil = \lceil 11/3 \rceil = 4$. Ō£ō

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace ŌĆö `while` Loop with Conditional Logic

### Problem
Trace the output of the following code:

```python
n = 1
s = 0
while n <= 10:
    if n % 2 == 0:
        s += n
    n += 1
print(s)
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

| Iteration | `n` (start) | `n <= 10` | `n % 2 == 0` | `s += n` | `n += 1` (end) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | True | False | s = 0 | n = 2 |
| 2 | 2 | True | True | s = 2 | n = 3 |
| 3 | 3 | True | False | s = 2 | n = 4 |
| 4 | 4 | True | True | s = 6 | n = 5 |
| 5 | 5 | True | False | s = 6 | n = 6 |
| 6 | 6 | True | True | s = 12 | n = 7 |
| 7 | 7 | True | False | s = 12 | n = 8 |
| 8 | 8 | True | True | s = 20 | n = 9 |
| 9 | 9 | True | False | s = 20 | n = 10 |
| 10 | 10 | True | True | s = 30 | n = 11 |
| 11 | 11 | False | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | exit loop |

**Final Output:** `30`

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$S = \sum_{n=1}^{10} \{n : n \equiv 0 \pmod 2\} = 2 + 4 + 6 + 8 + 10 = 30 \quad \checkmark$$

This can be expressed via the formula for the sum of the first $k$ even numbers:

$$S = k(k+1), \quad k = 5 \implies S = 5 \times 6 = 30$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Trace ŌĆö Nested Loops with `break` & `continue`

### Problem
Determine the output:

```python
for i in range(1, 4):
    for j in range(1, 4):
        if i * j == 4:
            break
        if (i + j) % 2 == 0:
            continue
        print(f"{i}{j}", end=' ')
    print()
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

We construct the full iteration grid:

| `(i, j)` | `i * j` | `i*j == 4` (break?) | `(i+j) % 2 == 0` (continue?) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (1,1) | 1 | No | `2 % 2 == 0` ŌåÆ True | **continue** (skip print) |
| (1,2) | 2 | No | `3 % 2 == 1` ŌåÆ False | Print `12` |
| (1,3) | 3 | No | `4 % 2 == 0` ŌåÆ True | **continue** (skip print) |
| (2,1) | 2 | No | `3 % 2 == 1` ŌåÆ False | Print `21` |
| (2,2) | 4 | **Yes** | ŌĆö | **break** inner loop |
| (3,1) | 3 | No | `4 % 2 == 0` ŌåÆ True | **continue** (skip print) |
| (3,2) | 6 | No | `5 % 2 == 1` ŌåÆ False | Print `32` |
| (3,3) | 9 | No | `6 % 2 == 0` ŌåÆ True | **continue** (skip print) |

**Output:**
```
12 
21 
32 
```

> [!NOTE]
> The outer `print()` after each inner loop produces a **newline**, hence the spaced-out appearance. The `break` exits only the **innermost** loop.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Trace ŌĆö `for-else` Search Pattern

### Problem
Analyze:

```python
nums = [4, 8, 15, 16, 23, 42]
target = 10
for n in nums:
    if n == target:
        print("Found")
        break
else:
    print("Not Found")
```

### Analytical Walkthrough

**Step 1:** Iter 1: `n = 4`. `4 == 10`? No. Continue.
**Step 2:** Iter 2: `n = 8`. `8 == 10`? No. Continue.
**Step 3:** Iter 3: `n = 15`. `15 == 10`? No. Continue.
**Step 4:** Iter 4: `n = 16`. `16 == 10`? No. Continue.
**Step 5:** Iter 5: `n = 23`. `23 == 10`? No. Continue.
**Step 6:** Iter 6: `n = 42`. `42 == 10`? No. Continue.
**Step 7:** Iterator exhausted ŌåÆ no `break` triggered ŌåÆ `else` clause executes.

**Output:** `Not Found`

---

## 3.5 Comparative Complexity Analysis

| Loop Type | Time Complexity (avg) | Space Complexity | Termination Guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|
| `for x in range(n)` | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Guaranteed (iterator exhaustion) |
| `while condition` | Depends on condition | $O(1)$ | NOT guaranteed (risk of infinite loop) |
| Nested `for` (m ├Ś n) | $O(mn)$ | $O(1)$ | Guaranteed |
| `while True: if X: break` | Depends on `X` | $O(1)$ | Guaranteed if break reachable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For ML contexts**, `for` loops over `range(epochs)` or `range(len(dataset))` are preferred over `while` because the training schedule is **deterministic** ŌĆö the number of epochs is fixed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
