A **lambda function** is a small **anonymous** (unnamed) function defined using the `lambda` keyword. It can have any number of arguments but only **one expression** (no statements, no assignments).

**Syntax:**
```text
lambda arguments: expression
```

**Example ŌĆö sort list of tuples by second element:**

```python
students = [("Anu", 88), ("Bala", 75), ("Cyril", 92), ("Diya", 80)]
sorted_students = sorted(students, key=lambda t: t[1])
print(sorted_students)
# [('Bala', 75), ('Diya', 80), ('Anu', 88), ('Cyril', 92)]
```

**Limitations of lambda:**
1. ŌØī Cannot contain **statements** (e.g., `if` blocks, `for` loops, `print()` for side-effects, though `print` itself is allowed inside expression context).
2. ŌØī Cannot contain **assignments** (e.g., `x = 5`).
3. ŌØī Cannot have **docstrings**.
4. ŌØī Limited to a **single expression** ŌĆö poor readability for complex logic.
5. ŌØī Cannot be **decorated** with `@staticmethod`/`@classmethod`.

**(b) LEGB rule with diagram and example (7 marks):**

The **LEGB rule** dictates the order in which Python searches for a variable name when it is referenced inside a function:

1. **L**ocal ŌĆö inside the current function.
2. **E**nclosing ŌĆö in any enclosing (outer) function for nested functions.
3. **G**lobal ŌĆö at the module's top level.
4. **B**uilt-in ŌĆö names pre-defined in `builtins` (e.g., `print`, `len`, `range`).

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    L["L ŌĆö Local scope (innermost function)"] --> E["E ŌĆö Enclosing scope (outer function)"]
    E --> G["G ŌĆö Global scope (module level)"]
    G --> B["B ŌĆö Built-in scope (predefined names)"]
```

**Example demonstrating LEGB:**

```python
x = "global x"          # Global

def outer():
    x = "enclosing x"   # Enclosing

    def inner():
        x = "local x"   # Local
        print(x)        # Resolves to "local x"

    inner()
    print(x)            # Resolves to "enclosing x"

outer()
print(x)                # Resolves to "global x"
```

**Output:**
```text
local x
enclosing x
global x
```

**Mathematical model:** If $L, E, G, B$ are sets of names, lookup is:
$$
\text{resolve}(x) = \begin{cases} x \in L & \Rightarrow L(x) \\ x \in E & \Rightarrow E(x) \\ x \in G & \Rightarrow G(x) \\ x \in B & \Rightarrow B(x) \\ \text{otherwise} & \Rightarrow \text{NameError} \end{cases}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to import `reduce`** ŌĆö `reduce` is in `functools`, *not* built-in like `map`/`filter`. Writing `reduce(...)` without `from functools import reduce` causes `NameError` ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing `*args` AFTER keyword arguments in the call site** ŌĆö `f(1, b=2, 3)` is a `SyntaxError`. Always: positional first, then keyword. ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing `return` and `print`** ŌĆö methods must *return* values; `print` only displays. If the question asks "return the result" and you `print` it, **ŌłÆ2 marks**.
> 4. **In lambda, using `if-else` as a statement** ŌĆö students often write `lambda x: if x>0: x else -x` which is a **syntax error**. Correct form: `lambda x: x if x > 0 else -x`. ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram for the LEGB question** ŌĆö Part (b) explicitly asks for a "neat diagram" ŌåÆ **ŌłÆ2 marks** if omitted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (save this for the night before the exam!)**

- ŌŁÉ **Function vs Method** ŌĆö function lives at module level; method lives inside a class and takes `self`.
- ŌŁÉ **Argument Order (in call):** `positional ŌåÆ keyword ŌåÆ *iterable ŌåÆ **dict`. In signature: `def f(positional, default=val, *args, **kwargs)`.
- ŌŁÉ **Default arguments are evaluated ONCE** at function-definition time ŌĆö never use mutable defaults like `def f(x=[])`; use `def f(x=None): x = x or []`.
- ŌŁÉ **Return vs Print** ŌĆö `return` sends a value back to the caller; `print` only displays. Methods should `return`.
- ŌŁÉ **Lambda** ŌĆö single expression, anonymous, `lambda args: expr`. Cannot contain statements or assignments.
- ŌŁÉ **`map(func, iter)`** ŌĆö applies `func` to every element ŌåÆ returns a lazy `map` object. Wrap with `list()` to materialize.
- ŌŁÉ **`filter(func, iter)`** ŌĆö keeps only elements where `func(elem)` is truthy. Returns a lazy `filter` object.
- ŌŁÉ **`reduce(func, iter)`** ŌĆö fold-left cumulative aggregation. Lives in `functools`. Returns a single value.
- ŌŁÉ **LEGB Rule** ŌĆö Local ŌåÆ Enclosing ŌåÆ Global ŌåÆ Built-in. Use `global` and `nonlocal` keywords to *modify* outer-scope variables.
- ŌŁÉ **`@staticmethod` vs `@classmethod`** ŌĆö static gets no implicit first arg; classmethod gets `cls`. Both are accessible via class name *or* instance.
- ŌŁÉ **`*args` ŌåÆ tuple**, `**kwargs` ŌåÆ dict. Use them when argument count is dynamic (e.g., hyperparameter sweeps).
- ŌŁÉ **Docstrings** ŌĆö triple-quoted string right after `def` line. KTU examiners reward them with **┬ĮŌĆō1 bonus mark**.
- ŌŁÉ **Type Hints** ŌĆö `def f(x: int) -> int:` ŌĆö not mandatory but professional and KTU 2024-aligned.
- ŌŁÉ **Higher-order functions** form the bridge to **ML libraries** ŌĆö `map` Ōåö `DataFrame.apply`, `filter` Ōåö boolean masking, `reduce` Ōåö `cumsum`/gradient accumulation.

> Ō£ģ **One-line mantra for the exam hall:**
> *"Define, decorate, document, default, and deliver ŌĆö that's the 5D rule of writing KTU-grade Python methods."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instance Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Instance Variables # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** under Python, an **Instance Variable** is a data attribute that is: - Declared **inside any instance method** (most commonly the constructor `__init__`), - Prefixed with the mandatory first parameter **`self`**, which binds the variable to the *specific* object being constructed or operated upon, and - Belongs **uniquely to each individual object (instance)** of the class ŌĆö meaning every object gets its **own independent copy** in memory. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Instance variables are attributes whose value is bound to the specific instance of a class. They are introduced via the `self` qualifier inside methods (usually `__init__`) and are accessed using the dot-operator (`object.variable_name`). Each object maintains its own dictionary-backed namespace (`__dict__`) for these variables." ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The University ID Card Imagine a classroom of 60 students. Each student has: - A **personal ID card** (name, roll number, blood group, address) ŌĆö these differ from one student to the next. - A **common classroom** they all share (blackboard, projector, class teacher) ŌĆö these are shared. In this analogy: - **Student** ŌåÆ Object (Instance) of the class `Student` - **Personal ID card fields** ŌåÆ **Instance Variables** (unique per object) - **Classroom / Teacher** ŌåÆ Class Variables (shared, covered later) So when you say `student1.name = "Anand"` and `student2.name = "Priya"`, the `.name` of `student1` and the `.name` of `student2` are **two completely different memory locations**, even though both objects belong to the same class. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Footprint Rule (Board-Exam Favourite):** If a class has `n` instance variables and you create `k` objects, Python allocates `n ├Ś k` separate memory slots in RAM ŌĆö one set per object. This is *why* they are called "instance"-bound. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of an Instance Variable | Pillar | Description | Python Keyword | |---|---|---| | **Declaration** | Done inside a method using `self.` | `self.x = value` | | **Access (read)** | Use object reference or `self` | `obj.x` or `self.x` | | **Access (write/modify)** | Reassign using object or `self` | `obj.x = new_value` | > [!TIP] > The identifier **`self`** is a *convention*, not a keyword. You may name it `this`, `me`, or `apple` ŌĆö but renaming it will cost you KTU board marks. **Always use `self`** in examinations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics ŌĆö Step-by-Step Breakdown When Python executes the statement `obj = MyClass(arg1, arg2)`, the following sequence occurs internally: 1. **Object Allocation:** Python allocates a *new blank object* in the heap memory and assigns the reference `obj` to it. 2. **Class Lookup:** Python identifies the class `MyClass` and locates its `__init__` method. 3. **`self` Binding:** The newly created object is passed as the **first argument** to `__init__` and is locally named `self`. 4. **Variable Attachment:** Every line of the form `self.attribute = value` creates a new keyŌĆōvalue pair inside the object's internal dictionary `obj.__dict__`. 5. **Method Return:** After `__init__` finishes, `self` is automatically discarded; the object is returned, bound to `obj`. 6. **Subsequent Access:** Reading `obj.attribute` triggers Python's attribute-resolution algorithm: - First, it searches the **instance namespace** (`obj.__dict__`). - If not found, it searches the **class namespace** (`MyClass.__dict__`). - If still not found, the built-in `__getattr__` hook is invoked, finally raising `AttributeError`. ## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet ŌĆö Instance Variable Cheat Sheet | # | Rule / Syntax | Meaning / Engineering Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | `self.var = val` | Declares / Initializes instance variable inside a method. | | 2 | `obj.var` | Reads the instance variable of object `obj`. | | 3 | `obj.var = new_val` | Modifies the instance variable **only for that object**. | | 4 | `del obj.var` | Deletes the instance variable from that object alone. | | 5 | `obj.__dict__` | Returns the dictionary holding **all** instance variables of that object. | | 6 | `vars(obj)` | Built-in function equivalent to `obj.__dict__`. | | 7 | `hasattr(obj, 'var')` | Returns `True` if instance variable exists in the object. | | 8 | `setattr(obj, 'var', val)` | Programmatically sets an instance variable. | | 9 | `getattr(obj, 'var')` | Programmatically reads an instance variable. | | 10 | `self._var` | **Protected** convention (single underscore). | | 11 | `self.__var` | **Private** convention (name-mangled by Python to `_ClassName__var`). | | 12 | `self.__var__` | **Dunder / Magic** method (reserved by Python ŌĆö do not invent). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Naming Rule (Python PEP 8):** > - `public_var` ŌåÆ freely accessible > - `_protected_var` ŌåÆ for internal/subclass use only (single underscore) > - `__private_var` ŌåÆ name-mangled; do not touch from outside > - `__magic_var__` ŌåÆ Python's reserved dunder space ŌĆö never create custom ones ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In **Machine Learning pipelines**, every trained model object (e.g., an `sklearn` estimator) carries dozens of instance variables such as: - `model.coef_` ŌĆö learned weight vector - `model.intercept_` ŌĆö bias term - `model.n_features_in_` ŌĆö feature dimensionality - `model.classes_` ŌĆö list of class labels - `model.feature_importances_` ŌĆö tree-based importance scores Each instance holds **its own copy** of these learned parameters ŌĆö that is precisely why fitting two `RandomForestClassifier` objects on different datasets produces two different `.coef_` arrays. This real-world tie-in is a **high-yield KTU viva point**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example #1 ŌĆö Basic Declaration & Initialization ```python class Student: def __init__(self, name, roll, cgpa): # Step 1: Three instance variables are declared and attached to `self` self.name = name self.roll = roll self.cgpa = cgpa def display(self): # Step 2: Reading instance variables via `self` print(f"Name: {self.name}, Roll: {self.roll}, CGPA: {self.cgpa}") # Step 3: Two distinct objects created s1 = Student("Anand", 101, 9.1) s2 = Student("Priya", 102, 8.8) # Step 4: Reading instance variables via object reference print(s1.name) # Output: Anand print(s2.name) # Output: Priya # Step 5: Modifying only s1's cgpa leaves s2 untouched s1.cgpa = 9.5 print(s1.cgpa) # Output: 9.5 print(s2.cgpa) # Output: 8.8 (unchanged) # Step 6: Inspecting the instance namespace print(s1.__dict__) # Output: {'name': 'Anand', 'roll': 101, 'cgpa': 9.5} ``` **Logical derivation of memory layout:** $$\text{Memory}_{\text{s1}} = \{ \text{name} \mapsto \text{"Anand"},\ \text{roll} \mapsto 101,\ \text{cgpa} \mapsto 9.5 \}$$ $$\text{Memory}_{\text{s2}} = \{ \text{name} \mapsto \text{"Priya"},\ \text{roll} \mapsto 102,\ \text{cgpa} \mapsto 8.8 \}$$ The two dictionaries are **disjoint** in RAM. Modifying one does not propagate to the other ŌĆö this is the formal guarantee of *instance-level isolation*. ## 3.2 Worked Example #2 ŌĆö Three Access Modifiers in Action ```python class BankAccount: def __init__(self, holder, balance): self.holder = holder # PUBLIC ŌĆö freely accessible self._bank = "SBI" # PROTECTED ŌĆö single underscore self.__pin = 1234 # PRIVATE ŌĆö name-mangled a = BankAccount("Anand", 5000) print(a.holder) # OK: 'Anand' print(a._bank) # Works but discouraged: 'SBI' # Direct access fails: # print(a.__pin) # AttributeError # But Python's name-mangling reveals it: print(a._BankAccount__pin) # OK: 1234 (proof of mangling) ``` **Logical derivation of name-mangling:** $$\text{self.\_\_pin} \xrightarrow{\text{mangle}} \text{self.\_BankAccount\_\_pin}$$ Python rewrites every identifier starting with `__` (and not ending with `__`) by prefixing the class name and an extra underscore. This is a **soft encapsulation**, not a security feature. ## 3.3 Worked Example #3 ŌĆö Dynamic Addition of Instance Variables Python uniquely allows you to **add new instance variables to an already-existing object**. ```python class Car: def __init__(self, brand): self.brand = brand c1 = Car("Tesla") c2 = Car("BMW") # Add a new instance variable ONLY to c1 c1.color = "Red" print(c1.brand, c1.color) # Tesla Red print(c2.brand) # BMW # print(c2.color) # AttributeError ŌĆö c2 has no `color` ``` **Logical derivation:** $$\text{c1.}\_\text{dict} = \{ \text{brand} \mapsto \text{"Tesla"},\ \text{color} \mapsto \text{"Red"} \}$$ $$\text{c2.}\_\text{dict} = \{ \text{brand} \mapsto \text{"BMW"} \}$$ The asymmetry proves that instance variables are bound **per object**, not per class. ## 3.4 Worked Example #4 ŌĆö ML-Flavoured Application ```python class LinearRegressor: def __init__(self, learning_rate=0.01): self.learning_rate = learning_rate # hyperparameter self.weights = None # to be learned self.bias = None # to be learned self.is_fitted = False # state flag def fit(self, X, y): # Simulate training self.weights = [0.5, -0.3] self.bias = 1.2 self.is_fitted = True m1 = LinearRegressor(learning_rate=0.001) m2 = LinearRegressor(learning_rate=0.1) m1.fit(X_train, y_train) # m1.is_fitted is True; m2.is_fitted is still False ``` **Logical derivation of independence:** $$\text{m1.learning\_rate} = 0.001 \neq \text{m2.learning\_rate} = 0.1$$ $$\text{m1.weights} = [0.5, -0.3],\quad \text{m2.weights} = \text{None}$$ Two models, two isolated parameter sets ŌĆö **this is the cornerstone of multi-model ML experimentation**. ## 3.5 Summary Matrix ŌĆö Allowed vs Disallowed Operations | Operation | Syntax | Allowed? | Effect | |---|---|---|---| | Declare in `__init__` | `self.x = 10` | Ō£ģ | Creates `x` in object | | Declare in any method | `self.x = 10` | Ō£ģ | Creates / overwrites `x` | | Declare in class body (no `self`) | `x = 10` | ŌØī (becomes class var) | Shared by all | | Read via object | `obj.x` | Ō£ģ | Returns value | | Read via `self` inside class | `self.x` | Ō£ģ | Returns value | | Modify via object | `obj.x = 20` | Ō£ģ | Affects only that object | | Delete | `del obj.x` | Ō£ģ | Removes from that object only | | Add dynamically | `obj.new_attr = val` | Ō£ģ | Allowed (Pythonic flexibility) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Object Creation & Instance Variable Attachment ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Python sees: obj = MyClass arg1 arg2] --> B[Allocate empty object in heap memory] B --> C[Bind the new object to self inside __init__] C --> D[Execute self.attr1 = value1] C --> E[Execute self.attr2 = value2] C --> F[Execute self.attr3 = value3] D --> G[obj.__dict__ now contains attr1] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Return obj to the caller's namespace] H --> I[Read/Write via obj.attr1, obj.attr2 ...] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Attribute Resolution Order ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Program asks: obj.attr1] --> Q{Is attr1 in obj.__dict__?} Q -- YES --> R[Return value from instance namespace] Q -- NO --> S{Is attr1 in MyClass.__dict__?} S -- YES --> T[Return value from class namespace] S -- NO --> U{Is there a __getattr__ hook?} U -- YES --> V[Invoke the hook and return result] U -- NO --> W[Raise AttributeError] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Three Access Modifiers ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PUBLIC PUB[public_var
No underscore
Freely accessible] end subgraph PROTECTED PRO[_protected_var
Single underscore
Internal hint] end subgraph PRIVATE PRI[__private_var
Double underscore
Name-mangled] end OUTSIDE[External code] -->|reads| PUB OUTSIDE -.->|discouraged| PRO OUTSIDE -.->|FAILS| PRI PRI -->|mangled to _ClassName__private_var| MANGLED[Accessible only via mangled name] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Memory Layout: Two Objects, Same Class ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HEAP[Heap Memory] O1[Object s1] --> D1["__dict__: {name: 'Anand', roll: 101, cgpa: 9.5}"] O2[Object s2] --> D2["__dict__: {name: 'Priya', roll: 102, cgpa: 8.8}"] O3[Class Student] --> D3["__dict__: methods only __init__, display"] end STACK[Stack: references s1, s2] --> O1 STACK --> O2 HEAP --> O3 ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture ŌĆö Why Each Object Needs Its Own Copy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Single Class Definition] --> B[Object 1 created] A --> C[Object 2 created] A --> D[Object 3 created] B --> B1[Own copy of attr1, attr2, attr3] C --> C1[Own copy of attr1, attr2, attr3] D --> D1[Own copy of attr1, attr2, attr3] B1 --> E[Modify attr1 only here] C1 --> F[Unaffected by change in Object 1] D1 --> G[Unaffected by change in Object 1] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## ­¤ōś PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **Define instance variables in Python. How are they different from local variables declared inside a method?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** Instance variables are attributes declared inside methods using the `self` qualifier (typically in `__init__`); they are bound to a specific object and persist for the lifetime of that object. - **(1 mark)** Local variables are declared inside a method **without** `self`; they are temporary, exist only during method execution, and are destroyed when the method returns. - **(1 mark)** Instance variables are accessed across methods via `self` or via the object reference; local variables cannot be accessed outside the defining method's scope. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Supplementary Paper]** **What is the role of `self` in Python? Can we rename `self` to something else?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** `self` is the first parameter of any instance method; it is a reference to the *current object* on which the method is invoked. It is automatically passed by Python when `obj.method(args)` is called. - **(1 mark)** Through `self`, instance variables and other methods of the same object are accessed inside the class definition. - **(1 mark)** Yes, technically `self` can be renamed (e.g., `this` or `me`), because it is just a conventional name, not a keyword. However, it is **strongly discouraged** and KTU examiners **deduct marks** for deviating from the standard convention. --- ## ­¤ōĢ PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### ­¤öĘ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Main Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **instance variables** in Python with a suitable example. Discuss how they are declared, accessed, and modified. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Demonstrate with a Python program the **three access specifiers** (public, protected, private) for instance variables. What is *name-mangling*? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) [7 marks]** - **(1 mark)** Definition: Instance variables are object-bound attributes prefixed with `self`, unique to each instance. - **(1 mark)** Declaration example inside `__init__`. - **(1 mark)** Access via `self` inside class and via `obj.attr` outside. - **(1 mark)** Modification via `obj.attr = new_val` affects only that object. - **(1 mark)** Each object has its own `__dict__`. - **(1 mark)** Code demonstration. - **(1 mark)** Memory-isolation explanation with two-object example (refer to Section 3.1). **(b) [7 marks]** - **(2 marks)** Code showing `public`, `_protected`, `__private` instance variables (refer to Section 3.2). - **(2 marks)** Demonstrate that `obj.__pin` raises `AttributeError`. - **(2 marks)** Show the mangled name `obj._ClassName__pin` retrieves the value. - **(1 mark)** Definition: Name-mangling is Python's automatic rewriting of identifiers starting with `__` (and not ending with `__`) into `_ClassName__identifier` to simulate private attributes. --- ### ­¤öĘ Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Main Paper]** **(a)** Write a Python class `Employee` with instance variables `name`, `salary`, and `department`. Show that modifying the salary of one employee object does **not** affect another employee object. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain **dynamic addition of instance variables** in Python. Why is this feature considered Pythonic but potentially risky in large codebases? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) [7 marks]** ```python class Employee: def __init__(self, name, salary, department): self.name = name self.salary = salary self.department = department e1 = Employee("Anand", 50000, "ML") e2 = Employee("Priya", 60000, "Data") e1.salary = 70000 # Only e1 is modified print(e1.salary) # 70000 print(e2.salary) # 60000 (proves isolation) ``` - **(3 marks)** Correct class + objects. - **(2 marks)** Modification + verification of isolation. - **(2 marks)** Explanation backed by `__dict__` print. **(b) [7 marks]** - **(2 marks)** Definition: Python allows adding new instance variables to an existing object via `obj.new_attr = value` (refer to Section 3.3). - **(2 marks)** Demonstration that `c1.color = "Red"` adds `color` only to `c1`, not `c2`. - **(2 marks)** Pythonic advantage: flexibility, dynamic prototyping, runtime configuration. - **(1 mark)** Risk: silent `AttributeError`s, harder debugging, breaks uniform object interface, hurts code maintainability in ML pipelines where schemas matter. --- ## ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Writing `var = value` instead of `self.var = value` inside `__init__`** ŌĆö this silently creates a *local* variable, not an instance variable. The board examiner deducts **2 marks** immediately. > 2. **Confusing class variables with instance variables** ŌĆö any variable assigned at the *class level* (outside methods) is a class variable and is **shared**. > 3. **Forgetting the `self` parameter in `__init__`** ŌĆö causes a `TypeError` at runtime. > 4. **Using `self` outside a class** ŌĆö raises `NameError`. > 5. **Inventing custom dunder methods** like `__myvar__` ŌĆö violates Python's reserved-namespace convention. > 6. **Not demonstrating `__dict__`** ŌĆö board examiners specifically look for evidence that the student understands the per-object namespace. --- ## ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Ō£ģ Instance variables are **declared with `self`** inside any instance method (most commonly `__init__`). - Ō£ģ They are **unique per object**; one object cannot influence another's instance variables. - Ō£ģ Access syntax: `self.var` (inside class) and `obj.var` (outside class). - Ō£ģ Modification via `obj.var = new_val` is **object-local**. - Ō£ģ Use `obj.__dict__` to inspect all instance variables of an object. - Ō£ģ Three access conventions: `public`, `_protected`, `__private` (name-mangled). - Ō£ģ Python allows **dynamic addition** of new instance variables to existing objects. - Ō£ģ **Never** declare `var = value` inside `__init__` without `self.` ŌĆö it becomes a *local* variable. - Ō£ģ In ML pipelines, model parameters like `weights`, `bias`, `coef_`, `classes_` are all instance variables ŌĆö distinct per model object. - Ō£ģ Always use **`self`** as the first parameter name in instance methods ŌĆö renaming costs marks. - Ō£ģ Instance variables **persist for the lifetime of the object**; `del obj.var` removes them.

topics.title: Constructor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constructor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Python Object-Oriented Programming (OOP), a **Constructor** is a special method that is automatically invoked at the moment an object (instance) of a class is created. Its primary responsibility is to **initialize** the newly allocated object's attributes (instance variables) to valid, meaningful initial states.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Python Basics for Machine Learning*, the constructor is formally recognized through Python's reserved magic method named:

$$\texttt{\_\_init\_\_(self)}$$

The name `__init__` stands for *initialize*. The double underscores (also called **dunder** ŌĆö short for *double underscore*) on either side signal to the Python interpreter that this is a system-defined, special-purpose method, not a regular user-defined function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEERT526 - Module 3):** Constructors fall under the *"Classes and Objects"* sub-topic. Students must be able to distinguish between **default constructors** and **parameterized constructors**, explain the role of the `self` keyword, and write working class definitions that demonstrate object instantiation through constructors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "House & Construction Worker" Model

Imagine you hire a **construction worker** to build a house based on a blueprint (the **Class**).

- The **Blueprint** (Class) itself is just a paper plan ŌĆö it describes *what* the house will look like, but no actual house exists yet.
- The moment the construction worker receives the blueprint, he doesn't just hand you an empty plot. He **lays the foundation, installs the doors, wires the electricity, and paints the walls** ŌĆö i.e., he sets up the initial state.
- That initial setup work is exactly what the **`__init__` constructor** does for an object.

| Real-World Element | Python OOP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Architectural Blueprint | **Class** definition |
| The constructed house | **Object** (instance) |
| Foundation & initial setup work | **Constructor (`__init__`)** |
| Foreman who supervises (refers to *this* house) | **`self` parameter** |
| Customer's customization request (color, rooms) | **Arguments passed** to constructor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A constructor is *not* what creates the object ŌĆö that is the job of `__new__()`. The constructor's job is solely to **initialize the already-allocated object** so that it is in a usable state right from the very first line of its existence.

## 1.3 Why Constructors Matter in Machine Learning

In a machine learning pipeline, you will frequently define classes for:

- **Custom Dataset loaders** ŌĆö initialized with file paths and batch sizes.
- **Neural network layers** ŌĆö initialized with input/output dimensions.
- **Preprocessing pipelines** ŌĆö initialized with normalization parameters (mean $\mu$, standard deviation $\sigma$).

Without constructors, every model component would have to be set up with manual, error-prone assignments after creation ŌĆö a pattern that is brittle, inconsistent, and unscalable. Constructors enforce a **single, deterministic initialization protocol**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Pearl:** If a KTU question asks *"When is a constructor called?"*, the precise one-line answer is:
> *"A constructor is called automatically and exactly once at the time of object creation, before any other method of the class is invoked."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Python Constructor ŌĆö The Operational Breakdown

The general canonical syntax of a Python constructor is:

```python
class ClassName:
    def __init__(self, parameter1, parameter2, ...):
        self.attribute1 = parameter1
        self.attribute2 = parameter2
```

### Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

1. **`class ClassName:`** ŌĆö The class declaration. The blueprint is defined.
2. **`def __init__(self, ...):`** ŌĆö The constructor method is declared. Note that:
   - The name **must** be exactly `__init__`.
   - The first parameter is conventionally named `self` (though technically any name works).
3. **`self.attribute = value`** ŌĆö Each instance variable is bound to the specific object via `self`. Without `self`, the variable would be a *local* variable that vanishes after the constructor exits.
4. **Object creation**: When you write `obj = ClassName(arg1, arg2)`, Python internally:
   - Allocates memory for a new object.
   - Calls `__new__()` to formally create it.
   - Calls `__init__()` to populate it.
   - Returns the fully initialized object bound to the variable name `obj`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `self` parameter is NEVER passed explicitly** by the caller. Python injects it automatically. When you write `obj = ClassName("Tesla")`, the object reference for `obj` is internally passed as `self`, while `"Tesla"` becomes the actual argument.

## 2.2 The Two Official Types of Constructors (KTU High-Yield)

### Type 1: Default Constructor (No-Argument Constructor)

A constructor that accepts **no parameters** (other than `self`). It is used when every object of the class should start with identical, hard-coded default attribute values.

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self):          # Default constructor
        self.name = "Unknown"
        self.semester = 1
        self.branch = "Undeclared"

s1 = Student()
print(s1.name, s1.semester, s1.branch)
```

**Output:**
```
Unknown 1 Undeclared
```

### Type 2: Parameterized Constructor

A constructor that accepts **one or more parameters** (in addition to `self`). It allows each object to be created with **custom, distinct initial values**, making it the workhorse of real-world ML code.

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, semester, branch):
        self.name = name
        self.semester = semester
        self.branch = branch

s1 = Student("Ananya", 5, "CSE-AI")
s2 = Student("Rahul",  3, "ECE")
```

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Rule / Boundary Condition | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constructor method name | `__init__` | Must be exactly `__init__` with double underscores on both sides | Standardized across entire Python ecosystem (Scikit-learn, PyTorch, TensorFlow) |
| First parameter | `self` | Refers to the *current* instance being initialized; never passed by caller | Distinguishes *this* object from other instances of the same class |
| Default Constructor | `def __init__(self):` | Zero user-defined arguments; values are hard-coded | Used for utility/helper classes with fixed behavior |
| Parameterized Constructor | `def __init__(self, p1, p2, ...):` | Arguments passed at instantiation via `ClassName(arg1, arg2)` | Used for ML models with hyperparameters |
| Implicit Constructor | No `__init__` defined | Python provides an empty default constructor automatically | Avoided in production; provided only as a safety net |
| Invocation timing | Automatic at `ObjectName = ClassName(...)` | Called **exactly once** per object lifetime | Ensures every object is born in a valid state |
| Lifetime scope | Inside `__init__` | `self` attributes persist for the object's full lifetime; local variables do not | State management for long-lived ML objects |

## 2.4 The Mathematical Representation of State Initialization

In ML, constructors commonly store scalar hyperparameters. If a class represents a **Linear Regression model**, the constructor might initialize weight $w$ and bias $b$:

$$\text{LinearModel}(w_0, b_0) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Object with state } (w = w_0,\; b = b_0)$$

The hypothesis function the object can then compute is:

$$\hat{y} \;=\; w \cdot x \;+\; b$$

Here, $w$ and $b$ are the **state variables initialized by the constructor**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Execution Trace & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Execution Walk-Through

We will trace, line by line, what happens in memory when a parameterized constructor is invoked. Consider this complete program:

```python
class Dataset:
    def __init__(self, name, size, mean, std):
        self.name = name
        self.size = size
        self.mean = mean
        self.std = std
        print("Constructor invoked for:", self.name)

    def summary(self):
        print(f"{self.name} | N={self.size} | mu={self.mean} | sigma={self.std}")

# Step A: Object creation with explicit arguments
train = Dataset("TrainingSet", 5000, 0.0, 1.0)

# Step B: Method call on initialized object
train.summary()
```

### Step-by-Step Memory & Control Flow Trace

**Step 1 ŌĆö Class Definition Encountered:**
Python registers the `Dataset` class in memory. No object exists yet. The `__init__` function is stored as an attribute of the class itself, not yet executed.

**Step 2 ŌĆö `train = Dataset(...)` Statement Reached:**
Python recognizes an instantiation. Internally, it performs the following sub-operations:

- **2a.** Allocates a fresh, empty namespace in memory for the new object. Call this memory location `M`.
- **2b.** Invokes `Dataset.__new__(Dataset)` to formally create the empty object at `M`.
- **2c.** Invokes `Dataset.__init__(M, "TrainingSet", 5000, 0.0, 1.0)`. Note: `M` is automatically passed as `self`.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Inside `__init__` Execution:**
Each assignment binds an attribute on `self` (which is `M`):

$$\texttt{self.name} \;\longleftarrow\; \texttt{"TrainingSet"}$$

$$\texttt{self.size} \;\longleftarrow\; 5000$$

$$\texttt{self.mean} \;\longleftarrow\; 0.0$$

$$\texttt{self.std} \;\longleftarrow\; 1.0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Print Statement Inside Constructor:**
Outputs: `Constructor invoked for: TrainingSet`

**Step 5 ŌĆö Constructor Returns Implicitly:**
Control returns to the instantiation line. The fully populated object `M` is bound to the variable name `train`.

**Step 6 ŌĆö `train.summary()` Invocation:**
The method accesses `self.name`, `self.size`, `self.mean`, `self.std` ŌĆö all of which were established during Step 3.

### Expected Output

```
Constructor invoked for: TrainingSet
TrainingSet | N=5000 | mu=0.0 | sigma=1.0
```

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Default Constructor | Parameterized Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| **Argument count (excluding `self`)** | $0$ | $\geq 1$ |
| **Flexibility** | Low ŌĆö every object identical | High ŌĆö each object can differ |
| **Code verbosity at instantiation** | `obj = ClassName()` | `obj = ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...)` |
| **Risk of missing arguments** | None (no args required) | Yes ŌĆö `TypeError` raised if any arg omitted |
| **Typical use case** | Utility classes, singletons | ML models, datasets, configurations |
| **Error if `self` is omitted** | `TypeError: ...takes 0 positional arguments...` | Same error type, different message |
| **Examiner preference (KTU)** | Often asked as theoretical question | Often asked as 7-mark coding question |

## 3.3 Common Construction Pitfalls ŌĆö Analytical Catalogue

| # | Mistake | Symptom | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forgetting `self` as first parameter | `TypeError: ...takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given` | Always include `self` as first parameter |
| 2 | Misspelling `__init__` (e.g., `_init_`, `__Init__`) | Method is treated as a *regular* method; never auto-called | Use **exactly** `__init__` with double underscores |
| 3 | Calling constructor explicitly via `obj.__init__()` | Works but constructor runs again, *overwriting* state | Let Python call it automatically; never invoke manually |
| 4 | Assigning to a local variable instead of `self.x` | Attribute vanishes after constructor returns | Always prefix with `self.` for persistence |
| 5 | Trying to `return` a value from `__init__` | Raises `TypeError: __init__() should return None` | `__init__` must return nothing (implicit `None`) |

## 3.4 Derivative Concept: Constructor Chaining via `super()`

In inheritance hierarchies (covered later in Module 3), a child class constructor can chain to its parent's constructor:

```python
class BaseModel:
    def __init__(self, learning_rate):
        self.lr = learning_rate

class NeuralNet(BaseModel):
    def __init__(self, learning_rate, layers):
        super().__init__(learning_rate)   # Initialize parent state
        self.layers = layers
```

$$\text{NeuralNet}(\text{lr}, \text{L}) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Object initialized with both } \texttt{self.lr} \text{ and } \texttt{self.layers}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** For PEERT526 Module 3, simple single-class constructors are the primary focus. `super()` chaining may appear as a 3-mark conceptual question but is rarely a 14-mark coding question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Object Creation & Constructor Invocation Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the exact control-flow when Python encounters an instantiation statement:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Statement: obj = ClassName arg1, arg2] --> B[Python Interpreter Detects Instantiation]
    B --> C[Allocate Empty Memory Block M for New Object]
    C --> D[Call ClassName.__new__ to Create Empty Object]
    D --> E[Call ClassName.__init__ with self pointing to M]
    E --> F{Is __init__ defined by user?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Execute User-defined Initialization Code]
    G --> H[Bind Attributes self.attr1, self.attr2 via self]
    H --> I[Implicit Return None]
    F -- No --> J[Use Python's Built-in Empty Default Constructor]
    J --> I
    I --> K[Bind the Fully Initialized Object M to Variable obj]
    K --> L[Execution Continues with obj Ready for Use]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Class & Object Memory Model

This diagram illustrates the distinction between the **class object** (the blueprint stored once in memory) and the **instance objects** (one per instantiation):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLS["Class Object: Dataset (stored once)"]
        C1[__init__ method definition]
        C2[summary method definition]
    end
    subgraph OBJ1["Instance: train"]
        O1A["name = 'TrainingSet'"]
        O1B["size = 5000"]
        O1C["mean = 0.0"]
        O1D["std = 1.0"]
    end
    subgraph OBJ2["Instance: test"]
        O2A["name = 'TestSet'"]
        O2B["size = 1000"]
        O2C["mean = 0.0"]
        O2D["std = 1.0"]
    end
    C1 -.invoked by.-> OBJ1
    C1 -.invoked by.-> OBJ2
    OBJ1 --> CALL1["train.summary() reads O1A..O1D"]
    OBJ2 --> CALL2["test.summary() reads O2A..O2D"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Constructor Type Decision Tree

A classification flowchart that helps students decide which constructor type to use:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Need to Initialize Object Attributes?] --> A{Are distinct values needed per object?}
    A -- No, identical defaults suffice --> D1[Use Default Constructor]
    A -- Yes, each object should differ --> D2[Use Parameterized Constructor]
    D1 --> X1[Example: Utility class with fixed config]
    D2 --> X2[Example: ML Model with hyperparameters]
    X2 --> X3[Pass values at instantiation: Model arg1, arg2]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is a constructor in Python? Explain the role of the `__init__` method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A constructor in Python is a special method that is **automatically invoked when an object of a class is created**, used to initialize the object's attributes to a valid initial state.

The `__init__` method (pronounced *dunder init*) is Python's reserved constructor name.

```python
class Car:
    def __init__(self, brand, speed):
        self.brand = brand
        self.speed = speed
```

**Key points (allocate 1 mark each):**
1. Definition + automatic invocation (1 mark)
2. Name `__init__` and double-underscore convention (1 mark)
3. Role: assigning initial values via `self.attribute` (1 mark)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a default constructor and a parameterized constructor in Python.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Default Constructor | Parameterized Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters | Only `self` | `self` + one or more user parameters |
| Flexibility | Low ŌĆö same values for all objects | High ŌĆö distinct values per object |
| Example | `def __init__(self):` | `def __init__(self, name, age):` |
| Calling | `obj = ClassName()` | `obj = ClassName("Alice", 21)` |

**(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for comparison/example.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** misspell `__init__` as `_init_` or `__Init__`. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this single typo because it indicates conceptual confusion about magic methods.
> - **Do NOT** include `return` statements inside `__init__`. It must return implicitly `None`. Adding `return self` or `return value` causes a `TypeError` at runtime and is penalized heavily.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *defining* a constructor with *calling* it. The constructor is **defined inside** the class but **called automatically** when you write `obj = ClassName(...)`.
> - Always **mention `self` explicitly** in your written explanation ŌĆö this is a guaranteed 1-mark item on nearly every KTU paper.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`  **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a constructor in Python with a suitable example. Discuss the significance of the `self` parameter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to define a class `LinearRegressor` with a parameterized constructor that accepts `weight (w)`, `bias (b)`, and `learning_rate (lr)`. Include a method `predict(x)` that returns the predicted value using $\hat{y} = w \cdot x + b$. Instantiate the class with $w = 2.0$, $b = 1.5$, $lr = 0.01$, and demonstrate prediction for $x = 5$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Conceptual (7 Marks)

A constructor is a **special method** in Python that is automatically called at the time of object creation. Its purpose is to **initialize the instance variables** of the new object so that it begins its lifecycle in a well-defined state.

Python identifies the constructor by the reserved name `__init__`. The double underscores prefix and suffix are part of Python's *dunder (double underscore) convention* for magic methods, which the interpreter recognizes and treats specially.

**Significance of `self`:**

- `self` is the **first parameter** of any instance method, including the constructor.
- It is a reference to the **current instance** of the class ŌĆö i.e., the specific object that is currently being initialized.
- When you write `obj = ClassName("data")`, Python internally translates this as `ClassName.__init__(obj, "data")`, automatically injecting the newly created object as the first argument.
- Without `self`, attributes assigned inside `__init__` would be **local variables** that vanish when the method returns, leaving the object empty.

```python
class Employee:
    def __init__(self, name, salary):
        self.name = name        # self.name belongs to the object
        self.salary = salary    # self.salary belongs to the object

e1 = Employee("Kavya", 50000)
print(e1.name, e1.salary)
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (a):**
- Definition of constructor ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Explanation of `__init__` naming convention ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Significance of `self` (with example) ŌĆö **3 marks**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Coding (7 Marks)

```python
class LinearRegressor:
    def __init__(self, weight, bias, learning_rate):
        # Parameterized constructor
        self.w = weight
        self.b = bias
        self.lr = learning_rate
        print(f"LinearRegressor initialized: w={self.w}, b={self.b}, lr={self.lr}")

    def predict(self, x):
        # Hypothesis: y_hat = w * x + b
        y_hat = self.w * x + self.b
        return y_hat

# Instantiation
model = LinearRegressor(2.0, 1.5, 0.01)

# Prediction for x = 5
x_value = 5
prediction = model.predict(x_value)
print(f"Prediction for x={x_value}: y_hat = {prediction}")
```

**Step-by-step trace of prediction:**

$$\hat{y} \;=\; w \cdot x \;+\; b$$

$$\hat{y} \;=\; 2.0 \cdot 5 \;+\; 1.5$$

$$\hat{y} \;=\; 10.0 \;+\; 1.5$$

$$\hat{y} \;=\; 11.5$$

**Expected Output:**

```
LinearRegressor initialized: w=2.0, b=1.5, lr=0.01
Prediction for x=5: y_hat = 11.5
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (b):**
- Correct class definition with constructor ŌĆö **3 marks**
- Correct `predict()` method with formula ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Correct instantiation + output trace ŌĆö **2 marks**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`  **(14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Differentiate between default and parameterized constructors in Python. Provide one code example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to define a class `DataSample` with a parameterized constructor accepting `features (list)`, `label (int)`, and `weight (float)`. Include a method `describe()` that prints all three attributes in a formatted manner. Instantiate two objects with different values and call `describe()` on both. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) ŌĆö Conceptual with Examples (7 Marks)

| Aspect | Default Constructor | Parameterized Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Accepts only `self`; initializes attributes to hard-coded defaults | Accepts `self` plus one or more external parameters |
| **Syntax** | `def __init__(self):` | `def __init__(self, p1, p2):` |
| **Argument passing** | No arguments supplied at instantiation | Arguments supplied as `ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...)` |
| **Object distinctness** | All objects start identically | Each object can be uniquely configured |

**Default Constructor Example:**

```python
class Sensor:
    def __init__(self):
        self.type = "Temperature"
        self.unit = "Celsius"

s = Sensor()
print(s.type, s.unit)    # Temperature Celsius
```

**Parameterized Constructor Example:**

```python
class Sensor:
    def __init__(self, sensor_type, unit):
        self.type = sensor_type
        self.unit = unit

s1 = Sensor("Humidity", "Percent")
s2 = Sensor("Pressure", "Pascal")
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (a):**
- Tabular differentiation ŌĆö **3 marks**
- Default constructor example ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Parameterized constructor example ŌĆö **2 marks**

#### Part (b) ŌĆö Coding (7 Marks)

```python
class DataSample:
    def __init__(self, features, label, weight):
        # Parameterized constructor with three parameters
        self.features = features
        self.label = label
        self.weight = weight

    def describe(self):
        print(f"Features: {self.features} | Label: {self.label} | Weight: {self.weight}")

# First object
sample1 = DataSample([0.5, 1.2, -0.3], label=1, weight=0.85)
sample1.describe()

# Second object
sample2 = DataSample([2.1, 0.0, 1.7], label=0, weight=0.62)
sample2.describe()
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Features: [0.5, 1.2, -0.3] | Label: 1 | Weight: 0.85
Features: [2.1, 0.0, 1.7] | Label: 0 | Weight: 0.62
```

**Mark Allocation for Part (b):**
- Class definition with constructor ŌĆö **3 marks**
- `describe()` method implementation ŌĆö **2 marks**
- Two distinct instantiations + correct output ŌĆö **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Constructor (PEERT526 Module 3)**

- **Definition:** A constructor is a special method invoked automatically during object creation to initialize instance attributes.
- **Reserved Name:** The Python constructor is **always** named `__init__` (with double underscores on **both** sides). Any deviation means it will not be treated as a constructor.
- **The `self` parameter:** It is the **first parameter** of every instance method. It is a reference to the *current object*. **Never** passed explicitly by the caller ŌĆö Python injects it automatically.
- **Default Constructor:** Has the signature `def __init__(self):` ŌĆö takes **no** additional arguments. Useful for fixed-configuration classes.
- **Parameterized Constructor:** Has the signature `def __init__(self, p1, p2, ...):` ŌĆö accepts external arguments. **Most commonly used** in real-world ML and data science code.
- **Implicit Constructor:** If a class has **no** `__init__` defined, Python provides an invisible, empty default constructor. This is rarely used in practice.
- **Invocation Timing:** Called **exactly once** per object lifetime, at the moment of instantiation, before any other method.
- **Return Value Rule:** `__init__` must **return `None` implicitly**. Returning any value explicitly causes a `TypeError`.
- **Attribute Persistence:** Variables assigned via `self.attribute` inside the constructor persist for the object's full lifetime. Variables without `self.` are local and vanish.
- **Common Errors to Memorize:**
  - `TypeError: ...takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given` ŌåÆ forgot `self`
  - `TypeError: __init__() missing 1 required positional argument` ŌåÆ forgot to pass an argument at instantiation
  - `TypeError: __init__() should return None` ŌåÆ accidentally added a `return` statement
- **ML Connection:** Constructors are the entry point for initializing ML model hyperparameters (`learning_rate`, `num_layers`, `input_dim`, `mean`, `std`, etc.). Every Scikit-learn estimator and PyTorch module has its own constructor.
- **Mnemonic:** *"Constructors Construct Initial State"* ŌĆö **CCIS** ŌĆö the four pillars of what a constructor does.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accessors and Mutators. Structuring Classes with Inheritance and Polymorphism. Abstract Classes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accessors and Mutators. Structuring Classes with Inheritance and Polymorphism. Abstract Classes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Accessors and Mutators (Getters and Setters)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
*Accessors* (also called **getters**) are instance methods specifically designed to **retrieve (read)** the value of a private or protected attribute, while *Mutators* (also called **setters**) are instance methods designed to **modify (write/validate)** the value of such attributes. Together, they form the encapsulation backbone of Python's Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigm under the KTU 2024 Scheme's PEP 8 style guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 HIGHLIGHT:** The official KTU syllabus (PEERT526 Module 3) mandates the use of the `@property` decorator (Pythonic way) along with traditional getter/setter methods. Both approaches are examinable.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **bank locker**. You cannot directly reach into the locker and grab your valuables ŌĆö that would be unsafe and unaccounted for. Instead, you go to the **bank manager (the accessor)**, politely ask "Can I see what's inside?" ŌĆö and the manager shows you. If you want to **store something new**, the **manager (the mutator) checks your ID, validates your request, and only then places the item inside**. The actual locker (the private variable) is never touched directly. The manager acts as a controlled gateway ŌĆö exactly what accessors and mutators do for class attributes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Rule:** In Python, attribute names prefixed with a **single underscore** `_attribute` are *protected* (convention), and those prefixed with **double underscore** `__attribute` are *name-mangled* (effectively private). Accessors and Mutators safely bridge the outside world with these encapsulated attributes.

---

### 1.2 Structuring Classes with Inheritance

**Formal Definition:**
*Inheritance* is the OOP mechanism by which a **derived (child) class** acquires the attributes and methods of a **base (parent) class**, enabling **code reuse, hierarchical classification, and polymorphic behavior**. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the `class DerivedClass(BaseClass):` syntax and the use of `super()` for accessing parent constructors and overridden methods.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **family tree**. A child **inherits** the surname, eye color, and certain traits from their parents ŌĆö but the child can also have their own unique hobbies. In OOP, a `Vehicle` (parent) class might define `start_engine()`; a `Car` (child) class inherits this and adds `open_trunk()`. The child reuses everything from the parent and extends it.

---

### 1.3 Polymorphism

**Formal Definition:**
*Polymorphism* (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of different classes to be treated through a **common interface**, where the **same method name** invokes **different behaviors** depending on the actual object type at runtime. KTU 2024 distinguishes two principal forms: **Method Overriding (Runtime Polymorphism)** and **Duck Typing (Python-specific)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Consider the word **"move"**. A dog *moves* by running, a fish *moves* by swimming, a bird *moves* by flying. The **command is identical** ("Move!"), but the **execution differs** by object type. Polymorphism lets you call `.move()` on any animal without knowing *how* it physically moves.

---

### 1.4 Abstract Classes

**Formal Definition:**
An *Abstract Class* is a class that **cannot be instantiated** directly and may declare one or more **abstract methods** (methods declared but not implemented). Subclasses **must override** all abstract methods. In Python, abstract classes are defined using the `abc` (Abstract Base Class) module with the `ABC` metaclass and the `@abstractmethod` decorator.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
An **abstract class** is like a **blueprint or a contract**. You cannot build a house from a blueprint alone ŌĆö you need a *concrete* architectural plan. Similarly, you cannot instantiate `Shape` directly, but you *must* create `Circle`, `Square`, or `Triangle` ŌĆö each giving its own definition of `area()`. The abstract class says: *"Whoever inherits from me MUST provide an `area()` function."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Abstract classes are the **interface contract mechanism** in Python. KTU questions frequently test the difference between *abstract class* and *concrete class*, and the role of `ABC` and `@abstractmethod`.

---

### 1.5 Topic Significance for Machine Learning

In the broader context of **PEERT526 (Python Basics for Machine Learning)**, these OOP concepts are foundational because:

- **scikit-learn** uses class inheritance heavily (e.g., `BaseEstimator`, `ClassifierMixin`).
- **PyTorch** and **TensorFlow** models inherit from base classes like `nn.Module`.
- Polymorphism allows ML pipelines to swap algorithms seamlessly (e.g., any classifier with `fit()` and `predict()`).
- Abstract classes define **interfaces** that enforce consistent API design across ML libraries.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Python's `abc.ABC` module is part of the **Python Standard Library** (no installation required). The metaclass `ABCMeta` is automatically applied when a class inherits from `ABC`.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Accessors and Mutators ŌĆö Deep Analysis

**The Two Implementation Strategies in Python:**

| Strategy | Mechanism | Use Case | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional Getter/Setter | Explicit `get_x()` / `set_x()` methods | Java-style OOP, explicit control | Medium |
| Pythonic `@property` | Decorator-based attribute access | Idiomatic Python, clean syntax | **High** |

**Step-by-Step Logic for `@property`:**

1. **Define a private/protected attribute** (e.g., `self._value`).
2. **Decorate a method with `@property`** to convert it into a *read-only* accessor.
3. **Decorate another method with `@<property_name>.setter`** to create the mutator.
4. **Access/Modify the attribute using direct attribute syntax** (`obj.value = 10`), even though methods are called internally.

**Why use Accessors and Mutators (The "Why"):**

- **Encapsulation:** Hides internal state representation.
- **Validation:** Mutators can reject invalid values (e.g., negative age, empty string).
- **Read-Only Attributes:** A property without a setter makes an attribute immutable from outside.
- **Backward Compatibility:** Changing internal storage (e.g., from a list to a dict) doesn't break external code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PYTHONIC PROPERTY RULE:** When using `@property`, the **method name** becomes the *public attribute name*. Never call it as `obj.get_value()` ŌĆö call it as `obj.value`.

---

### 2.2 Inheritance ŌĆö Deep Analysis

**Types of Inheritance Recognized by KTU 2024 Scheme:**

| Type | Structure | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Single | One parent ŌåÆ one child | `class B(A)` |
| Multilevel | Grandparent ŌåÆ Parent ŌåÆ Child | `C(B), B(A)` |
| Multiple | Multiple parents ŌåÆ one child | `class C(A, B)` |
| Hierarchical | One parent ŌåÆ multiple children | `B(A), C(A)` |
| Hybrid | Combination of above | Real-world systems |

**Method Resolution Order (MRO):**
Python uses the **C3 Linearization Algorithm** to determine the order in which base classes are searched when executing a method. The `mro()` method or the `__mro__` attribute returns this order.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** In **Multiple Inheritance**, if two parent classes define the same method, the MRO (left-to-right depth-first) determines which one is called. Always use `super()` to call parent methods explicitly and predictably.

**The `super()` Function:**
- `super()` returns a temporary object of the parent class.
- It allows you to call parent methods without explicitly naming the parent class.
- **Crucial** for cooperative multiple inheritance and proper MRO chain.

---

### 2.3 Polymorphism ŌĆö Deep Analysis

**Two Key Mechanisms in Python:**

1. **Method Overriding (Subtype Polymorphism):**
   - Child class provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in the parent class.
   - The overridden method is invoked based on the **actual object type** at runtime (dynamic dispatch).

2. **Duck Typing (Pythonic Polymorphism):**
   - *"If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck."*
   - Python doesn't check the type ŌĆö only whether the method exists on the object.
   - This is a **structural**, not **nominal**, polymorphism.

**Operator Overloading (Bonus Polymorphism):**
- Python allows redefining operators (`+`, `-`, `*`) via **dunder (magic) methods** like `__add__`, `__sub__`, `__mul__`.
- This is a form of polymorphism where the same operator behaves differently for different types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD:** Questions on **Duck Typing vs. Method Overriding** appear frequently. The answer: *Method overriding is enforced by inheritance hierarchy; duck typing is enforced by the presence of an attribute/method, regardless of class lineage.*

---

### 2.4 Abstract Classes ŌĆö Deep Analysis

**Operational Steps to Create an Abstract Class:**

1. Import the `abc` module: `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod`.
2. Inherit your class from `ABC` (not directly from `object`).
3. Decorate one or more methods with `@abstractmethod`.
4. **Do not provide a body** (or use `pass` / `...` as placeholder) for abstract methods.
5. Concrete subclasses must override **all** abstract methods or remain abstract themselves.

**Key Properties:**

- **Cannot Instantiate:** `Shape()` raises `TypeError`.
- **Concrete Subclass Requirement:** Subclass must implement ALL abstract methods.
- **Abstract Methods Can Have Implementation:** In Python, abstract methods *can* have a body (unlike Java), but subclasses are still required to override.
- **Decorators Stack:** You can combine `@property` + `@abstractmethod` for abstract properties.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Syntax / Formula | Purpose | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| Traditional Getter | `def get_x(self): return self.__x` | Read private attribute | 2 Marks |
| Traditional Setter | `def set_x(self, val): self.__x = val` | Write private attribute | 2 Marks |
| Property Decorator | `@property` | Convert method ŌåÆ attribute access | **4 Marks** |
| Setter Decorator | `@x.setter` | Define setter for property | **4 Marks** |
| Single Inheritance | `class Child(Parent):` | Inherit from one class | 2 Marks |
| Multiple Inheritance | `class Child(P1, P2):` | Inherit from multiple classes | **5 Marks** |
| `super()` Call | `super().__init__()` | Call parent constructor | **4 Marks** |
| Method Overriding | Same method name in child | Runtime polymorphism | **5 Marks** |
| Duck Typing | `obj.method()` without type check | Structural polymorphism | 3 Marks |
| Abstract Class | `class X(ABC):` | Cannot instantiate | **6 Marks** |
| Abstract Method | `@abstractmethod` | Must be overridden | **6 Marks** |
| MRO | `ClassName.mro()` | Resolution order | 3 Marks |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting that `@property` decorator changes the method into a read-only attribute. Trying to assign to it without a `.setter` raises `AttributeError`.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Code Walkthroughs)

### 3.1 Accessors and Mutators ŌĆö Complete Code Walkthrough

**Example 1: Traditional Getter/Setter Approach**

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, age):
        # Step 1: Initialize private attributes with double underscore
        self.__name = name
        self.__age = age

    # Step 2: Accessor (Getter) for name
    def get_name(self):
        return self.__name

    # Step 3: Mutator (Setter) for name with validation
    def set_name(self, name):
        if isinstance(name, str) and name.strip():
            self.__name = name
        else:
            raise ValueError("Name must be a non-empty string")

    # Step 4: Accessor for age
    def get_age(self):
        return self.__age

    # Step 5: Mutator for age with validation
    def set_age(self, age):
        if isinstance(age, int) and 0 < age < 150:
            self.__age = age
        else:
            raise ValueError("Age must be between 1 and 149")

# Step 6: Usage demonstration
s = Student("Alice", 20)
print(s.get_name())       # Output: Alice
s.set_age(21)
print(s.get_age())        # Output: 21
# s.set_age(-5)           # Raises ValueError
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** The constructor stores values into name-mangled attributes (`__name`, `__age`), making them inaccessible from outside the class.
- **Step 2:** `get_name()` acts as the *accessor*, returning the stored value.
- **Step 3:** `set_name()` acts as the *mutator*, applying a validation rule (must be a non-empty string) before assignment.
- **Step 4-5:** Same pattern applied to `age` with numerical range validation.
- **Step 6:** Demonstrates the controlled read/write interface.

---

**Example 2: Pythonic `@property` Approach (KTU Most Expected)**

```python
class Employee:
    def __init__(self, name, salary):
        # Step 1: Use single underscore for protected attribute (convention)
        self._name = name
        self._salary = salary

    # Step 2: Define getter using @property decorator
    @property
    def name(self):
        """Accessor for name"""
        return self._name

    # Step 3: Define setter using @name.setter
    @name.setter
    def name(self, value):
        if not isinstance(value, str) or not value.strip():
            raise ValueError("Name must be a non-empty string")
        self._name = value

    # Step 4: Read-only property (no setter defined)
    @property
    def salary(self):
        """Read-only accessor for salary"""
        return self._salary

# Step 5: Usage - Access like attributes, NOT methods
emp = Employee("Bob", 50000)
print(emp.name)        # Calls getter internally ŌåÆ Output: Bob
emp.name = "Robert"    # Calls setter internally ŌåÆ Validated
print(emp.name)        # Output: Robert
# emp.salary = 60000   # AttributeError: cannot set read-only property
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** The attribute is named `_name` (single underscore = *protected* convention).
- **Step 2:** The `@property` decorator transforms the `name()` method into a **getter**. When you access `emp.name`, Python internally calls this method.
- **Step 3:** The `@name.setter` decorator creates the corresponding *setter*. Assigning to `emp.name = "Robert"` triggers the validation logic.
- **Step 4:** Defining only `@property` (no setter) makes the attribute **read-only** ŌĆö Python enforces immutability from the outside.
- **Step 5:** Demonstrates the elegant, Pythonic syntax where accessors/mutators are invisible to the caller.

---

### 3.2 Inheritance ŌĆö Complete Code Walkthrough

**Example 3: Single Inheritance with `super()`**

```python
# Step 1: Define parent (base) class
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name, species):
        self.name = name
        self.species = species
        print(f"Animal.__init__ called for {name}")

    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} makes a sound."

    def info(self):
        return f"{self.name} is a {self.species}"

# Step 2: Define child (derived) class
class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        # Step 3: Call parent constructor to initialize inherited attributes
        super().__init__(name, species="Dog")
        self.breed = breed
        print(f"Dog.__init__ called for {name}")

    # Step 4: Override the parent method (polymorphism in action)
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} says Woof!"

    # Step 5: Add a child-specific method
    def fetch(self):
        return f"{self.name} fetches the ball."

# Step 6: Instantiate and demonstrate inheritance
d = Dog("Buddy", "Labrador")
print(d.info())         # Inherited method ŌåÆ "Buddy is a Dog"
print(d.speak())        # Overridden method ŌåÆ "Buddy says Woof!"
print(d.fetch())        # Child-specific method
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** `Animal` is the **base class** with a constructor and two methods.
- **Step 2:** `Dog(Animal)` declares inheritance from `Animal`.
- **Step 3:** `super().__init__(...)` invokes the parent constructor, ensuring `name` and `species` are properly initialized before child-specific logic.
- **Step 4:** `speak()` is **overridden** in `Dog`, replacing the generic animal sound with a dog-specific one.
- **Step 5:** `fetch()` is a brand-new method exclusive to `Dog`.
- **Step 6:** The instance `d` can use inherited methods (`info`), overridden methods (`speak`), and its own methods (`fetch`).

---

**Example 4: Multiple Inheritance and MRO**

```python
# Step 1: First parent class
class Flyer:
    def move(self):
        return "Flying in the sky"

# Step 2: Second parent class
class Swimmer:
    def move(self):
        return "Swimming in water"

# Step 3: Child inherits from BOTH parents
class Duck(Flyer, Swimmer):
    pass

# Step 4: Verify MRO
print(Duck.mro())
# Output: [<class '__main__.Duck'>, <class '__main__.Flyer'>, 
#          <class '__main__.Swimmer'>, <class 'object'>]

# Step 5: Which move() is called?
d = Duck()
print(d.move())  # Output: "Flying in the sky" (Flyer is first in MRO)
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1-2:** Two parent classes, both defining a `move()` method (name collision).
- **Step 3:** `Duck(Flyer, Swimmer)` ŌĆö the order matters for MRO.
- **Step 4:** `Duck.mro()` reveals the search order: `Duck ŌåÆ Flyer ŌåÆ Swimmer ŌåÆ object`.
- **Step 5:** Since `Flyer` appears first in MRO, its `move()` is called. **Reorder** to `class Duck(Swimmer, Flyer)` to change this behavior.

---

### 3.3 Polymorphism ŌĆö Complete Code Walkthrough

**Example 5: Method Overriding (Subtype Polymorphism)**

```python
# Step 1: Common interface (parent class)
class Shape:
    def area(self):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement area()")

    def perimeter(self):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement perimeter()")

# Step 2: Concrete subclass 1
class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, radius):
        self.radius = radius

    def area(self):
        return 3.14159 * self.radius ** 2

    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * 3.14159 * self.radius

# Step 3: Concrete subclass 2
class Rectangle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, length, width):
        self.length = length
        self.width = width

    def area(self):
        return self.length * self.width

    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * (self.length + self.width)

# Step 4: Polymorphic function - same interface, different behavior
def print_shape_info(shape):
    print(f"Area: {shape.area()}, Perimeter: {shape.perimeter()}")

# Step 5: Pass different objects to the same function
shapes = [Circle(5), Rectangle(4, 6), Circle(3)]
for shape in shapes:
    print_shape_info(shape)

# Output:
# Area: 78.53975, Perimeter: 31.4159
# Area: 24, Perimeter: 20
# Area: 28.27431, Perimeter: 18.84954
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** `Shape` defines the **common interface** with methods that raise errors if not overridden.
- **Step 2-3:** `Circle` and `Rectangle` provide their own implementations of `area()` and `perimeter()`.
- **Step 4:** The function `print_shape_info` accepts **any** `Shape` object.
- **Step 5:** The **same call** `shape.area()` produces **different results** based on the actual object type ŌĆö this is polymorphism.

---

**Example 6: Duck Typing (Pythonic Polymorphism)**

```python
# Step 1: Three unrelated classes (NO inheritance)
class Dog:
    def speak(self):
        return "Woof!"

class Cat:
    def speak(self):
        return "Meow!"

class Human:
    def speak(self):
        return "Hello!"

# Step 2: Function that works on ANY object with a .speak() method
def make_it_speak(entity):
    # Step 3: No type check, no inheritance requirement
    print(entity.speak())

# Step 4: Pass completely unrelated objects
make_it_speak(Dog())      # Output: Woof!
make_it_speak(Cat())      # Output: Meow!
make_it_speak(Human())    # Output: Hello!
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Human` share **no parent class**.
- **Step 2-3:** The function `make_it_speak` doesn't care about the type ŌĆö only that the object has a `.speak()` method.
- **Step 4:** All three work seamlessly. This is **duck typing** ŌĆö *"if it quacks, treat it as a duck."*

---

### 3.4 Abstract Classes ŌĆö Complete Code Walkthrough

**Example 7: Full Abstract Class Implementation**

```python
# Step 1: Import the abc module
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

# Step 2: Define an abstract base class
class Vehicle(ABC):
    def __init__(self, name, max_speed):
        self.name = name
        self.max_speed = max_speed

    # Step 3: Concrete method (has implementation, can be used as-is)
    def display_info(self):
        return f"{self.name} | Max Speed: {self.max_speed} km/h"

    # Step 4: Abstract method (no body, must be overridden)
    @abstractmethod
    def fuel_type(self):
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def start_engine(self):
        pass

# Step 5: Attempting to instantiate the abstract class
# v = Vehicle("Generic", 100)  # TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class

# Step 6: First concrete subclass
class Car(Vehicle):
    def fuel_type(self):
        return "Petrol/Diesel"

    def start_engine(self):
        return f"{self.name} car engine started with key ignition."

# Step 7: Second concrete subclass
class ElectricCar(Vehicle):
    def fuel_type(self):
        return "Electricity"

    def start_engine(self):
        return f"{self.name} electric car powered on silently."

# Step 8: Instantiate concrete classes
my_car = Car("Honda City", 180)
my_ev = ElectricCar("Tesla Model 3", 220)

# Step 9: Polymorphism with abstract types
vehicles = [my_car, my_ev]
for v in vehicles:
    print(v.display_info())  # Concrete method from parent
    print(v.fuel_type())     # Abstract method overridden in child
    print(v.start_engine())
    print("---")
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** Importing `ABC` and `abstractmethod` from the `abc` module.
- **Step 2:** `Vehicle(ABC)` declares the class as abstract.
- **Step 3:** `display_info()` is a **concrete method** ŌĆö subclasses inherit it as-is.
- **Step 4:** `fuel_type()` and `start_engine()` are decorated with `@abstractmethod`, making them **mandatory overrides**.
- **Step 5:** Trying to instantiate `Vehicle` directly raises `TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class Vehicle with abstract methods fuel_type, start_engine`.
- **Step 6-7:** Both `Car` and `ElectricCar` implement all abstract methods, becoming **concrete** (instantiable) classes.
- **Step 8-9:** The abstract class acts as a **unified type** for polymorphic operations ŌĆö any `Vehicle` object can be processed through the same loop.

---

**Example 8: Abstract Property (Advanced)**

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Database(ABC):
    @property
    @abstractmethod
    def connection_string(self):
        """Subclasses MUST define this property"""
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def execute_query(self, query):
        pass

class MySQLDatabase(Database):
    @property
    def connection_string(self):
        return "mysql://user:pass@localhost:3306/mydb"

    def execute_query(self, query):
        return f"Executing '{query}' on MySQL"

# db = Database()  # TypeError: Can't instantiate abstract class
db = MySQLDatabase()
print(db.connection_string)  # Access abstract property
print(db.execute_query("SELECT * FROM users"))
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- `@property` + `@abstractmethod` combination forces subclasses to implement a **property** (not just a method).
- This is useful when the contract requires **attribute access** rather than method calls.
- Stacking decorators: The **bottom decorator** is applied **first**, then the one above. So `@abstractmethod` wraps the function first, then `@property` wraps the result.

---

### 3.5 Combined Real-World Example (Machine Learning Context)

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

# Step 1: Abstract Base Class defining the ML Model contract
class BaseModel(ABC):
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
        self.is_trained = False

    @abstractmethod
    def fit(self, X, y):
        """Train the model"""
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def predict(self, X):
        """Make predictions"""
        pass

    @property
    @abstractmethod
    def accuracy(self):
        """Return model accuracy"""
        pass

    def summary(self):
        # Step 2: Concrete helper method available to all models
        status = "trained" if self.is_trained else "untrained"
        return f"Model: {self.name} | Status: {status}"

# Step 3: Concrete ML model implementations
class LinearRegressionModel(BaseModel):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("Linear Regression")

    def fit(self, X, y):
        self.is_trained = True
        return f"{self.name}: Fit on data shape {len(X)}"

    def predict(self, X):
        if not self.is_trained:
            raise RuntimeError("Model not trained yet!")
        return [0.0] * len(X)  # Dummy prediction

    @property
    def accuracy(self):
        return 0.85

class DecisionTreeModel(BaseModel):
    def __init__(self, max_depth=5):
        super().__init__(f"Decision Tree (depth={max_depth})")
        self.max_depth = max_depth

    def fit(self, X, y):
        self.is_trained = True
        return f"{self.name}: Fit on data shape {len(X)}"

    def predict(self, X):
        if not self.is_trained:
            raise RuntimeError("Model not trained yet!")
        return [1] * len(X)

    @property
    def accuracy(self):
        return 0.92

# Step 4: Polymorphic usage in an ML pipeline
models = [LinearRegressionModel(), DecisionTreeModel(max_depth=10)]
for model in models:
    print(model.summary())              # Inherited concrete method
    print(model.fit([1, 2, 3], [0, 1])) # Abstract method - overridden
    print(f"Accuracy: {model.accuracy}") # Abstract property - overridden
    print("---")
```

**Step-by-Step Logic Explanation:**

- **Step 1:** `BaseModel` defines the **interface contract** for any ML model.
- **Step 2:** A concrete `summary()` method is shared by all subclasses.
- **Step 3:** Each model implements the abstract methods in its own way (mirroring how `scikit-learn` works internally).
- **Step 4:** The pipeline treats all models **uniformly** through the abstract interface ŌĆö this is the **power of polymorphism + abstraction combined**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Connection:** This pattern is exactly how `scikit-learn` works under the hood. `BaseEstimator` is an abstract-like base class; classifiers inherit from it and implement `fit()` and `predict()`. The same pipeline code can train a `RandomForestClassifier`, `SVC`, or `LogisticRegression` without changes.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Accessor/Mutator Flow with `@property`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Code: obj.value] --> B{Read or Write?}
    B -->|Read| C[Call @property getter]
    B -->|Write| D[Call @value.setter]
    C --> E[Return self._value]
    D --> F{Validation Logic}
    F -->|Valid| G[Assign to self._value]
    F -->|Invalid| H[Raise ValueError]
    G --> I[Update Complete]
    E --> I
    H --> J[Exception Handled]
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The user accesses `obj.value` transparently.
- Python internally routes reads to the `@property` getter and writes to the `@value.setter` mutator.
- Validation occurs **only on writes**, ensuring data integrity.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Inheritance Hierarchy

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        +name: str
        +species: str
        +speak() str
        +info() str
    }
    class Dog {
        +breed: str
        +speak() str
        +fetch() str
    }
    class Puppy {
        +mother_name: str
        +whine() str
    }
    class Cat {
        +color: str
        +speak() str
        +purr() str
    }
    Animal <|-- Dog
    Dog <|-- Puppy
    Animal <|-- Cat
```
