**Diagram Explanation:**
- `Animal` is the **base class**.
- `Dog` and `Cat` are **direct children** (single-level inheritance).
- `Puppy` is a **grandchild** of `Animal` (multilevel inheritance).
- Each child overrides `speak()` with a species-specific implementation.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Polymorphism via Common Interface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client Code] -->|Calls shape.area| Interface{Shape Interface}
    Interface -->|Dispatches to actual type| Circle[Circle.area]
    Interface -->|Dispatches to actual type| Rectangle[Rectangle.area]
    Interface -->|Dispatches to actual type| Triangle[Triangle.area]
    Circle --> Result1[pi times r squared]
    Rectangle --> Result2[length times width]
    Triangle --> Result3[0.5 times base times height]
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- The client code calls `shape.area()` without knowing the concrete type.
- Python's dynamic dispatch routes the call to the correct method based on the **runtime type** of the object.
- This is **runtime polymorphism** in action.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Abstract Class Enforcement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attempt: Vehicle] --> B{Is class abstract?}
    B -->|Yes, has @abstractmethod| C[TypeError raised]
    B -->|No, all methods implemented| D[Object created successfully]
    E[Subclass: Car inherits Vehicle] --> F{All abstract methods overridden?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Car becomes concrete - instantiable]
    F -->|No| H[Car remains abstract - cannot instantiate]
    G --> I[Polymorphic usage allowed]
    H --> J[Must implement remaining methods]
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- Abstract classes **enforce** implementation contracts.
- A subclass with missing abstract methods **remains abstract** and cannot be instantiated.
- This guarantees interface compliance across the inheritance hierarchy.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: MRO in Multiple Inheritance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object Root] --> B[Swimmer]
    A --> C[Flyer]
    B --> D[Duck]
    C --> D
    D -->|d.move() called| E{Check MRO Order}
    E -->|1. Duck itself| F{Defines move?}
    F -->|No| G[2. Flyer]
    G --> H{Defines move?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Call Flyer.move]
    I --> J[Output: Flying in the sky]
```

**Diagram Explanation:**
- MRO follows a **depth-first, left-to-right** traversal.
- Python checks `Duck` first, then moves to the **leftmost parent** (`Flyer`).
- The first class with a matching method wins.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What are accessors and mutators in Python? Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Accessor (1 Mark):** An accessor, also called a *getter*, is a method used to **retrieve** the value of a private or protected attribute of a class. It provides **read-only** access to the data.
- **Mutator (1 Mark):** A mutator, also called a *setter*, is a method used to **modify or assign** a new value to a private attribute, often with validation logic.
- **Example (1 Mark):**
  ```python
  class Demo:
      def __init__(self): self._x = 0
      def get_x(self):      # Accessor
          return self._x
      def set_x(self, val): # Mutator
          self._x = val
  ```

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define polymorphism and method overriding. How does Python support polymorphism without inheritance?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Polymorphism (1 Mark):** Polymorphism is the ability of different objects to respond to the **same method call** in **different ways** based on their actual type.
- **Method Overriding (1 Mark):** A child class provides a **specific implementation** of a method already defined in its parent class, replacing the parent's version.
- **Without Inheritance ŌĆö Duck Typing (1 Mark):** Python supports polymorphism via *duck typing* ŌĆö if an object has the required method/attribute, it can be used regardless of its class hierarchy. For example, a custom class with a `len()`-like method works with `len()` if it implements `__len__`.

---

### Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
*(a)* Explain the concept of **inheritance** in Python with suitable examples. Differentiate between **single** and **multiple** inheritance. What is the role of the `super()` function? **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* What is an **abstract class** in Python? Explain with the help of the `abc` module. Write a Python program to create an abstract class `Shape` with abstract methods `area()` and `perimeter()`, and implement two concrete subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle`. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution ŌĆö Inheritance (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Inheritance is an OOP mechanism in Python where a **derived (child) class** acquires the attributes and methods of a **base (parent) class**. It promotes **code reuse**, **hierarchical classification**, and **polymorphic behavior**. The syntax is:
```python
class ChildClass(ParentClass):
    # child body
```

**Single vs. Multiple Inheritance (2 Marks):**

| Feature | Single Inheritance | Multiple Inheritance |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | One parent ŌåÆ one child | Multiple parents ŌåÆ one child |
| Syntax | `class B(A):` | `class C(A, B):` |
| Complexity | Low | Higher (MRO consideration) |
| KTU Use | Most common | Used in mixin patterns |

**`super()` Function (1 Mark):**
The `super()` function returns a **proxy object** of the parent class, allowing you to call parent methods (especially `__init__`) without explicitly naming the parent. This is essential in multiple inheritance for correct MRO traversal.

**Code Example (2 Marks):**
```python
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name
    def speak(self):
        return f"{self.name} makes a sound"

class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        super().__init__(name)        # Call parent constructor
        self.breed = breed
    def speak(self):                  # Method overriding
        return f"{self.name} barks"

d = Dog("Bruno", "Lab")
print(d.speak())  # Output: Bruno barks
```

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution ŌĆö Abstract Classes (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 Marks):**
An **abstract class** in Python is a class that:
- **Cannot be instantiated** directly.
- May define one or more **abstract methods** (declared with `@abstractmethod`).
- Forces subclasses to **implement all abstract methods**; otherwise, the subclass also remains abstract.

**`abc` Module (1 Mark):**
The `abc` (Abstract Base Classes) module provides:
- `ABC` ŌĆö a helper class to mark a class as abstract.
- `abstractmethod` ŌĆö a decorator to declare abstract methods.
- `ABCMeta` ŌĆö the metaclass that enforces the abstract contract.

**Code Implementation (4 Marks):**
```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
import math

class Shape(ABC):                          # Abstract class
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self):
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def perimeter(self):
        pass

class Circle(Shape):                       # Concrete subclass 1
    def __init__(self, radius):
        self.radius = radius
    def area(self):
        return math.pi * self.radius ** 2
    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * math.pi * self.radius

class Rectangle(Shape):                    # Concrete subclass 2
    def __init__(self, length, width):
        self.length = length
        self.width = width
    def area(self):
        return self.length * self.width
    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * (self.length + self.width)

# Demonstration
shapes = [Circle(5), Rectangle(4, 6)]
for s in shapes:
    print(f"Area: {s.area()}, Perimeter: {s.perimeter()}")
```

**Output:**
```
Area: 78.5398..., Perimeter: 31.4159...
Area: 24, Perimeter: 20
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **-1 Mark:** Forgetting to import `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod`.
> - **-1 Mark:** Trying to instantiate the abstract class directly in the code (`Shape()` will crash).
> - **-1 Mark:** Declaring abstract methods with a body using `return` (use `pass` or `...` instead).
> - **-1 Mark:** Subclass not overriding ALL abstract methods (the subclass will remain abstract and un-instantiable).
> - **-2 Marks:** Using `@abstractmethod` without inheriting from `ABC` ŌĆö the decorator alone does nothing.

---

### Part B Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**
*(a)* What are **accessors** and **mutators**? Explain the **Pythonic approach** using the `@property` decorator with a complete example. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* Define **polymorphism** in Python. Differentiate between **method overriding** and **duck typing** with examples. How is polymorphism related to **abstract classes**? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution ŌĆö Accessors and Mutators with `@property` (7 Marks)

**Definitions (1 Mark):**
- **Accessor:** A method that returns (reads) the value of a private/protected attribute.
- **Mutator:** A method that sets (writes) the value of a private/protected attribute, often with validation.

**Traditional vs. Pythonic Approach (1 Mark):**
- **Traditional:** Explicit `get_x()` and `set_x(val)` methods (verbose).
- **Pythonic:** Use `@property` for elegant attribute-like access with hidden logic.

**Detailed Code with `@property` (5 Marks):**
```python
class Temperature:
    def __init__(self, celsius=0):
        self._celsius = celsius

    @property                       # Accessor
    def celsius(self):
        return self._celsius

    @celsius.setter                 # Mutator with validation
    def celsius(self, value):
        if value < -273.15:
            raise ValueError("Temperature below absolute zero!")
        self._celsius = value

    @property                       # Computed property (no setter)
    def fahrenheit(self):
        return (self._celsius * 9/5) + 32

# Usage
t = Temperature(25)
print(t.celsius)        # 25 (calls getter)
t.celsius = 30          # Calls setter with validation
print(t.fahrenheit)     # 86.0 (computed property)
# t.celsius = -300      # ValueError
```

**Key Points Highlighted in Code:**
- `@property` converts `celsius()` method into a **getter**.
- `@celsius.setter` defines the **setter** with validation.
- `fahrenheit` is a **read-only computed property** (no setter defined).

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution ŌĆö Polymorphism (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Polymorphism allows the **same interface** (method name) to invoke **different behaviors** depending on the **actual type** of the object at runtime.

**Method Overriding (2 Marks):**
- Occurs within an **inheritance hierarchy**.
- Child class **redefines** a method from the parent.
- **Example:**
  ```python
  class Bird:
      def fly(self): return "Bird flies"
  class Sparrow(Bird):
      def fly(self): return "Sparrow flies fast"
  class Penguin(Bird):
      def fly(self): return "Penguin cannot fly"
  
  for bird in [Sparrow(), Penguin()]:
      print(bird.fly())  # Different outputs
  ```

**Duck Typing (2 Marks):**
- Operates **without inheritance**.
- Type checking is replaced by **attribute/method presence check**.
- **Example:**
  ```python
  class Dog:
      def speak(self): return "Woof"
  class Car:
      def speak(self): return "Vroom"
  
  def make_sound(obj):
      print(obj.speak())  # Works for any object with speak()
  
  make_sound(Dog())  # Woof
  make_sound(Car())  # Vroom
  ```

**Polymorphism and Abstract Classes Connection (2 Marks):**
- **Abstract classes define the polymorphic interface** (the contract).
- They declare **what methods** must exist (without specifying implementation).
- Each concrete subclass provides its **own version**, enabling polymorphic dispatch.
- Example: `Shape` (abstract) ŌåÆ `Circle.area()`, `Rectangle.area()` (different implementations of the same interface).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Polymorphism):**
> - **-1 Mark:** Confusing **overloading** (Python doesn't support traditional overloading) with **overriding**.
> - **-1 Mark:** Stating that Python "doesn't support polymorphism" because it lacks formal interfaces ŌĆö incorrect; duck typing is a valid polymorphism mechanism.
> - **-2 Marks:** Forgetting that in duck typing, the **method must actually exist**; otherwise, an `AttributeError` is raised at runtime (no compile-time check).
> - **-1 Mark:** Not mentioning `NotImplementedError` as a Pythonic way to signal that a subclass must override a parent method.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 ŌĆö OOP Advanced):**

**Accessors and Mutators:**
- `@property` decorator ŌåÆ makes a method behave like a **read-only attribute**.
- `@<name>.setter` decorator ŌåÆ defines the mutator with validation.
- Single underscore `_attr` = *protected*; double underscore `__attr` = *name-mangled private*.
- Read-only property: define `@property` **without** a corresponding `.setter`.

**Inheritance:**
- Syntax: `class Child(Parent):`
- `super().__init__()` ŌĆö always call the parent constructor in the child.
- **MRO** (`ClassName.mro()`) ŌĆö defines the method search order in multiple inheritance.
- Multiple inheritance: `class C(A, B)` ŌĆö order matters.

**Polymorphism:**
- **Method Overriding** ŌĆö same method name, different behavior in child class.
- **Duck Typing** ŌĆö *"If it quacks, it's a duck"* ŌĆö no inheritance required.
- Enables flexible, extensible code (e.g., ML pipelines).

**Abstract Classes:**
- Import: `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod`.
- Inherit from `ABC` to make a class abstract.
- Use `@abstractmethod` to declare methods that **must** be overridden.
- Abstract classes **cannot be instantiated**; they enforce a contract.
- Combining `@property` + `@abstractmethod` creates abstract properties.

**Common KTU 2024 Pitfalls:**
- Forgetting `pass` or `...` body in abstract methods.
- Trying to instantiate an abstract class.
- Confusing `@staticmethod` and `@classmethod` with `@property`.
- Not calling `super().__init__()` in child classes ŌåÆ lost parent attributes.
- Reversing MRO order unintentionally in multiple inheritance.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfaces. Exceptions - Handle a Single Exception
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interfaces. Exceptions - Handle a Single Exception

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Interfaces in Python

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An **Interface** in Python is a formal contract that defines a set of method signatures (and optionally properties) that any implementing class must provide. Python implements interfaces primarily through two mechanisms: (a) **Abstract Base Classes (ABCs)** using the `abc` module ŌĆö a nominal-typing interface where a class explicitly registers its inheritance, and (b) **Protocols** from the `typing` module (PEP 544) ŌĆö a structural-typing interface where compatibility is inferred by the presence of methods, not by inheritance. A third, informal mechanism is **Duck Typing**, where the suitability of an object is determined solely by the presence of methods/attributes at runtime.

$$ \text{Interface}(I) = \{ m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n \mid \forall c \in \text{Implementations}(I), \, c \text{ provides } m_i \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Unlike Java or C#, Python does **not** have a dedicated `interface` keyword. The closest equivalent is the `abc.ABC` (Abstract Base Class) class combined with `@abstractmethod` decorator. Always mention `abc` module in exam answers for full marks.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Power Socket" Analogy:**
Think of an interface as a **universal power socket specification**. The socket *defines the shape and voltage* (the contract ŌĆö "you must accept a 3-pin plug at 230V"). Any appliance (implementing class) ŌĆö whether it is a laptop charger, a fan, or a TV ŌĆö must conform to this shape to "fit in." Python's ABC is like a *labelled* socket (you must explicitly register your appliance as "compatible"), while a Protocol is like an *unlabelled* socket (any appliance with the correct plug shape works, regardless of brand).

### 1.2 Exceptions & Handling a Single Exception

**Formal Definition:**
An **Exception** is a runtime event that disrupts the normal flow of program execution, raised when the interpreter encounters an error (e.g., division by zero, type mismatch, file not found). **Handling a Single Exception** refers to the specific `try...except` construct in Python that catches **one** pre-specified exception type and executes a recovery block, preventing program termination.

$$ \text{Execution Flow} = \begin{cases} \text{Normal path} & \text{if no exception raised in \texttt{try}} \\ \text{Recovery path} & \text{if exception matches \texttt{except} type} \\ \text{Termination} & \text{if exception is uncaught} \end{cases} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition ŌĆö Handle a Single Exception:**
> The `try...except` block with **exactly one `except` clause** targeting **one specific exception class** is what KTU refers to as "handling a single exception." This is the foundational building block before moving to multiple-exception handling and `finally` blocks.

**Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Safety Net" Analogy:**
Imagine a tightrope walker (the main program). The `try` block is the *high-wire act*. The `except` block is the **safety net below**, designed to catch *only a specific type of fall* (say, "losing balance to the left"). If a different kind of fall occurs, the net won't catch it ŌĆö the program still crashes. This specificity is what "single exception handling" means: one net, one designed failure mode.

> [!TIP]
> **Default Exception Handlers:** Python provides built-in exception classes like `ZeroDivisionError`, `ValueError`, `TypeError`, `IndexError`, `KeyError`, `FileNotFoundError`, and the base `Exception`. Knowing these by name is essential for KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Interfaces ŌĆö Operational Breakdown

**Step-by-Step Logic of Interface Creation via ABC:**

1. **Import the `abc` module** ŌĆö This brings in `ABC` (the base metaclass for abstract classes) and `abstractmethod` (the decorator that marks a method as abstract).
2. **Subclass `ABC`** ŌĆö Declaring a class as a child of `abc.ABC` makes it abstract. You cannot instantiate it directly; attempting `MyInterface()` raises `TypeError`.
3. **Decorate methods with `@abstractmethod`** ŌĆö Each decorated method defines a contract. Subclasses **must override** every abstract method, otherwise they too become abstract and uninstantiable.
4. **Concrete subclasses provide implementation** ŌĆö The implementing class inherits from the ABC and provides full definitions for all abstract methods.
5. **Runtime check via `isinstance()`** ŌĆö Once a concrete class implements the interface, its instances pass `isinstance(obj, InterfaceABC)` checks, enabling polymorphism.

**The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step:**

- **Why subclass `ABC`?** ŌĆö It uses Python's metaclass system (`ABCMeta`) to register the class as abstract, intercepting instantiation and enforcing the contract at object creation time.
- **Why `@abstractmethod`?** ŌĆö It marks methods as placeholders. Python's metaclass `ABCMeta.__call__` checks for unimplemented abstract methods before allowing instantiation.
- **Why use Protocols (`typing.Protocol`)?** ŌĆö They enable **structural subtyping** (PEP 544): a class is considered a subtype if it has the *shape* (methods/attributes) of the protocol, without explicit inheritance. This is Pythonic and aligns with the duck-typing philosophy.

### 2.2 Single Exception Handling ŌĆö Operational Breakdown

1. **Python enters the `try` block** ŌĆö Statements inside `try:` are executed sequentially.
2. **Normal execution path** ŌĆö If no exception is raised, the `except` block is **skipped entirely**, and control jumps to the first statement after the `except`.
3. **Exception-triggered path** ŌĆö If an exception is raised inside `try`, the interpreter immediately searches for a matching `except` clause based on the exception's class (using `isinstance` semantics).
4. **Single-match rule** ŌĆö With a single `except`, **only one exception type** is caught. If a *different* exception type is raised, it propagates upward (uncaught).
5. **Control transfer** ŌĆö Once the matching `except` block completes, execution continues normally after the entire `try...except` construct.

**The "Why" Behind Single Exception Handling:**

- **Granular error recovery:** Different errors need different recovery strategies. Single-exception blocks force the programmer to be explicit.
- **Avoiding bare `except:` pitfalls:** Catching a specific exception is safer than a catch-all, as it prevents masking unrelated bugs (e.g., catching `KeyboardInterrupt` accidentally).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Equation | Boundary Condition / Rule | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Abstract Base Class | `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod` | Must subclass `ABC` | Interface creation |
| Abstract Method | `@abstractmethod` | Must be overridden by subclass | Method contract |
| Instantiation Check | `isinstance(obj, InterfaceClass)` | Returns `True` only if fully implemented | Polymorphism |
| Protocol | `from typing import Protocol` | Structural typing ŌĆö no inheritance needed | Duck-typed interfaces |
| Single Exception Catch | `try: ... except SpecificError: ...` | Catches only `SpecificError` and subclasses | Targeted error recovery |
| Built-in Exceptions | `ZeroDivisionError`, `ValueError`, `TypeError`, `IndexError`, `KeyError`, `FileNotFoundError` | All subclass `Exception` | Common runtime errors |
| Base Exception Class | `Exception` (parent of most errors) | Use as last-resort catch | Generic fallback |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A single `except` clause matches the specified exception **and all its subclasses** (polymorphic matching). For example, `except ArithmeticError:` will catch both `ZeroDivisionError` and `OverflowError`.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Machine Learning Pipelines:** Interfaces standardize model classes (`fit()`, `predict()`, `transform()`) so that scikit-learn estimators are interchangeable. Single-exception handling catches data-loading errors (e.g., `FileNotFoundError`) without crashing long training jobs.
- **IoT Systems:** ABC interfaces define sensor contracts (temperature sensor, humidity sensor) ŌĆö every concrete sensor must implement `read_value()`. Single-exception handling recovers from sensor read timeouts (`TimeoutError`).
- **Web Backends:** Interface contracts ensure database adapters (`MySQL`, `PostgreSQL`) implement the same `connect()`, `query()`, `close()` methods. Single-exception handling returns user-friendly 500 responses on `ConnectionError`.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Interface Enforcement Mechanism

Let us trace the internal metaclass call when an abstract class is instantiated.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Class Declaration with ABC**

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
```

At import time, `abc.ABCMeta` (a metaclass) is loaded. `ABC` is a helper class whose metaclass is `ABCMeta`.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Abstract Class Definition**

```python
class Shape(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self):
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def perimeter(self):
        pass
```

The decorator `@abstractmethod` sets `area.__isabstractmethod__ = True`. The metaclass `ABCMeta` collects these into `Shape.__abstractmethods__` (a frozenset).

$$ \text{Shape.\_\_abstractmethods\_\_} = \{\text{"area"}, \text{"perimeter"}\} $$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Attempted Direct Instantiation**

```python
s = Shape()  # Raises TypeError
```

Internally, Python calls `type.__call__`, which delegates to `ABCMeta.__call__`. This method checks:

$$ \text{if } \text{cls.\_\_abstractmethods\_\_} \neq \emptyset: \text{raise TypeError} $$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TypeError message:} \quad & \text{"Can't instantiate abstract class Shape} \\
& \text{with abstract methods area, perimeter"}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Concrete Subclass Implementation**

```python
class Circle(Shape):
    def __init__(self, r):
        self.r = r

    def area(self):
        return 3.14159 * self.r ** 2

    def perimeter(self):
        return 2 * 3.14159 * self.r
```

Here, `Circle.area` and `Circle.perimeter` have `__isabstractmethod__ = False`. The metaclass rebuilds `Circle.__abstractmethods__`:

$$ \text{Circle.\_\_abstractmethods\_\_} = \emptyset \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{instantiation allowed} $$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Polymorphic Type Check**

```python
c = Circle(5)
print(isinstance(c, Shape))   # True
print(isinstance(c, Circle))  # True
```

The check holds because `Circle` is a registered subclass of `Shape` via inheritance.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Single Exception Handling Flow

Consider the following program:

```python
try:
    numerator = int(input("Enter numerator: "))
    denominator = int(input("Enter denominator: "))
    result = numerator / denominator
    print("Result:", result)
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Error: Cannot divide by zero.")
```

**Execution Trace ŌĆö Case 1: No Exception**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Python enters \texttt{try} block.} \\
&\text{Step 2: \texttt{int(input())} executes normally, e.g., numerator=10, denominator=2.} \\
&\text{Step 3: \texttt{10 / 2 = 5.0} ŌĆö no exception raised.} \\
&\text{Step 4: \texttt{print("Result:", 5.0)} executes.} \\
&\text{Step 5: \texttt{except ZeroDivisionError} block is SKIPPED (no match).} \\
&\text{Step 6: Program terminates normally.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Execution Trace ŌĆö Case 2: ZeroDivisionError Raised**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Python enters \texttt{try} block.} \\
&\text{Step 2: \texttt{int(input())} returns numerator=10, denominator=0.} \\
&\text{Step 3: \texttt{10 / 0} triggers \texttt{ZeroDivisionError: division by zero}.} \\
&\text{Step 4: Python unwinds the \texttt{try} block (no further statements execute).} \\
&\text{Step 5: Python checks \texttt{except ZeroDivisionError} ŌĆö match found!} \\
&\text{Step 6: \texttt{except} block executes: prints "Error: Cannot divide by zero."} \\
&\text{Step 7: Program continues normally after the \texttt{try...except} construct.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Execution Trace ŌĆö Case 3: Different Exception (e.g., ValueError)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Python enters \texttt{try} block.} \\
&\text{Step 2: User types "abc" for numerator. \texttt{int("abc")} raises \texttt{ValueError}.} \\
&\text{Step 3: Python searches \texttt{except} clauses ŌĆö only \texttt{ZeroDivisionError} is registered.} \\
&\text{Step 4: \texttt{ValueError} is NOT a subclass of \texttt{ZeroDivisionError}, so NO match.} \\
&\text{Step 5: Exception propagates upward ŌĆö program CRASHES with unhandled \texttt{ValueError}.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Exceptions & Their Triggers

| Exception Class | Trigger Condition | Common Scenario | Inherits From |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ZeroDivisionError` | Division or modulo by zero | `10 / 0` | `ArithmeticError` |
| `ValueError` | Correct type, inappropriate value | `int("abc")` | `Exception` |
| `TypeError` | Operation applied to wrong type | `"hello" + 5` | `Exception` |
| `IndexError` | Sequence index out of range | `[1,2,3][10]` | `LookupError` |
| `KeyError` | Dict key not found | `{"a":1}["b"]` | `LookupError` |
| `FileNotFoundError` | File path does not exist | `open("missing.txt")` | `OSError` |
| `NameError` | Variable not defined | `print(undefined)` | `Exception` |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Interface Enforcement Flow (ABC Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Python loads abc module"] --> B["ABCMeta metaclass registered"]
    B --> C["Define Shape class inheriting ABC"]
    C --> D["Mark methods with abstractmethod decorator"]
    D --> E["Shape abstractmethods set populated"]
    E --> F{"Attempt Shape instantiation"}
    F -- Yes --> G["ABCMeta call intercepts"]
    G --> H["Check abstractmethods empty?"]
    H -- No --> I["Raise TypeError abstract class"]
    H -- Yes --> J["Object created successfully"]
    F -- No --> K["Define Circle subclass"]
    K --> L["Override all abstract methods"]
    L --> M["Circle abstractmethods set empty"]
    M --> N["Instantiate Circle allowed"]
    N --> O["isinstance check passes"]
```

### 4.2 Single Exception Handling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Program enters try block"] --> B["Execute statements sequentially"]
    B --> C{"Exception raised?"}
    C -- No --> D["Skip except block entirely"]
    D --> E["Continue execution after construct"]
    C -- Yes --> F["Identify exception type and instance"]
    F --> G{"Matches except SingleException?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Execute recovery block in except"]
    H --> I["Program continues normally"]
    G -- No --> J["Exception propagates upward"]
    J --> K["Unhandled exception crashes program"]
    K --> L["Traceback printed to stderr"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Interface + Exception in ML Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERFACE_LAYER["Interface Contract Layer"]
        I1["Abstract Base Model ABC"]
        I2["Abstract Method fit X y"]
        I3["Abstract Method predict X"]
    end

    subgraph IMPLEMENTATION_LAYER["Concrete Implementation Layer"]
        C1["LinearRegression implements fit and predict"]
        C2["DecisionTree implements fit and predict"]
        C3["NeuralNetwork implements fit and predict"]
    end

    subgraph EXCEPTION_LAYER["Single Exception Handler Layer"]
        E1["try block model training and prediction"]
        E2["except ValueError print data format error"]
        E3["except FileNotFoundError print dataset missing"]
    end

    I1 --> I2
    I1 --> I3
    I2 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I2 --> C3
    I3 --> C1
    I3 --> C2
    I3 --> C3
    C1 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E1 --> E3
```

### 4.4 Exception Class Hierarchy (Relevant Subset)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["BaseException"] --> B["Exception"]
    A --> C["KeyboardInterrupt"]
    A --> D["SystemExit"]
    B --> E["ArithmeticError"]
    B --> F["LookupError"]
    B --> G["ValueError"]
    B --> H["TypeError"]
    B --> I["FileNotFoundError"]
    E --> J["ZeroDivisionError"]
    F --> K["IndexError"]
    F --> L["KeyError"]
    I --> M["OSError"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1:** Define an interface in Python. Name the module used to create formal interfaces. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
An interface in Python is a formal contract that defines a set of methods that implementing classes must provide. It specifies *what* methods should exist, not *how* they are implemented. Python uses the `abc` (Abstract Base Classes) module to create formal interfaces. The `ABC` class is subclassed, and methods are decorated with `@abstractmethod`. Additionally, the `typing.Protocol` class (PEP 544) enables structural typing interfaces without explicit inheritance. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2:** What is an exception in Python? Give two examples of built-in exceptions. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An exception is a runtime error event that disrupts the normal flow of program execution. When raised, it propagates up the call stack until caught by a `try...except` handler or crashes the program. Examples: (i) `ZeroDivisionError` ŌĆö raised when dividing by zero (e.g., `5/0`), and (ii) `ValueError` ŌĆö raised when a function receives an argument of correct type but inappropriate value (e.g., `int("abc")`). **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Interfaces**

**(a)** Explain the concept of an interface in Python. Using the `abc` module, write a Python program to define an abstract class `Vehicle` with abstract methods `start()` and `stop()`, and implement two concrete subclasses `Car` and `Bike`. Demonstrate polymorphism using `isinstance()`. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Vehicle(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def start(self):
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def stop(self):
        pass

class Car(Vehicle):
    def start(self):
        return "Car engine started."

    def stop(self):
        return "Car engine stopped."

class Bike(Vehicle):
    def start(self):
        return "Bike kick-started."

    def stop(self):
        return "Bike engine switched off."

# Polymorphism demonstration
vehicles = [Car(), Bike()]
for v in vehicles:
    print(v.start())
    print(v.stop())
    print("Is Vehicle instance:", isinstance(v, Vehicle))
```

**Output:**
```
Car engine started.
Car engine stopped.
Is Vehicle instance: True
Bike kick-started.
Bike engine switched off.
Is Vehicle instance: True
```

**Explanation (7 marks breakdown):**
- Import and ABC inheritance: **1 mark**
- `@abstractmethod` decorator usage: **1 mark**
- `Car` and `Bike` overriding both abstract methods: **2 marks**
- Polymorphic iteration and `isinstance` check: **2 marks**
- Correct output and explanation: **1 mark**

---

**(b)** Differentiate between Abstract Base Classes (ABCs) and Protocols (`typing.Protocol`) in Python. Provide a code example for each. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | ABC (`abc.ABC`) | Protocol (`typing.Protocol`) |
|---|---|---|
| Typing Style | Nominal (explicit inheritance) | Structural (duck-typing based) |
| Inheritance Required | Yes ŌĆö must subclass `ABC` | No ŌĆö class just needs matching methods |
| Import | `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod` | `from typing import Protocol` |
| Enforcement | Metaclass checks at instantiation | Static type checker (`mypy`) checks |
| Use Case | Strict contracts, framework design | Flexible interfaces, library compatibility |

**ABC Example:**

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Drawable(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def draw(self):
        pass

class Circle(Drawable):
    def draw(self):
        print("Drawing circle")

c = Circle()
c.draw()
```

**Protocol Example:**

```python
from typing import Protocol

class Drawable(Protocol):
    def draw(self) -> None: ...

class Square:  # No inheritance!
    def draw(self) -> None:
        print("Drawing square")

def render(obj: Drawable) -> None:
    obj.draw()

render(Square())  # Works due to structural typing
```

**Explanation (7 marks breakdown):**
- Differences table with 4+ criteria: **3 marks**
- ABC working example: **2 marks**
- Protocol working example: **2 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Handle a Single Exception**

**(a)** Explain the `try...except` block in Python for handling a single exception. Write a program that reads two integers from the user and performs division, handling only the `ZeroDivisionError`. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The `try...except` block is Python's primary mechanism for runtime error handling. The `try` block contains code that *might* raise an exception. The `except` block specifies a **single exception type** to catch. If the exception inside `try` matches the `except` type (or is a subclass of it), the `except` block executes; otherwise, the exception propagates.

```python
try:
    num = int(input("Enter numerator: "))
    den = int(input("Enter denominator: "))
    result = num / den
    print("Result:", result)
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Error: Division by zero is not allowed.")
```

**Execution Flow Explanation:**
- If `den = 0`, `num / den` raises `ZeroDivisionError`. Python searches for a matching `except`, finds it, and runs the error message instead of crashing.
- If `den ŌēĀ 0`, division succeeds, result prints, and the `except` block is bypassed entirely.

**Explanation (7 marks breakdown):**
- Conceptual explanation of `try...except`: **2 marks**
- Complete program with single exception: **3 marks**
- Flow analysis (normal + error case): **2 marks**

---

**(b)** Write a Python program that accesses an element from a list using an index provided by the user. Handle only the `IndexError` using a single `except` block. What happens if a `ValueError` occurs? Explain. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

```python
numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

try:
    idx = int(input("Enter index (0-4): "))
    print("Element:", numbers[idx])
except IndexError:
    print("Error: Index out of valid range.")
```

**Explanation for ValueError scenario:**

If the user enters `"abc"` instead of a number, `int("abc")` raises a `ValueError`. Since the `except` clause only specifies `IndexError`, the `ValueError` does **not match** (it is not a subclass of `IndexError`). Therefore, the `except` block is **not executed**, and the `ValueError` propagates upward, causing the program to terminate with an unhandled exception traceback.

**Key Insight (KTU-Favorite Question Pattern):**
This question tests whether students understand that a **single `except` block catches only ONE exception type** and its subclasses. To handle `ValueError` as well, a second `except` clause must be added (covered in the next topic: *Handle Multiple Exceptions*).

**Explanation (7 marks breakdown):**
- Correct program with `IndexError` handling: **3 marks**
- ValueError scenario explanation: **2 marks**
- Conclusion on single-exception limitation: **2 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `@abstractmethod`:** Students often define a class with `ABC` parent but forget the `@abstractmethod` decorator on methods. This means the class is *technically* abstract (cannot instantiate) but the methods are NOT enforced as contracts. Always decorate methods.
> 2. **Misspelling exception class names:** Writing `except ZeroDivisionError:` as `except ZeroDivisionErrror:` (typo) will silently fail to catch the exception, and the program will crash. KTU checkers look for exact spelling.
> 3. **Putting the `except` BEFORE the `try`:** Syntax error! Always `try` first, then `except`.
> 4. **Catching the wrong exception:** `except Exception:` catches almost everything and is considered bad practice for single-exception handling. KTU prefers specific exception types.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interface in Python** = contract of methods, enforced via `abc.ABC` + `@abstractmethod` or via `typing.Protocol`.
- **ABC = nominal typing** (explicit `class X(ABC)` inheritance required).
- **Protocol = structural typing** (no inheritance, methods must just match).
- **Abstract class cannot be instantiated**; attempting `Shape()` raises `TypeError`.
- **Concrete subclasses MUST override ALL abstract methods** or remain abstract.
- **`isinstance(obj, ABC)`** returns `True` only for fully implemented subclasses.
- **Exception** = runtime error event disrupting normal flow.
- **Single exception handling** = `try...except SpecificException:` ŌĆö catches ONE type and its subclasses.
- **If exception type doesn't match**, it propagates upward and crashes the program (unless outer `try...except` catches it).
- **Common built-in exceptions:** `ZeroDivisionError`, `ValueError`, `TypeError`, `IndexError`, `KeyError`, `FileNotFoundError`, `NameError`.
- **All built-in exceptions inherit from `Exception`** (except `SystemExit`, `KeyboardInterrupt`, `GeneratorExit`).
- **`except ArithmeticError:` catches `ZeroDivisionError`, `OverflowError`, `FloatingPointError`** ŌĆö polymorphic catch.
- **Flow when no exception:** `try` block runs fully, `except` block is skipped.
- **Flow when exception matches:** `try` block halts immediately, `except` block runs, program continues.
- **Flow when exception doesn't match:** `try` block halts, `except` is skipped, exception propagates ŌåÆ crash.
- **Real-world use:** ML model interfaces, IoT sensor contracts, database adapters, web API error recovery.
- **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always specify the module name (`abc`) and decorator (`@abstractmethod`) in interface answers to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handle Multiple Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handle Multiple Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In Python, **Exception Handling** is a robust programming construct designed to gracefully manage runtime errors (anomalies) that disrupt the normal flow of a program. **Handling Multiple Exceptions** is the advanced extension of this construct where a single `try` block is paired with multiple `except` clauses (or a tuple-based `except` clause) to anticipate and recover from **distinct categories of runtime faults** ŌĆö such as `ZeroDivisionError`, `ValueError`, `TypeError`, `FileNotFoundError`, or generic `Exception` ŌĆö without terminating the program abruptly.

This aligns directly with the KTU 2024 Scheme **Course Outcome (CO3)** of the PEERT526 syllabus: *"Illustrate robust Python programming using control structures, functions, and exception handling mechanisms suitable for machine learning pipelines."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The topic *Handle Multiple Exceptions* sits within the broader unit of **Exception Handling & File Operations**. Students are expected to demonstrate competency in writing resilient ML preprocessing scripts that do not crash on bad input data (e.g., NaN values, malformed CSV rows, missing labels).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hospital emergency ward (ER)**:

- A patient walks in. The doctor first **screens** the patient (this is the `try` block).
- The doctor then **triage-sorts** the patient based on the ailment:
  - If it's a **heart attack** ŌåÆ route to the **Cardiologist** (`except CardiologyError`).
  - If it's a **broken bone** ŌåÆ route to the **Orthopedic surgeon** (`except BoneFractureError`).
  - If it's something **unknown / generic** ŌåÆ route to the **General Physician** (`except Exception`).
- If the patient is **healthy** (no error) ŌåÆ send them home with a **wellness check** (`else` block).
- Regardless of what happened, the **hospital bills are always generated** (`finally` block).

In Python, a single `try` block performs an action that *might* fail, and multiple `except` clauses act as specialized **triage stations** ŌĆö each designed to catch a specific exception type and respond appropriately. This prevents one error from cascading and crashing the entire program.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World ML Context:** When loading a 10 GB CSV dataset, a single malformed row can crash your training job. Multiple exception handling ensures the pipeline **skips bad rows**, logs them, and **continues training** ŌĆö exactly what production-grade ML demands.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

There are **no physical constants** in this topic. However, the following **standard Python exception hierarchy metrics** are critical to memorize:

- **Built-in exception base class:** `BaseException` (the root of all exceptions)
- **Most commonly used exception class:** `Exception` (parent of almost all user-relevant errors)
- **Standard hierarchy depth:** `BaseException` ŌåÆ `Exception` ŌåÆ specific errors (e.g., `ArithmeticError` ŌåÆ `ZeroDivisionError`)

> [!TIP]
> **Order Matters Rule:** Always catch **specific exceptions FIRST**, then the **generic `Exception`** last. Reversing this order causes a `SyntaxError` at compile-time because the generic handler would shadow the specific ones ŌĆö a classic KTU board pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### The Operational Concept Decomposed

Python's multiple exception handling operates on **four architectural patterns**. Each pattern solves a different programming need:

#### Pattern 1: Multiple Distinct `except` Blocks (Vertical Pattern)

This is the **most explicit and KTU-favored pattern**. Each exception type has its own dedicated handler block, allowing custom recovery logic per error.

```python
try:
    # risky code
    numerator = int(input("Enter numerator: "))
    denominator = int(input("Enter denominator: "))
    result = numerator / denominator
except ValueError:
    print("Error: Please enter integers only.")
except ZeroDivisionError:
    print("Error: Denominator cannot be zero.")
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Error: Input cancelled by user.")
```

**Why this pattern?**
- Maximum clarity for the reader.
- Each error gets a **bespoke message** and **bespoke recovery strategy** (e.g., retry, log, default value).
- KTU examiners reward this pattern with full marks because it shows **explicit understanding**.

#### Pattern 2: Single `except` with a Tuple (Horizontal Pattern)

When multiple exception types require **identical handling logic**, bundling them into a tuple avoids code duplication.

```python
try:
    value = int(input("Enter index: "))
    item = my_list[value]
except (ValueError, IndexError, TypeError) as e:
    print(f"Input/Index Error occurred: {e}")
```

**Why this pattern?**
- **DRY principle** (Don't Repeat Yourself).
- Concise, but loses granular error-specific messages.

#### Pattern 3: Generic `except Exception` Catch-All

Used as a **safety net** to catch any unanticipated error. The variable `as e` binds the exception instance for introspection.

```python
try:
    file = open("data.csv", "r")
    contents = file.read()
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("File missing ŌĆö using default dataset.")
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Unexpected error: {type(e).__name__} ŌåÆ {e}")
```

#### Pattern 4: The Full `try-except-else-finally` Quad-Block

This is the **complete professional pattern** that KTU 2024 expects for **14-mark questions**.

```python
try:
    file = open("dataset.csv", "r")
    data = file.read()
except FileNotFoundError:
    print("Dataset not found. Downloading fallback...")
    data = ""
except PermissionError:
    print("Permission denied. Aborting.")
    data = None
else:
    print(f"Dataset loaded successfully. Size: {len(data)} bytes.")
finally:
    print("Cleanup complete. Closing any open resources.")
```

| Block | Executes When | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `try` | Always entered first | Houses the **risky/guarded** code |
| `except TypeA` | `TypeA` exception raised | **Specific recovery** for Type A |
| `except TypeB` | `TypeB` exception raised | **Specific recovery** for Type B |
| `except (C, D)` | Either `C` or `D` raised | **Grouped recovery** for similar errors |
| `except Exception` | Any non-system error | **Safety-net** catch-all |
| `else` | `try` succeeded (no exception) | **Success-path** logic |
| `finally` | **Always** (error or not) | **Cleanup** (close files, release locks) |

### KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Syntax Element | Purpose | Exam Priority |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `try:` | Begin guarded block | **Critical** |
| 2 | `except ErrorType:` | Catch one specific error | **Critical** |
| 3 | `except (E1, E2):` | Catch multiple errors, same handler | **High** |
| 4 | `except ErrorType as e:` | Bind exception to variable `e` | **Critical** |
| 5 | `except Exception:` | Catch-all for non-system errors | **High** |
| 6 | `else:` | Run only on success | **Moderate** |
| 7 | `finally:` | Always-run cleanup | **Critical** |
| 8 | `raise` | Re-raise caught exception | **Moderate** |
| 9 | `except BaseException:` | Catches even `SystemExit` (avoid) | **Low** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In Machine Learning pipelines, this construct is used in:
> - **Data Ingestion:** Handling missing files, corrupt CSVs.
> - **Data Preprocessing:** Catching `ValueError` when `pd.to_numeric()` fails on a string column.
> - **Model Inference:** Catching `KeyError` when a feature is missing at prediction time.
> - **API Calls:** Catching `ConnectionError` / `Timeout` during model fetch from cloud.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

### Example 1: Multi-Exception Handling in ML Data Loading

**Problem Statement:** Write a Python function `safe_divide(a, b)` that handles both `ZeroDivisionError` and `TypeError`, returns `None` on failure, and prints the result on success. Demonstrate with multiple test cases.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```python
def safe_divide(a, b):
    """
    Safely divides a by b, handling multiple exception types.
    Returns the quotient on success, None on failure.
    """
    try:
        # Step 1: Risky operation (division may fail in multiple ways)
        result = a / b
    
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        # Step 2a: Caught when b == 0
        print(f"[ERROR] Cannot divide {a} by zero.")
        return None
    
    except TypeError:
        # Step 2b: Caught when a or b is non-numeric (e.g., str)
        print(f"[ERROR] Non-numeric operand detected: a={type(a).__name__}, b={type(b).__name__}")
        return None
    
    except Exception as e:
        # Step 2c: Safety net for any unforeseen error
        print(f"[UNEXPECTED] {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
        return None
    
    else:
        # Step 3: Runs ONLY if try succeeded (no exception)
        print(f"[SUCCESS] {a} / {b} = {result}")
        return result
    
    finally:
        # Step 4: ALWAYS runs ŌĆö used for logging, cleanup
        print(f"[LOG] safe_divide({a}, {b}) execution finished.\n")


# --- Test Cases ---
print("Test 1: Valid integers")
safe_divide(10, 2)

print("Test 2: Division by zero")
safe_divide(10, 0)

print("Test 3: Non-numeric input")
safe_divide("hello", 5)

print("Test 4: Valid floats")
safe_divide(7.5, 2.5)
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Test 1: Valid integers
[SUCCESS] 10 / 2 = 5.0
[LOG] safe_divide(10, 2) execution finished.

Test 2: Division by zero
[ERROR] Cannot divide 10 by zero.
[LOG] safe_divide(10, 0) execution finished.

Test 3: Non-numeric input
[ERROR] Non-numeric operand detected: a=str, b=int
[LOG] safe_divide("hello", 5) execution finished.

Test 4: Valid floats
[SUCCESS] 7.5 / 2.5 = 3.0
[LOG] safe_divide(7.5, 2.5) execution finished.
```

---

### Example 2: Tuple-Based Grouped Exception Handling (ML Use Case)

**Problem Statement:** Load and parse a column from a CSV. Handle `FileNotFoundError`, `PermissionError`, `KeyError`, and `ValueError` using tuple grouping where appropriate.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

```python
import csv

def load_column(filepath, column_name):
    try:
        with open(filepath, "r") as f:
            reader = csv.DictReader(f)
            column_data = [row[column_name] for row in reader]
        return column_data
    
    except FileNotFoundError:
        print(f"[ERROR] File '{filepath}' does not exist.")
        return []
    
    except PermissionError:
        print(f"[ERROR] Permission denied for '{filepath}'.")
        return []
    
    except (KeyError, ValueError) as e:
        # Grouped handler: both indicate malformed data structure
        print(f"[ERROR] Column issue in '{filepath}': {type(e).__name__} ŌåÆ {e}")
        return []
    
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[UNEXPECTED] {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
        return []
    
    else:
        print(f"[SUCCESS] Loaded {len(column_data)} rows from '{filepath}'.")
    
    finally:
        print("[LOG] Column loading operation completed.\n")


# Test cases
load_column("missing.csv", "age")
load_column("/root/protected.csv", "age")
load_column("data.csv", "nonexistent_column")
load_column("valid_data.csv", "age")
```

---

### Example 3: Nested `try-except` for Multi-Layer ML Pipelines

```python
def train_model(data_path, epochs):
    try:
        # Outer layer: file access
        try:
            with open(data_path, "r") as f:
                lines = f.readlines()
        except (FileNotFoundError, PermissionError) as e:
            print(f"[LAYER 1] File access failed: {e}")
            return
        
        # Inner layer: data validation
        try:
            epochs = int(epochs)
            if epochs <= 0:
                raise ValueError("Epochs must be positive.")
        except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:
            print(f"[LAYER 2] Invalid epoch parameter: {e}")
            return
        
        print(f"[TRAINING] Starting training on {len(lines)} samples for {epochs} epochs.")
    
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[FATAL] Unexpected error: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
    
    finally:
        print("[CLEANUP] Releasing GPU memory and closing log files.")


train_model("train.csv", 10)        # Valid
train_model("missing.csv", 10)     # Layer 1 error
train_model("train.csv", "abc")    # Layer 2 error
train_model("train.csv", -5)       # Layer 2 ValueError (manually raised)
```

---

### Example 4: Raising Custom Exceptions in ML Context

```python
class InvalidShapeError(Exception):
    """Custom exception for tensor shape mismatches in ML."""
    pass

def validate_tensor_shape(tensor_shape, expected_shape):
    try:
        if len(tensor_shape) != len(expected_shape):
            raise InvalidShapeError(
                f"Rank mismatch: got {len(tensor_shape)}D, expected {len(expected_shape)}D."
            )
        for dim, exp in zip(tensor_shape, expected_shape):
            if dim != exp:
                raise InvalidShapeError(
                    f"Dimension mismatch: got {dim}, expected {exp}."
                )
    except InvalidShapeError as e:
        print(f"[SHAPE ERROR] {e}")
        return False
    else:
        print(f"[OK] Tensor shape {tensor_shape} matches expected {expected_shape}.")
        return True
    finally:
        print("[LOG] Shape validation complete.")


validate_tensor_shape((32, 224, 224, 3), (32, 224, 224, 3))   # OK
validate_tensor_shape((32, 224), (32, 224, 224, 3))            # Rank mismatch
validate_tensor_shape((32, 128, 224, 3), (32, 224, 224, 3))    # Dim mismatch
```

---

### Analytical Matrix: Common ML Exceptions & Their Handling Strategy

| Exception | Common ML Trigger | Recommended Handler Block |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `FileNotFoundError` | Missing dataset path | Log error, load fallback dataset |
| `PermissionError` | Restricted directory access | Abort with user-friendly message |
| `ValueError` | NaN in numeric conversion | Skip row, append to error log |
| `KeyError` | Missing feature column | Impute with default / mean |
| `TypeError` | Str passed to float() | Coerce using `pd.to_numeric(errors='coerce')` |
| `ZeroDivisionError` | Normalization by zero sum | Return zero vector |
| `MemoryError` | Dataset > RAM | Load in chunks using `pd.read_csv(chunksize=)` |
| `ImportError` | Missing library (e.g., `torch`) | `pip install` suggestion |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Exception Handling Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program enters try block] --> B{Risky operation executes}
    B -->|No exception raised| C[Execute else block]
    C --> D[Execute finally block]
    D --> E[Continue normal program flow]
    
    B -->|Exception raised| F{Which exception type?}
    F -->|TypeA match| G[Handle TypeA in except block 1]
    F -->|TypeB match| H[Handle TypeB in except block 2]
    F -->|TypeC or TypeD match| I[Handle grouped tuple exception]
    F -->|No specific match| J[Handle in generic Exception block]
    
    G --> D
    H --> D
    I --> D
    J --> D
```

### Diagram 2: Exception Class Hierarchy (Python Built-ins)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BaseException] --> B[Exception]
    A --> C[SystemExit]
    A --> D[KeyboardInterrupt]
    A --> E[GeneratorExit]
    
    B --> F[ArithmeticError]
    B --> G[LookupError]
    B --> H[TypeError]
    B --> I[ValueError]
    B --> J[OSError]
    B --> K[RuntimeError]
    
    F --> L[ZeroDivisionError]
    F --> M[OverflowError]
    
    G --> N[IndexError]
    G --> O[KeyError]
    
    J --> P[FileNotFoundError]
    J --> Q[PermissionError]
```

### Diagram 3: ML Pipeline Exception Mapping (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: Data Ingestion]
        S1[Load CSV / API call] --> S1Check{File exists?}
        S1Check -->|No| E1[except FileNotFoundError]
        S1Check -->|Yes| S1Parse
        E1 --> Fallback[Load synthetic fallback]
        Fallback --> S1Parse
        S1Parse[Parse rows] --> S1TypeCheck{Numeric?}
        S1TypeCheck -->|No| E2[except ValueError ŌåÆ coerce]
        E2 --> Clean
        S1TypeCheck -->|Yes| Clean[Cleaned DataFrame]
    end
    
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2: Feature Engineering]
        Clean --> S2[Compute features] --> S2Check{Key exists?}
        S2Check -->|No| E3[except KeyError ŌåÆ impute mean]
        S2Check -->|Yes| S2Scale
        E3 --> S2Scale[Scaled features]
        S2Scale --> S2Div[Normalize rows] --> S2DivCheck{Std is zero?}
        S2DivCheck -->|Yes| E4[except ZeroDivisionError ŌåÆ return zeros]
        S2DivCheck -->|No| Output
        E4 --> Output[Feature matrix X]
    end
    
    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3: Model Training - finally block always runs]
        Output --> Train[Train model] --> FinalLog[Log metrics & release memory]
    end
    
    Stage1 --> Stage2
    Stage2 --> Stage3
```

### Diagram 4: Decision Tree ŌĆö Which Exception Pattern to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need different recovery logic per error?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| P1[Pattern 1: Multiple except blocks]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{How many error types share the same handler?}
    Q2 -->|One| P2[Pattern 2a: Single except]
    Q2 -->|Multiple| P3[Pattern 2b: except Type1, Type2 as e]
    
    Q3{Need a catch-all safety net?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P4[Pattern 3: Append except Exception at end]
    Q3 -->|No| P5[Skip catch-all]
    
    P1 --> Q3
    P2 --> Q3
    P3 --> Q3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is the purpose of handling multiple exceptions in Python? Name any two built-in exception types.*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
Handling multiple exceptions allows a program to anticipate and recover from **different categories of runtime errors** within a single `try` block, preventing abrupt termination. Each error type can have a **bespoke recovery strategy**.

Two built-in exception types:
1. **`ZeroDivisionError`** ŌĆö raised when dividing by zero.
2. **`ValueError`** ŌĆö raised when a function receives an argument of correct type but inappropriate value (e.g., `int("abc")`).

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 marks each for two examples)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Sample]**
*Differentiate between `except ValueError` and `except (ValueError, TypeError) as e`. When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**

| Aspect | `except ValueError` | `except (ValueError, TypeError) as e` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Catches | Only `ValueError` | Both `ValueError` AND `TypeError` |
| Use when | Errors need **different recovery** logic | Errors need the **same recovery** logic |
| Granularity | High | Low (bundled) |

**Preference rule:** Use the single-type form when each error demands a **unique user message or corrective action**. Use the tuple form when errors are **semantically equivalent** (e.g., both indicate bad input) and can share one response.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]

**(a)** Explain with a suitable Python code example how **multiple exceptions can be handled using separate `except` blocks**. Mention the rule regarding the order of `except` clauses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program that reads a list of integers from the user. The program must handle the following exceptions separately and demonstrate the use of the `else` and `finally` clauses:
- `ValueError` (when input is not an integer)
- `KeyboardInterrupt` (when user cancels input via Ctrl+C)
- A generic `Exception` catch-all for any other error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

When a single `try` block can raise multiple exception types, Python allows pairing it with **multiple `except` clauses**, each targeting a specific error.

**Code Example (5 marks):**

```python
def process_data(filename, divisor):
    try:
        with open(filename, "r") as f:
            data = f.read()
        number = int(data)
        result = 100 / divisor
        final = number + result
        return final
    
    except FileNotFoundError:
        print("Error: The specified file does not exist.")
        return -1
    
    except ValueError:
        print("Error: File contents are not a valid integer.")
        return -2
    
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        print("Error: Divisor cannot be zero.")
        return -3
    
    except TypeError:
        print("Error: Divisor must be numeric.")
        return -4
    
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Unexpected error: {type(e).__name__} ŌåÆ {e}")
        return -99


# Demonstration
print(process_data("missing.txt", 5))
print(process_data("data.txt", 0))
print(process_data("data.txt", "abc"))
```

**Order Rule (2 marks):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> `except` clauses are evaluated **top-to-bottom, in the order written**. Python executes the **first matching handler** and ignores the rest. Therefore:
> 1. **Specific exceptions** (e.g., `ZeroDivisionError`, `FileNotFoundError`) must come **first**.
> 2. **Generic exceptions** (e.g., `Exception`, `BaseException`) must come **last**.
> 3. Violating this order causes the specific handler to become **unreachable (dead code)**.

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

```python
def read_integer_list():
    numbers = []
    print("Enter integers one by one. Type 'done' to finish.")
    
    while True:
        try:
            user_input = input("Enter an integer (or 'done'): ")
            
            if user_input.lower() == 'done':
                break
            
            num = int(user_input)
            numbers.append(num)
        
        except ValueError:
            # Specific handler for non-integer input
            print(f"  [ValueError] '{user_input}' is not a valid integer. Try again.")
        
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            # Specific handler for Ctrl+C cancellation
            print("\n  [KeyboardInterrupt] Input cancelled by user.")
            break
        
        except Exception as e:
            # Generic safety net
            print(f"  [Exception] {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
        
        else:
            # Runs ONLY if try block succeeded
            print(f"  [Success] Added {num} to the list. Current count: {len(numbers)}")
        
        finally:
            # ALWAYS runs
            print(f"  [Finally] Iteration complete. List size: {len(numbers)}\n")
    
    return numbers


# Main execution
result = read_integer_list()
print(f"\nFinal list: {result}")
print(f"Sum of valid integers: {sum(result) if result else 0}")
```

**Sample Output Trace:**
```
Enter an integer (or 'done'): 10
  [Success] Added 10 to the list. Current count: 1
  [Finally] Iteration complete. List size: 1

Enter an integer (or 'done'): hello
  [ValueError] 'hello' is not a valid integer. Try again.
  [Finally] Iteration complete. List size: 1

Enter an integer (or 'done'): 20
  [Success] Added 20 to the list. Current count: 2
  [Finally] Iteration complete. List size: 2

Enter an integer (or 'done'): done
  [Finally] Iteration complete. List size: 2

Final list: [10, 20]
Sum of valid integers: 30
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **`except (ExceptionType1, ExceptionType2) as e`** syntax for grouped exception handling. Provide a real-world ML scenario where this pattern is preferred over separate `except` blocks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python function `safe_model_predict(model, input_data)` that demonstrates **nested `try-except` blocks** to handle: outer-level `FileNotFoundError` (loading the model file) and inner-level `ValueError` (invalid input shape). Include `else` and `finally` clauses. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B**

**Part (a) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

The syntax `except (ExceptionType1, ExceptionType2) as e` allows a **single handler block** to catch **multiple exception types** that share the same recovery logic. The variable `e` is bound to the exception instance for introspection via `str(e)` or `type(e).__name__`.

**Syntax Breakdown (2 marks):**
```python
try:
    risky_operation()
except (ErrorA, ErrorB, ErrorC) as e:
    print(f"Caught {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
    # unified recovery logic
```

**ML Real-World Scenario (3 marks):** Data Preprocessing Pipeline
When reading a numeric column from a pandas DataFrame, both `ValueError` (e.g., non-numeric string `"N/A"`) and `TypeError` (e.g., mixed `int` and `None`) indicate the same root cause: **malformed cell**. Using grouped handling avoids duplicating the imputation code:

```python
import pandas as pd

def clean_numeric_column(df, column_name):
    try:
        df[column_name] = pd.to_numeric(df[column_name], errors='raise')
    except (ValueError, TypeError) as e:
        print(f"[DATA CLEAN] Coercing bad cells in '{column_name}': {e}")
        df[column_name] = pd.to_numeric(df[column_name], errors='coerce')
        df[column_name].fillna(df[column_name].median(), inplace=True)
    return df
```

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Criterion | Separate `except` Blocks | Grouped Tuple `except` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Code length | Verbose (repeated logic) | Concise |
| Custom messaging per error | Ō£ģ Yes | ŌØī No (generic message) |
| Best for | Different recovery actions | Identical recovery actions |

---

**Part (b) ŌĆö 7 marks:**

```python
import pickle
import numpy as np

def safe_model_predict(model_path, input_data):
    """
    Loads a pickled ML model and makes a prediction.
    Uses nested try-except for layered error handling.
    """
    model = None
    prediction = None
    
    try:
        # OUTER layer: model loading
        try:
            with open(model_path, "rb") as f:
                model = pickle.load(f)
            print(f"[LAYER 1] Model loaded successfully from '{model_path}'.")
        
        except FileNotFoundError:
            print(f"[LAYER 1 ERROR] Model file not found: '{model_path}'.")
            print("  ŌåÆ Falling back to dummy predictor (returns zeros).")
            model = "DUMMY"
        
        except PermissionError:
            print(f"[LAYER 1 ERROR] Permission denied for '{model_path}'.")
            return None
        
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"[LAYER 1 UNEXPECTED] {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
            return None
        
        # INNER layer: prediction
        try:
            if model == "DUMMY":
                prediction = np.zeros(len(input_data))
            else:
                arr = np.array(input_data)
                if arr.ndim != 2:
                    raise ValueError(f"Expected 2D array, got {arr.ndim}D.")
                prediction = model.predict(arr)
        
        except ValueError as ve:
            print(f"[LAYER 2 ERROR] Invalid input shape: {ve}")
            prediction = None
        
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"[LAYER 2 UNEXPECTED] {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
            prediction = None
    
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"[FATAL] Top-level error: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")
    
    else:
        print(f"[SUCCESS] Prediction completed. Output shape: "
              f"{np.array(prediction).shape if prediction is not None else 'N/A'}")
    
    finally:
        print("[CLEANUP] Releasing model from memory. Closing file handles.")
    
    return prediction


# Test cases
print("=== Test 1: Valid model & input ===")
safe_model_predict("trained_model.pkl", [[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])

print("\n=== Test 2: Missing model file ===")
safe_model_predict("ghost_model.pkl", [[1.0, 2.0, 3.0]])

print("\n=== Test 3: Invalid input shape (1D) ===")
safe_model_predict("trained_model.pkl", [1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Mark Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **ŌØī Wrong order of `except` clauses:** Putting `except Exception` before specific exceptions ŌåÆ **2 marks deducted** (compile-time dead-code).
> 2. **ŌØī Confusing `else` with `finally`:** Writing success logic in `finally` ŌåÆ **1.5 marks deducted**.
> 3. **ŌØī Forgetting `as e` in tuple form:** Writing `except (A, B):` without binding the error ŌåÆ **1 mark deducted** (loses introspection capability).
> 4. **ŌØī Using `except:` (bare except):** This catches `BaseException` including `KeyboardInterrupt` and `SystemExit`, which is a **dangerous anti-pattern**. Examiners deduct **2 marks**.
> 5. **ŌØī Not demonstrating output:** Code-only answers without sample input/output traces lose **1ŌĆō2 marks** depending on the examiner.

---

### ­¤ōī Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Ō£ģ A single `try` block can be paired with **multiple `except` clauses** to handle different error types independently.
- Ō£ģ The syntax `except (E1, E2, E3) as e` **groups exceptions** sharing the same handler ŌĆö promotes DRY code.
- Ō£ģ The **`else` block** runs only if the `try` block succeeds (no exception). Use it for **success-path logic**.
- Ō£ģ The **`finally` block** runs **unconditionally** ŌĆö use it for **resource cleanup** (closing files, releasing memory).
- Ō£ģ The variable `as e` binds the exception instance; access `str(e)` for message, `type(e).__name__` for error class.
- Ō£ģ **Order rule:** Specific exceptions ŌåÆ first; Generic `Exception` ŌåÆ last. Reversing causes **unreachable code**.
- Ō£ģ **Avoid bare `except:`** ŌĆö it catches `BaseException` including system interrupts. Always specify at least `except Exception:`.
- Ō£ģ **`raise`** keyword re-raises the current exception ŌĆö useful in custom exception classes for ML validation.
- Ō£ģ Custom exceptions (e.g., `InvalidShapeError`) extend the `Exception` base class and integrate seamlessly with the standard handling flow.
- Ō£ģ **ML Pipeline Integration:** Multi-exception handling is essential for **robust data ingestion, preprocessing, and inference** ŌĆö production ML demands graceful failure.
- Ō£ģ **Examiners reward:** clear comments, sample output, and demonstrating **all four code paths** (success, specific error, generic error, finally always running).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The os and sys Modules. Introduction to File I/O - Reading and Writing Text Files
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT526 - PYTHON BASICS FOR MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The os and sys Modules. Introduction to File I/O - Reading and Writing Text Files

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The `os` Module ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> The **`os` module** in Python is a part of the **Python Standard Library** that provides a **portable interface** to interact with the **operating system** on which Python is running. It exposes a rich set of functions for **file system operations** (creating, removing, renaming, listing directories), **path manipulations**, **environment variable handling**, and **process management**.

In simple words, the `os` module is Python's **"universal remote control"** for talking to the underlying Operating System (Windows, Linux, macOS) without needing to write platform-specific code.

### 1.2 The `sys` Module ŌĆö Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> The **`sys` module** provides access to **Python interpreter-specific variables and functions** that interact strongly with the interpreter. It is used to manipulate the **Python runtime environment**, including command-line arguments, module search paths, standard I/O streams, and interpreter version information.

Think of `sys` as a **"dashboard inside the Python engine room"** ŌĆö it lets you peek at and tweak the interpreter's own settings, while `os` deals with the operating system outside Python.

### 1.3 Introduction to File I/O ŌĆö Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)**
> **File I/O (Input/Output)** refers to the mechanism by which a Python program **reads data from** and **writes data to** files stored on secondary memory (HDD/SSD). Text files store data as **human-readable Unicode/ASCII characters**, and Python handles them using the built-in `open()` function and file object methods such as `read()`, `write()`, `readline()`, and `readlines()`.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "The Restaurant"**
> - **`os` module** ŌåÆ The **building manager** of the restaurant. He knows where the storeroom is, can open new rooms, remove old ones, and inspects the building structure.
> - **`sys` module** ŌåÆ The **restaurant's head chef**'s personal notebook. It records the chef's own name (Python version), today's specials (`sys.path`), the order tickets passed in (`sys.argv`), and the rules to close the kitchen (`sys.exit()`).
> - **File I/O** ŌåÆ The **waiter** who takes your order (write), goes to the kitchen, and brings back food (read). The `open()` function is the **notebook** the waiter uses to record and retrieve orders.

### 1.5 Why These Modules Matter in Machine Learning

In ML pipelines (the focus of PEERT526), you will constantly:
- **Load datasets** from CSV/text files (`File I/O`)
- **Traverse folders** containing thousands of images (`os.listdir`, `os.walk`)
- **Build cross-platform scripts** that run on any OS (`os.path.join`)
- **Parse command-line arguments** when training models (`sys.argv`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics & Keywords (Bolded for Recall)**
> - **Path Separator** ŌĆö OS-dependent: `/` (Linux/macOS), `\` (Windows) ŌåÆ use `os.path.join()` instead
> - **Default Encoding** ŌĆö `utf-8` for text mode in Python 3
> - **Line Terminator** ŌĆö `\n` (Unix), `\r\n` (Windows)
> - **Buffer Size** ŌĆö Default **8192 bytes** for file buffering

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

### 2.1 The `os` Module ŌĆö Structural Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 File & Directory Operations

| Function | Purpose | Example Call |
|---|---|---|
| `os.getcwd()` | Returns Current Working Directory as a string | `os.getcwd()` |
| `os.chdir(path)` | Changes the CWD to `path` | `os.chdir("/home/user")` |
| `os.listdir(path)` | Lists all entries (files + dirs) in `path` | `os.listdir(".")` |
| `os.mkdir(path)` | Creates **single** directory; raises error if exists | `os.mkdir("data")` |
| `os.makedirs(path)` | Creates **recursive** directory tree | `os.makedirs("a/b/c")` |
| `os.remove(path)` | Deletes a **file** | `os.remove("old.txt")` |
| `os.rmdir(path)` | Deletes an **empty** directory | `os.rmdir("empty_dir")` |
| `os.rename(src, dst)` | Renames/moves file or directory | `os.rename("a.txt","b.txt")` |
| `os.removedirs(path)` | Recursively removes empty directories | `os.removedirs("a/b/c")` |
| `os.walk(path)` | Generator yielding `(root, dirs, files)` tuples | `for r,d,f in os.walk(".")` |

#### 2.1.2 `os.path` Submodule ŌĆö Path Manipulation Logic

| Function | Returns | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `os.path.join(a, b, ...)` | Concatenated path with correct separator | Cross-platform path building |
| `os.path.exists(path)` | `True` / `False` | Check existence |
| `os.path.isfile(path)` | `True` / `False` | Validate it's a file |
| `os.path.isdir(path)` | `True` / `False` | Validate it's a directory |
| `os.path.basename(path)` | Filename portion | `"/a/b/c.txt"` ŌåÆ `"c.txt"` |
| `os.path.dirname(path)` | Directory portion | `"/a/b/c.txt"` ŌåÆ `"/a/b"` |
| `os.path.split(path)` | Tuple `(dirname, basename)` | Decompose path |
| `os.path.splitext(path)` | Tuple `(root, ext)` | Separate extension |
| `os.path.getsize(path)` | Size in **bytes** | File size check |
| `os.path.abspath(path)` | Absolute path | Convert relative ŌåÆ absolute |

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind `os.path.join()`**
> Hardcoding paths like `"data/train/img.jpg"` fails on Windows where the separator is `\`. `os.path.join("data", "train", "img.jpg")` automatically produces the correct separator for the host OS ŌĆö this is **cross-platform best practice**.

#### 2.1.3 `os.environ` and Process Functions

| Attribute/Function | Description |
|---|---|
| `os.environ` | A `Mapping` of environment variables (e.g., `os.environ["HOME"]`) |
| `os.getenv(key, default)` | Safer getter for env variables |
| `os.system(cmd)` | Executes shell command in a subshell |
| `os.cpu_count()` | Number of CPU cores |
| `os.sep` | The string used by OS to separate paths |
| `os.name` | `'posix'` (Linux/Mac) or `'nt'` (Windows) |

### 2.2 The `sys` Module ŌĆö Structural Breakdown

| Attribute/Function | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `sys.argv` | **List** of command-line arguments; `argv[0]` = script name | `python train.py 0.01 32` ŌåÆ `["train.py","0.01","32"]` |
| `sys.path` | **List** of directories Python searches for modules | `sys.path.append("/my/modules")` |
| `sys.version` | String containing Python version | `"3.12.4 (main, ...)"` |
| `sys.version_info` | Named tuple `(major, minor, micro, ...)` | `sys.version_info.major` |
| `sys.platform` | OS identifier string | `'linux'`, `'win32'`, `'darwin'` |
| `sys.exit(arg)` | Raises `SystemExit` to terminate the program | `sys.exit("Error occurred")` |
| `sys.stdin` | Standard input stream object | `line = sys.stdin.readline()` |
| `sys.stdout` | Standard output stream object | `sys.stdout.write("Hello")` |
| `sys.stderr` | Standard error stream object | `sys.stderr.write("Error")` |
| `sys.maxsize` | Maximum value a `Py_ssize_t` can hold (like `INT_MAX`) | Used in `range()` slicing |
| `sys.modules` | Dict of already-loaded modules | `sys.modules["numpy"]` |
| `sys.getrecursionlimit()` | Max recursion depth (default **1000**) | For stack-overflow checks |
| `sys.setrecursionlimit(n)` | Set max recursion depth | `sys.setrecursionlimit(5000)` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Distinction (Frequently Asked)**
> - `os` ŌåÆ talks to the **Operating System** (outside Python).
> - `sys` ŌåÆ talks to the **Python Interpreter** (inside Python).
> - `sys.path` is the **only** mechanism Python uses to locate `import`-ed modules.

### 2.3 File I/O ŌĆö The `open()` Function and Modes

The signature is:
```python
file_object = open(file, mode='r', buffering=-1, encoding=None, errors=None, newline=None)
```

#### 2.3.1 Complete File Access Modes Table

| Mode | Read | Write | Create | Truncate | Pointer Start |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `'r'` (default) | Ō£ģ | ŌØī | ŌØī | ŌØī | Beginning |
