List any **four** common data preprocessing steps. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Data preprocessing is the process of cleaning, transforming, and organizing raw data into a structured format suitable for training a Machine Learning model. > > **Four Steps (2 marks ŌĆö ┬Į mark each):** > 1. **Handling missing values** ŌĆö via imputation or removal. > 2. **Encoding categorical variables** ŌĆö One-Hot or Label Encoding. > 3. **Feature scaling** ŌĆö Standardization (Z-score) or Normalization (Min-Max). > 4. **Outlier detection and removal** ŌĆö using IQR or Z-score methods. > > *(Optional 5th: Feature engineering, deduplication, type conversion.)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is a *microservice*? Name **two** key advantages of microservice architecture over monolithic architecture. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** A microservice is a small, independent, single-purpose software component that performs one business function and communicates with other services over lightweight protocols (typically HTTP/JSON). > > **Advantages (2 marks ŌĆö 1 mark each):** > 1. **Independent Scalability** ŌĆö Only the high-traffic service needs to be scaled, saving resources. > 2. **Fault Isolation** ŌĆö A failure in one service does not crash the entire system. > > *(Optional additional: Independent deployment, technology polyglotism, easier maintenance of small codebases.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ŌĆö Internal Choice --- ### ŌŁÉ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **five major stages of the data preprocessing pipeline** with relevant Python/Pandas operations for each stage. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of a Flask-based microservice** for ML model inference. List the **four core components** and their roles. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question A **(a) The Five Major Stages of Data Preprocessing (7 Marks):** 1. **Data Ingestion (1 mark):** Loading data from various sources using `pd.read_csv()`, `pd.read_json()`, or `pd.read_sql()`. Example: `df = pd.read_csv("sales.csv")`. 2. **Data Inspection (1 mark):** Profiling the data using `df.head()`, `df.info()`, `df.describe()`, and `df.isnull().sum()` to understand shape, dtypes, and missingness. 3. **Data Cleaning (2 marks):** - **Missing values:** `df['age'].fillna(df['age'].mean(), inplace=True)` for numerical, or `df['city'].fillna(df['city'].mode()[0])` for categorical. - **Duplicates:** `df.drop_duplicates(inplace=True)`. - **Outliers:** Filter using IQR bounds $L = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ and $U = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$. 4. **Data Transformation (2 marks):** - **Scaling:** $x' = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ via `StandardScaler` from `sklearn.preprocessing`. - **Encoding:** `pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['category'])` converts strings to binary columns. 5. **Feature Engineering (1 mark):** Creating new features from existing ones, e.g., `df['year'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date']).dt.year`. Then export using `df.to_csv("processed.csv", index=False)`. --- **(b) Flask Microservice Architecture for ML Inference (7 Marks):** Refer to the architecture diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes. **Four Core Components:** | # | Component | Role | Mark | |---|-----------|------|:----:| | 1 | `Flask(__name__)` Application Object | Root container ŌĆö orchestrates routing and configuration | 2 | | 2 | `@app.route('/path', methods=[...])` | URL endpoint that maps a request path to a handler function | 2 | | 3 | View Function (Request Handler) | Executes business logic ŌĆö receives input, preprocesses, predicts, returns output | 2 | | 4 | `jsonify()` Response | Converts Python dict to JSON with proper `Content-Type` header | 1 | **Optional bonus point:** Mention of HTTP status codes (200, 400, 500) for proper REST semantics. --- ### ŌŁÉ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** With a suitable example, explain the difference between **Standardization (Z-score)** and **Min-Max Normalization**. Provide the formulas and state one scenario where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a short note on **microservice architecture** and explain how **Flask** can be used to build a simple prediction microservice. Include a minimal code snippet showing the route definition and JSON request handling. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution for Question B **(a) Standardization vs. Min-Max Normalization (7 Marks):** | Property | Standardization (Z-score) | Min-Max Normalization | |----------|---------------------------|------------------------| | Formula | $z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | | Output Range | Unbounded (typically $[-3, +3]$) | Bounded $[0, 1]$ | | Sensitivity to Outliers | Lower (uses mean & std) | Higher (uses min & max) | | Preferred Scenario | **Algorithms assuming Gaussian distribution** (Logistic Regression, SVM, PCA, Linear Regression) | **Neural Networks** (input must be in $[0,1]$) and image pixel scaling | **Example (2 marks):** - Suppose $x = 70$, $\mu = 60$, $\sigma = 10$, $x_{min} = 0$, $x_{max} = 100$. - **Z-score:** $z = (70 - 60)/10 = 1.0$ - **Min-Max:** $x' = (70 - 0)/(100 - 0) = 0.7$ **Conclusion (1 mark):** Use Z-score when data has outliers or unknown bounded range; use Min-Max when a strict bounded range is required (e.g., neural network inputs). --- **(b) Microservice Architecture & Flask Prediction Service (7 Marks):** **Microservice Architecture (3 marks):** A microservice architecture decomposes a large application into a set of small, independent services, each: - Owning its **own data** (database-per-service principle). - Communicating via **lightweight protocols** (HTTP/REST, gRPC, message queues). - **Independently deployable** and scalable. **Flask-Based Prediction Microservice (4 marks):** **Explanation:** Flask is a lightweight WSGI micro-framework ideal for building RESTful microservices. A minimal prediction service has: - An **application object** initialized once. - A **POST route** (`/predict`) that accepts JSON input features. - A **handler function** that loads the trained model, preprocesses input, and returns the prediction as JSON. **Minimal Code Snippet Structure:** ```python from flask import Flask, request, jsonify import pickle import numpy as np app = Flask(__name__) # Load the trained ML model (serialized via pickle/joblib) model = pickle.load(open("model.pkl", "rb")) @app.route("/predict", methods=["POST"]) def predict(): # Step 1: Extract JSON payload sent by the client data = request.get_json() # Step 2: Convert to numpy array (input shape must match training) features = np.array(data["features"]).reshape(1, -1) # Step 3: Generate prediction prediction = model.predict(features) # Step 4: Return JSON response with HTTP 200 return jsonify({"prediction": prediction.tolist()}), 200 if __name__ == "__main__": # Listen on all interfaces, port 5000 app.run(host="0.0.0.0", port=5000) ``` **Client-Side Invocation Example (1 mark):** A client can call the service using: ```python import requests response = requests.post("http://localhost:5000/predict", json={"features": [5.1, 3.5, 1.4, 0.2]}) print(response.json()) # {"prediction": [0]} ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **­¤ö┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing `fit_transform()` vs. `transform()`** ŌĆö You must *fit* on training data, then *transform* test data using the *same* scaler. Using `fit_transform()` on test data causes *data leakage* and **2-mark penalty**. > 2. **Forgetting to serialize the scaler** ŌĆö The same `StandardScaler` used in training must be saved (`pickle.dump`) and reloaded in the Flask service. A common mistake is to scale with a *new* scaler at inference time, leading to wrong predictions. > 3. **Using `app.run()` in production** ŌĆö Acceptable in dev, but in KTU answers, **explicitly mention Gunicorn/uWSGI** for production deployment to fetch the *full* 7 marks on the deployment sub-question. > 4. **Not setting `Content-Type: application/json`** ŌĆö `jsonify()` does this automatically, but if you use `return str(...)`, you'll lose the mark for proper REST semantics. > 5. **Missing HTTP status codes** ŌĆö Always return `(jsonify({...}), 200)` ŌĆö the tuple form is what the examiner expects. > 6. **Confusing `request.get_json()` with `request.args`** ŌĆö `get_json()` reads the **body**; `args` reads the **URL query string**. Wrong usage = 0 marks on input parsing sub-part. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ­¤ÜĆ > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)** - Ō£ģ **Data preprocessing** Ōēł 80% effort of an ML project; pipeline stages: **Ingest ŌåÆ Inspect ŌåÆ Clean ŌåÆ Transform ŌåÆ Engineer ŌåÆ Export**. - Ō£ģ **Missing values**: numerical ŌåÆ `mean`/`median`; categorical ŌåÆ `mode`; high-missingness columns ŌåÆ `drop`. - Ō£ģ **Outliers** detected via **IQR rule** ($1.5 \times IQR$ fence) or **Z-score** ($\vert z \vert > 3$). - Ō£ģ **Scaling formulas to memorize**: - Z-score: $z = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ - Min-Max: $x' = (x - x_{min})/(x_{max} - x_{min})$ - Ō£ģ **One-Hot Encoding** for nominal; **Label Encoding** for ordinal categorical features. - Ō£ģ A **microservice** is *single-purpose*, *independently deployable*, and *communicates over HTTP/JSON*. - Ō£ģ **Flask** = microframework; **Django** = full-stack framework. Know the difference. - Ō£ģ Core Flask building blocks: **`Flask(__name__)`** + **`@app.route`** + **view function** + **`jsonify`**. - Ō£ģ HTTP methods: **GET** (read), **POST** (create), **PUT** (update), **DELETE** (remove). - Ō£ģ Status codes: **200** (OK), **400** (Bad Request), **404** (Not Found), **500** (Server Error). - Ō£ģ **Critical pipeline rule**: Save the *preprocessor* (scaler/encoder) used in training and *reload* it in the Flask service ŌĆö never refit at inference time. - Ō£ģ In production, replace `app.run()` with **Gunicorn** (`gunicorn -w 4 app:app`) for concurrency. - Ō£ģ Flask is **synchronous** by default; for async ML inference, consider **Quart** (Flask-API-compatible async fork). - Ō£ģ **Architecture choice**: Use microservice when you need *independent scaling*, *polyglot tech stacks*, or *fault isolation*; use monolith when the team is small and the domain is simple. > **­¤Ä» Final Exam Mantra:** *Clean data in, clean predictions out. Decoupled services, decoupled failures.*
